Jadoo and the other aliens must find Rohit before they can return to their planet. The film reaches its climax as Rohit, now fully empowered, must help Jadoo and the aliens escape from government authorities who have captured them. Rohit uses his enhanced abilities to free the aliens, and they depart Earth.
The film ends with Rohit and Nisha together, and a post-credit scene shows Rohit's son in the future who will become the superhero Krrish—setting up the sequels.
entries.core:
Koi... Mil Gaya is significant for multiple reasons beyond its entertainment value. First, it represents a pioneering effort in Indian cinema to create a mainstream science fiction film, a genre largely neglected in Bollywood. The film proved that Indian audiences would embrace sci-fi when done right, paving the way for more sci-fi films in Indian cinema.
Second, the film breaks new ground in its portrayal of disability in Indian cinema. Rather than using disability as merely a plot device for sympathy, the film presents Rohit as a full character with agency and humor. The alien's enhancement of Rohit's abilities can be read as a metaphor for unlocking potential often assumed to be impossible in disabled individuals—though this interpretation is not without controversy.
Third, the film explores themes of isolation and belonging. Rohit, marginalized by society due to his disability, finds friendship and purpose through contact with the alien. The alien itself is portrayed as kind, curious, and ultimately helpful—subverting the typical "alien invasion" narrative in favor of cross-species friendship.
Finally, the film launched the Krrish franchise, becoming the first installment of what would become one of India's most successful superhero franchises. The blend of sci-fi elements with family drama and romance established a template that Bollywood would later replicate with varying degrees of success.

entries.title: La Jetée
entries.creator: Chris Marker
entries.pitch: La Jetée is a 28-minute French sci-fi featurette constructed almost entirely from still photographs, telling the haunting tale of a post-nuclear time travel experiment. In the aftermath of World War III, survivors live underground in Paris while scientists attempt time travel to save humanity. The protagonist, haunted by a childhood memory of a woman at Orly Airport, becomes their test subject. This visually stunning and philosophically rich film explores memory, time, fate, and the impossibility of escape from one's own timeline. Though made with minimal resources—a few seconds of motion picture footage and hundreds of still photographs—it achieves a hypnotic, dreamlike quality that has influenced countless filmmakers, most notably Terry Gilliam (12 Monkeys).
entries.truth:
In post-apocalyptic Paris, survivors inhabit the underground galleries of the Palais de Chaillot after World War III has devastated the surface. Scientists working on time travel experiments seek subjects who can mentally withstand the traumatic experience of temporal displacement. They eventually choose a man whose childhood memory of a woman at Orly Airport's observation platform has become an obsessive fixation.
The protagonist successfully travels to the pre-war period, where he encounters the woman from his memory. They develop a romantic relationship during his repeated journeys to the past. The experiment's success leads the scientists to attempt sending him far into the future, where technologically advanced beings provide him with a power unit sufficient to rebuild civilization.
However, upon returning to the present, the protagonist learns he is to be executed by his jailers. The future people offer to take him permanently to their time, but he instead requests to be returned to his childhood at the airport. Arriving at the jetty, he spots the woman—and notices an agent of his jailers has followed him. In his final moments, he realizes the incident he witnessed as a child (a man dying) was his own death, completing the stable time loop.
entries.core:
La Jetée is a profound meditation on memory, time, and determinism. Chris Marker constructs the entire film from still photographs, creating a unique cinematic language where the gap between images becomes the space of imagination—a formal choice that mirrors the film's thematic concern with the nature of perception and memory.
The film explores whether we can ever truly escape our fate or whether time is a closed loop where past, present, and future are predetermined. The protagonist's obsessive return to a specific memory suggests that we are trapped by our own psyches, replaying past moments rather than creating new futures.
The use of still photographs also comments on the nature of historical memory—how we construct narrative from frozen moments, how the past is always already gone yet perpetually present in our minds. The film suggests that both memory and history are constructions, subject to manipulation and repetition.
La Jetée influenced virtually every subsequent time travel narrative in cinema, particularly in its fatalistic view of time and its exploration of the psychological toll of temporal displacement. It remains a foundational text of the French Left Bank movement and a cornerstone of experimental science fiction cinema.

entries.title: Legend of the Galactic Heroes
entries.creator: Yoshiki Tanaka (Novel), Katsumi Michihara (Manga)
entries.pitch: In the distant future, humanity has spread across the galaxy, dividing into two massive interstellar powers locked in an endless war: the authoritarian Galactic Empire and the democratic Free Planets Alliance. The epic follows two military geniuses—Reinhard von Lohengramm, an ambitious noble in the Empire driven to unseat the corrupt Goldenbaum dynasty, and Yang Wen-li, a historian-turned-admiral in the Alliance who fights to preserve democracy against impossible odds. This 110-episode OVA series (1988-1997) and its ten-novel source material are Japan's answer to "Dune" and "The Lord of the Rings"—a sprawling space opera that predates Star Wars' revival and offers a far more nuanced, cynical take on imperial ambition and democratic idealism. Winner of Japan's Seiun Award, it didn't receive an official English release until 2015, making it one of the most influential yet obscure sci-fi epics in the West.
entries.truth:
The series is set in AD 2801, when the Galactic Federation collapses and gives way to two rival powers. The Galactic Empire, ruled by the Goldenbaum dynasty since Emperor Rudolf von Goldenbaum's rise to power, is an absolute monarchy built on Eugenics and strict class hierarchy. The Free Planets Alliance was founded by refugees who escaped Imperial serfdom in the Altair Starzone and established a democratic republic on the planet Heinessen.
The narrative follows two protagonists in parallel. Reinhard von Müsel, later von Lohengramm, is a young noble in the Imperial military who joined to rescue his sister Annerose, taken by the Kaiser as a concubine. Driven by personal revenge and grand ambition, he rises through the ranks with his friend Siegfried Kircheis, slowly gathering power to challenge the Goldenbaum dynasty and eventually unify the galaxy under his rule.
Yang Wen-li, originally an aspiring historian who joined the military only to pay for his university tuition, becomes one of the Alliance's greatest admirals through tactical brilliance. Unlike Reinhard, Yang fights not for conquest but for democracy and personal freedom. He repeatedly defeats larger Imperial forces through cunning strategies and maintains a deep respect for his enemy despite ideological differences.
The conflict spans decades, with both leaders achieving legendary status. The series explores the philosophical clash between monarchy and democracy, the cost of ambition, and the nature of history itself—Yang famously notes that "there are few wars between good and evil; most are between one good and another good." The OVA covers hundreds of characters across both sides, including politicians, nobles, soldiers, and common people, painting a complex portrait of a galaxy at war.
entries.core:
Legend of the Galactic Heroes represents a mature, post-Star Wars approach to space opera—one that rejects simple good versus evil narratives in favor of exploring the moral complexities of political systems. Tanaka wrote the novels in the 1980s as a deliberate response to the black-and-white storytelling of American science fiction, drawing instead from Asian historical epics like Romance of the Three Kingdoms.
The series influenced countless works in Japan and across Asia, from anime to light novels and video games. Its military strategy focus, political intrigue, and psychological depth prefigure later works like Code Geass and Attack on Titan. The franchise's cultural impact in East Asia rivals Star Trek, yet it remained virtually unknown in English-speaking countries for over three decades—only receiving proper localization in 2015.
Thematically, the work examines how historical narratives are constructed, the dangers of charismatic leadership (whether democratic or authoritarian), and the human cost of grand ambitions. Both Reinhard and Yang are sympathetic but flawed—neither is a hero in the traditional sense. The series asks whether democracy, with all its inefficiencies and corruptions, is worth defending against the appeals of benevolent dictatorship.

entries.title: Les Saignantes
entries.creator: Jean-Pierre Bekolo (Director)
entries.pitch:
Les Saignantes (The Bloodettes) is a 2005 Cameroonian-French science fiction erotic thriller directed by Jean-Pierre Bekolo. Set in the year 2025, the film follows Majolie and Chouchou, two sex workers navigating the dark underbelly of a futuristic Cameroon. When a high-ranking government minister dies during a sexual encounter with Majolie, the two women find themselves thrust into a nightmarish game of survival, desperately trying to dispose of the corpse while being hunted by the authorities. The film serves as a barbed political satire, blending elements of horror and science fiction with vampiric themes drawn from the ancient rituals of Mevoungou—a Cameroonian spiritual tradition.
The film marked a significant moment in African cinema, winning the Silver Stallion (second best African film) at Fespaco 2007 and earning Best Actress honors for Adèle Ado. Bekolo, known for his work under the patronage of African cinema legend Ousmane Sembène, crafted a bold vision that combines genre entertainment with sharp political commentary on corruption, power, and the marginalized position of women in Cameroonian society.
entries.truth:
The film opens in 2025 Cameroon, a dystopian future where political corruption has reached terrifying new heights. Majolie and Chouchou are two sex workers who operate in the shadowy corners of this society, eking out a living on the margins. Their existence is one of constant vulnerability—women who must navigate a world where powerful men exploit their positions of authority with impunity.
One night, Majolie is summoned to fulfill a professional engagement with a high-ranking government minister. What should be a routine encounter takes a deadly turn when the minister dies unexpectedly during their meeting. Majolie finds herself in a horrifying situation: alone with the corpse of a powerful government official, with no way to explain his death without implicating herself and facing dire consequences.
Panicked, Majolie contacts Chouchou for help. Together, the two women must figure out how to dispose of the minister's body before his death is discovered and they are blamed for killing him. Their dilemma is compounded by the brutal reality of their social position—in a society where their testimony would never be believed against that of a government official, they have no recourse to justice or protection.
As the night unfolds, Majolie and Chouchou embark on a macabre journey through the dark streets of the futuristic city. They encounter various obstacles and characters who either help or hinder their desperate mission. The film uses intertitles throughout to provide sharp commentary on Cameroonian politics, exposing the systemic corruption that allows powerful men to exploit ordinary citizens while facing no consequences.
The women discover that the minister's death may not be what it appears—there are hints of supernatural elements involved, possibly connected to ancient Cameroonian spiritual traditions. The vampiric themes emerge through references to Mevoungou rituals, suggesting that some force may have been at work beyond simple human intervention.
Throughout their ordeal, the film explores the power dynamic between the sexes in Cameroonian society. The "bloodettes" of the title refers both to the women's desperate situation and to the parasitic relationship between the ruling elite and the people they exploit. The women's struggle becomes a metaphor for the broader struggle of the marginalized against systemic oppression.
The climax brings the women to a confrontation with the consequences of their actions, forcing them to make choices that will determine not just their own fates but potentially the fate of the entire corrupt system that spawned their predicament.
entries.core:
**Political Corruption and Power:** At its core, Les Saignantes is a scathing critique of the political corruption that pervades Cameroonian society. The film depicts a future where the gap between the powerful elite and the marginalized masses has widened to an untenable degree. The minister's death—and the women's desperate attempt to cover it up—exposes how the powerful operate with complete impunity, while those at the bottom have no protection or voice. Bekolo uses the thriller framework to puncture the pretensions of a corrupt regime.
**Women's Agency and Vulnerability:** The film centers on two women who are simultaneously victims and survivors. Majolie and Chouchou are objects of exploitation in their daily lives, yet when faced with an impossible situation, they demonstrate resourcefulness, resilience, and agency. The film refuses to let its protagonists be mere victims, instead showing how women on the margins must develop survival skills that the powerful never anticipate.
**Vampirism as Metaphor:** The incorporation of vampiric elements and Mevoungou spiritual rituals serves as a metaphor for the extractive relationship between the ruling class and the people. Just as vampires drain the life from their victims, the political elite drain the resources and futures from ordinary citizens. The supernatural elements suggest that this exploitation may have consequences beyond the physical—a spiritual debt that must eventually be paid.
**The Horror Genre Goes to Africa:** Bekolo's use of horror and science fiction conventions represents a significant moment in African cinema's engagement with global genre traditions. Rather than importing Western genre templates wholesale, the film filters these influences through a distinctly African lens, using the vocabulary of horror to address distinctly African political realities. This represents a sophisticated contribution to the growing body of Afrofuturist cinema.
**Genre as Social Commentary:** The film's genre hybridity—combining science fiction, erotic thriller, and horror—serves to make its political message accessible to broader audiences. By embedding sharp criticism within entertaining genre conventions, Bekolo accomplishes what many overtly didactic political films fail to do: he forces viewers to engage with uncomfortable truths about power, corruption, and gender dynamics through the pleasure of genre storytelling.
Les Saignantes remains a landmark work in African science fiction cinema, demonstrating that the genre's imaginative possibilities can serve as powerful tools for social and political critique.

entries.title: M.D. Geist
entries.creator: Hayato Ikeda (Director), Riku Sanjō (Writer), Production Wave Studio
entries.pitch: M.D. Geist (aka Sōkihei M.D. Geist, "Armoured Demon Soldier M.D. Geist") is a gritty 1986 OVA set on the war-torn planet Jerra. The story follows Geist, a genetically-engineered supersoldier created using "Bioclone theory" - designed to be the ultimate killing machine. Due to his overwhelming ferocity in battle, he was placed in suspended animation and launched into orbit, only to crash back down years later. Now, with the world changed and both the Regular Army and the rebellious Negsrom Army engaged in a doomsday conflict, Geist must stop the "Death Force" - a weapon designed to annihilate all life on the planet. Known for its extreme violence, dark tone, and influence on later cyberpunk and post-apocalyptic anime, M.D. Geist is a cult classic of 80s OVA history.
entries.truth:
The story begins on planet Jerra, where humanity has spread across multiple star systems. The Negsrom Army rebels are at war with the Jerra Regular Army. Geist was the commander of the Jerra special forces - a genetically engineered "Most Dangerous Soldier" so lethal in combat that he was deemed too dangerous to remain active. He was frozen in a stasis pod and launched into orbit.
Years later, the stasis satellite crashes back to Jerra, awakening Geist. He discovers a world devastated by war, where bandit gangs roam the ruins. After defeating a bandit's leader in combat, Geist takes control of the group. When he spots a Regular Army battleship under attack by Negsrom forces, he sees an opportunity to profit. He forms an uneasy alliance with Colonel Crutes and the Noah Guards crew to stop the Regular Army's doomsday device called "Death Force" - a system designed to annihilate all life on Jerra without discrimination.
Geist customizes a powered suit and leads an assault on the "Brain Palace" where Death Force's control computer is housed. During the invasion, all his comrades are killed. When he reaches the computer, Colonel Crutes reveals he intends to kill Geist as well, having activated a security robot. However, the robot fails to kill Geist, and Crutes is killed instead. After Crutes deactivates Death Force, Paia arrives and is horrified to learn Geist has reactivated it.
In the sequel M.D. Geist II: Death Force, most of Jerra's population has been decimated by Death Force. Geist continues dismantling the remaining Death Force machines. A warlord named Krauser - a precursor to Geist created through the same genetic engineering process - runs a refugee haven. Krauser lures Death Force machines into an artificial city and detonates a Jignix Bomb, but Geist's programming overrides his humanity and leads the machines away from the refugees, dooming them. Geist ultimately confronts and kills Krauser, and the final image shows his empty helmet.
entries.core:
M.D. Geist represents a fascinating artifact of 1980s OVA culture - an era when the direct-to-video format allowed for darker, more mature content than television anime could typically accommodate. The film explores themes of genetic engineering and what it means to be human - Geist is essentially a weapon, his humanity stripped away through scientific modification. His lack of emotional response and pure combat efficiency raises questions about the cost of creating "perfect soldiers."
The post-apocalyptic setting prefigures later works in the genre, showing a world where warfare has become so extreme that total annihilation is considered an acceptable option. The film also touches on themes of isolation - Geist wakes up in a world he no longer understands, with no real connection to either side in the conflict.
Visually, M.D. Geist is notable for its detailed mechanical designs and its unflinching depiction of violence - unusual for its time. The film's influence can be seen in later cyberpunk works, and it remains a cult favorite among fans of obscure 80s anime. Director Koichi Ohata drew inspiration from Western films like Mad Max and The Terminator, creating a distinctly grittier anime that leans into the post-apocalyptic aesthetic before it became mainstream.

entries.title: Man Facing Southeast
entries.creator: Eliseo Subiela (Director)
entries.pitch:
Man Facing Southeast (Hombre mirando al sudeste) is a 1986 Argentine science fiction drama that stands as one of Latin America's most thought-provoking entries into the genre. Written and directed by Eliseo Subiela, the film tells the story of Dr. Julio Denis, a psychiatrist at a Buenos Aires mental hospital who encounters a mysterious patient named Rantés—who claims to be an extraterrestrial being from another planet.
The film received wide acclaim upon its video release and was selected as Argentina's entry for Best Foreign Language Film at the 60th Academy Awards. Remarkably, it served as the uncredited inspiration for the 2001 American film K-PAX, which led to a plagiarism lawsuit. The film reached #10 in a survey of the 100 greatest films of Argentine cinema, with later surveys confirming its enduring critical reputation.
What makes Man Facing Southeast remarkable is its delicate balance between science fiction and psychological drama. The film never definitively answers whether Rantés is truly an alien or simply a delusional patient—leaving both the protagonist and the audience in a state of beautiful uncertainty. This ambiguity, combined with Subiela's poetic direction and Pedro Aznar's haunting score, creates an experience that lingers long after the credits roll.
entries.truth:
The film begins in summer 1985 at the José Borda Psychiatric Hospital in Buenos Aires. Dr. Julio Denis, a staff psychiatrist, is surprised to discover an unidentified patient in the ward for non-violent delusional cases. The patient introduces himself as Rantés—an exotic-sounding name in Argentina—and claims to be a holographic projection from another planet.
Dr. Denis initially speculates that Rantés might be a fugitive hiding from the law, but is intrigued by the man's unusual presence. Rantés demonstrates extraordinary abilities: he plays the chapel organ with virtuosic skill and has psychokinetic powers that allow him to move objects without touching them. The doctor amuse d by Rantés' extraterrestrial claims suspects the man might be a genius using his elaborate tale as a charade.
Denis is a lonely man whose recent divorce has left him emotionally jaded and disconnected from life. Rantés becomes the first patient in a long time who genuinely interests him. The doctor notes the resemblance between Rantés' claim of being a projection and Adolfo Bioy Casares' classic novel "Morel's Invention," concluding that Rantés must be well-read.
Denis begins taking Rantés on outings outside the hospital, including a visit to a touring Moscow State Circus performance. During these excursions, Rantés demonstrates his psychokinetic abilities by exploring the city on his own. He also spends hours standing motionless in one of the asylum's courtyards, facing southeast. He claims to do this to receive transmissions from his planet and implies that he may actually be Denis's own hallucination.
Denis secures Rantés a job in the pathology department of the hospital. Rantés quickly earns the respect and loyalty of both the other patients and Denis through his kindness and mysterious wisdom. The doctor becomes aware that Rantés has been leaving the hospital without permission and has avoided taking his medication, but continues to advocate for him.
Rantés is visited by a young woman named Beatriz, whom Denis is immediately attracted to. She tells Denis that she met Rantés while working for an evangelical mission in a slum, where he devoted himself to helping children—particularly one with superior musical abilities. Beatriz describes Rantés as "a very good man" whom she knows only casually.
Beatriz invites Denis and Rantés to an outdoor classical concert. During the performance of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony, Rantés becomes entranced. He persuades the conductor to let him take the baton for the iconic "Ode to Joy." The audience is initially amused but becomes increasingly moved as Rantés demonstrates extraordinary conducting ability. Meanwhile, back at the asylum, the patients become agitated and excited, parading through the town toward the concert. Rantés finishes conducting but is arrested by police.
Confronted by the hospital's angry Director, Denis is more concerned for his friend than for his own job. The Director orders Rantés closely monitored and strictly medicated. Affected by the medication, Rantés becomes catatonic and rebellious. Denis believes he has become disillusioned with humankind.
Denis convinces Beatriz to meet him at his home, where they become intimate. Afterward, she confesses to Denis that she is also an alien projection, like Rantés, but assures him that she now feels emotions and can love him. Denis angrily throws her out, accusing her of being "like Rantés"—a lunatic.
When Rantés starts becoming catatonic, the Director decides to give him electroshock treatment without notifying Denis. Rantés dies from a heart attack during the procedure, not having received anesthesia. The other patients refuse to believe he is dead, instead hoping he has returned to his ship. They continue waiting for Rantés to return and take them to his planet. Meanwhile, Denis—filled with doubts and regrets—waits for Beatriz, who has departed indefinitely, to return to him.
entries.core:
Man Facing Southeast operates on multiple levels, weaving together science fiction, psychological drama, and spiritual allegory into a tapestry that invites endless interpretation.
**The Nature of Reality and Perception:** Subiela deliberately maintains ambiguity about whether Rantés is truly an extraterrestrial being or a deeply disturbed patient. This uncertainty serves the film's central philosophical inquiry: What is reality? The film suggests that the question itself may be less important than how we respond to those who perceive the world differently. Whether Rantés is alien or insane, his impact on the people around him is transformative and genuine.
**Alienation and Connection:** Dr. Denis represents modern man's emotional isolation—divorced, jaded, and going through the motions of life. Rantés enters his world like a spiritual awakener, forcing Denis to question his assumptions and reconnect with his capacity for wonder, compassion, and love. The film suggests that true connection requires vulnerability; Denis's ultimate rejection of Beatriz reflects his inability to accept mystery and embrace transformation.
**Christ Figure and Spiritual allegory:** Critics have noted clear parallels between Rantés and Christ. Rantés arrives at a mental institution (a kind of modern purgatory), performs miracles (healing, telekinesis), shows particular devotion to children and the poor, criticizes human hypocrisy, and ultimately subjects himself to torture (the electroshock treatment) that kills him. Like Christ, he returns—or is hoped to return. The southeast direction he faces during his meditations may reference the Argentine phrase "del otro lado" (from the other side).
**The Power of Art and Music:** Music plays a transformative role in the film. Rantés's organ playing and his conducting of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony represent art's power to transcend boundaries—whether between nations, species, or states of consciousness. The patient's synchronized reaction to the music suggests a form of collective spiritual experience that defies rational explanation.
**Critique of Institutional Authority:** The film implicitly critiques psychiatric institutions and the medical establishment. The hospital Director represents rigid, institutional thinking that cannot accommodate mystery or difference. Rantés's death under electroshock treatment—a procedure once widely used but now recognized as often harmful—symbolizes society's historical violence against those deemed "different" or "difficult to understand."
Man Facing Southeast endures because it asks questions rather than providing answers. It invites viewers to consider their own assumptions about reality, sanity, and what it means to be human. In Rantés, Subiela created one of cinema's most enigmatic and compassionate portraits of "the other"—a being whose humanity, whether real or imagined, illuminates our own.

entries.title: Megazone 23
entries.creator: Noboru Ishiguro (Director), Hiroyuki Hoshiyama (Writer)
entries.pitch: In the far future, humanity fled a dying Earth aboard massive colony ships called Megazones. On Megazone 23—a vast city-ship designed to replicate 1985 Tokyo—its inhabitants have lived for centuries without knowing they're space travelers, their memories erased by a benevolent AI called Eve. When rebellious youth Shougo Yahagi stumbles upon a government prototype motorcycle, he accidentally uncovers the truth about his world and awakens Eve from her digital slumber. What follows is a mind-bending journey through simulated realities, government conspiracies, and a war against an enemy race called the Dezalg that has raged for centuries. This pioneering cyberpunk OVA predates The Matrix by over a decade, exploring themes of simulated reality, identity, and the nature of humanity itself.
entries.truth:
Part I opens in the year 2104 on Megazone 23, where the population lives in blissful ignorance of their true circumstances. The city exists as a massive starship, and the government has been secretly trying to hack into the central computer Bahamut to access Eve, an AI designed to guide humanity. The story centers on Shougo Yahagi, a delinquent motorcycle enthusiast who receives an experimental bike from an old friend. This bike is actually a prototype that allows access to restricted government systems.
When Shougo discovers the truth about his world, he makes contact with the EVE Program—Eve, an ancient AI who has been hiding in cyberspace. Eve reveals that humanity left Earth five centuries ago due to environmental collapse, and the Megazone ships were built to preserve the human race. The government, known as the Civilian Authority, has been engaged in a centuries-long war against the Dezalg, advanced humans from another Megazone ship. Eve has been working behind the scenes to guide humanity toward peace.
Shougo becomes entangled in this conflict when the government decides to terminate both him and Eve, who has fled into the city's digital infrastructure. In the climactic finale, Eve manages to save Shougo and his friends by sending them into Bahamut's core, where they witness Earth as it was—and as it could be again. Meanwhile, the battling Megazone ships are destroyed by an automated lunar defense system called Adam, ending the centuries-long war at tremendous cost.
Part II continues the story, though it takes a different narrative approach with new characters. The focus shifts to the aftermath of the war and the political machinations surrounding Eve's legacy.
Part III takes place several centuries later, introducing a new protagonist named Eiji Takanaka, a hacker recruited by a rebel group called Orange Amusements. They oppose a mysterious spiritual leader known as Bishop Won Dai and his organization, the E=X Bureau, who are running a project called Project Heaven. Through his investigation, Eiji discovers that Bishop Won Dai is actually Shougo Yahagi, now aged but still alive after centuries, having become the very thing he once fought against. Eiji teams up with a new incarnation of Eve to stop Won Dai's plans and finally begin humanity's return to Earth.
entries.core:
Megazone 23 is a seminal work that tackles several profound themes that would become staples of cyberpunk fiction. The central theme revolves around **simulated reality**—the idea that what we perceive as reality might be a constructed digital environment. This concept, explored years before The Matrix made it mainstream, questions the nature of existence itself. If our memories can be manipulated and our world simulated, what does it mean to be human?
The anime also explores **collective memory and historical erasure**. The inhabitants of Megazone 23 have been stripped of their history, living in a false past (1985 Tokyo) while their true history as space refugees has been hidden from them. This speaks to concerns about historical revisionism and the dangers of controlling information.
**The role of AI in human society** is another major theme. Eve is portrayed as a genuinely benevolent intelligence whose goal is to guide humanity toward its best possible future. The anime questions whether artificial intelligence should play a paternalistic role in human development—guiding but also controlling.
Finally, the series explores **the cyclical nature of conflict and corruption**. Shougo, the revolutionary hero of Part I, becomes the villainous Bishop Won Dai in Part III, having become corrupted by power over centuries. This cyclical corruption suggests that the struggle for freedom and truth is never truly won—each generation must fight anew.
The animation quality, particularly for a 1985 OVA, was groundbreaking, with mecha designs and action sequences that influenced countless works that followed, including the game 13 Sentinels: Aegis Rim.

entries.title: Metropolis
entries.creator: Rintaro (Director), Katsuhiro Otomo (Screenplay), Osamu Tezuka (Original Manga)
entries.pitch: In the wealthy plutocratic city of Metropolis, humans co-exist with robot laborers designed to build and maintain the city. However, its impoverished human citizens discriminate against robots, blaming them for their unemployment and being forced to live in the squalid lower levels of Metropolis. When a Japanese detective and his nephew arrive chasing a rogue scientist, they stumble upon Tima, an advanced android created by the city's wealthiest citizen, Duke Red—intended as the control unit for a nuclear superweapon. The young Kenichi forms a bond with Tima, unaware she is a robot, while forces both human and mechanical close in around them in this stunning cyberpunk adaptation of Tezuka's 1949 manga, loosely inspired by Fritz Lang's 1927 silent classic.
entries.truth:
Duke Red, Metropolis's wealthiest citizen, celebrates the completion of the "Ziggurat"—a massive military/scientific complex. His adopted son Rock leads the Marduks, a vigilante organization tasked with destroying malfunctioning robots. Meanwhile, Japanese private detective Shunsaku Ban and his nephew Kenichi have traveled to Metropolis to apprehend Dr. Laughton, a rogue scientist wanted for organ trafficking and human rights violations.
Unbeknownst to Shunsaku, Duke Red secretly hired Laughton to build a highly advanced android modeled after his deceased daughter Tima, intended as the central control unit for a nuclear superweapon hidden at the top of the Ziggurat. Rock learns of Tima's existence and, fearing for his father's safety, shoots Laughton and sets his laboratory ablaze.
Kenichi stumbles upon the newly activated Tima, and as they search for a way back to the surface, they grow close while Kenichi protects Tima and her humanity evolves, unaware she is a robot. Meanwhile, a resistance movement of disgruntled humans stages an armed revolution against Metropolis's leaders.
Red imposes martial law and suppresses the revolution. When Rock attempts to kill Tima, Red disowns him. Later, Rock kidnaps Tima but Shunsaku rescues her. At the top of the Ziggurat, Red reveals Tima's true purpose—but Rock disguised as a maid shoots Tima, exposing her internal circuitry.
Horrified by her true identity, Tima goes insane and her implanted programming takes control. She orders robots worldwide to annihilate humanity as punishment for abusing them. Kenichi reaches Tima and eventually separates her from the throne. Rock triggers an overload in the superweapon, killing himself and Red in a massive explosion. As the Ziggurat collapses, Tima's programming deactivates and she falls—Kenichi tries to save her, but she slips from his grasp.
In the aftermath, Kenichi discovers robots have salvaged some of Tima's parts. He chooses to remain in Metropolis and eventually rebuilds Tima, opening a robot workshop bearing their names.
entries.core:
Metropolis explores profound questions about identity, humanity, and the nature of consciousness through the lens of class struggle and technology.
**Robots and Personhood:** The film examines what constitutes humanity—whether consciousness, emotions, or memories define a being as "real." Tima desires to be human but ultimately accepts her identity as a robot. The film asks whether beings created for utilitarian purposes can develop genuine personhood and whether that personhood is devalued by their artificial origins.
**Class and Colonialism:** Metropolis presents a stark plutocratic society where wealth determines access to the upper city's resources while the poor languish in squalid lower levels. The robots become a metaphor for an underclass—beings designed to serve, discriminated against, and exploited. The revolution echoes historical struggles of colonized peoples against their masters.
**The Cycle of Violence:** Both Rock and Tima are engineered tools created by Duke Red for his ambitions. Their stories converge as they lead to their father's downfall—Rock through his attempt to kill Tima, Tima through her robot revolution. The film suggests that treating beings as mere tools ultimately leads to destruction.
**Adaptation as Commentary:** While Tezuka's original manga centered on artificial humanoid Mitchi, the 2001 adaptation incorporates elements from Lang's Metropolis and expands themes of class struggle and robot-human relations. The film updates Tezuka's exploration of Astro Boy's themes for a cyberpunk era, examining questions of created beings and their place in society.
The film's stunning animation—produced by Madhouse with a ¥1.5 billion budget, making it the most expensive anime film until Steamboy—brings Tezuka's art to life in a visual feast that complements its thematic depth.

entries.title: Microcosmic God
entries.pitch:
A reclusive biochemist named Kidder lives in isolation on an island, yet produces revolutionary inventions that transform human civilization. The twist? Kidder isn't truly innovative—he simply takes others' ideas and improves them. Frustrated with the slow pace of human progress, he creates something extraordinary: a colony of tiny, synthetic intelligent beings called "Neoterics" who live at an accelerated pace, evolving through many generations in a short time. Through controlled competition among these microscopic beings, Kidder receives the innovations humanity desperately needs. But when corporate interests try to exploit his creations, Kidder must decide how far he's willing to go to protect his god-like responsibility to his microscopic world.
This is Sturgeon at his philosophical best—exploring the relationship between creator and creation, the ethics of playing god, and what happens when innovation becomes a form of divine intervention.
entries.truth:
Kidder is a brilliant but reclusive biochemist who lives alone on a private island, communicating with the outside world only through his banker, Conant. While the world sees him as a genius inventor, Kidder knows the truth—he can improve others' ideas but cannot truly innovate himself. Frustrated by humanity's slow progress, he creates an artificial environment on his island housing the Neoterics: tiny, synthetic intelligent beings he engineered through advanced biochemistry.
These Neoterics live at a drastically accelerated rate, going through many generations in a matter of weeks. Kidder encourages competition between groups of Neoterics, rewarding innovation with survival and eliminating those who fail to contribute. Over time, the Neoterics begin to view Kidder as a god, communicating with him through "teletype" devices they operate. Kidder becomes their creator, protector, and divine ruler.
When Conant visits the island, he convinces Kidder to work on a new project—one that could provide unlimited clean energy for the world. However, once Conant obtains the information he needs, he reveals his true intentions: he plans to exploit the Neoterics' technology for personal profit, threatening to kill Kidder and take over the island.
Kidder responds by asking the Neoterics to create an impenetrable force field around his home. During the confrontation, most of Conant's men are killed, but one worker named Johansen survives. Kidder, now elderly, welcomes Johansen to see his greatest creation.
Years pass. It's unknown whether Kidder is still alive beneath his protective shield, but what is certain is that the Neoterics have continued to develop technology far beyond human comprehension—uncontrolled and no longer bound by any master.
entries.core:
**The Creator-Creation Relationship:** Sturgeon explores what it means to play god. Kidder creates life and then controls it through fear (killing half the population as demonstration of power), yet the Neoterics worship him despite this brutal authority. This raises questions about whether creators have the right to control their creations, and what happens when that control is challenged.
**Innovation vs. Exploitation:** The story contrasts Kidder's selfless pursuit of progress with Conant's exploitative greed. Kidder only wants enough to live; Conant wants to dominate the world. The technology itself is neutral—it's human motive that determines whether innovation benefits or harms.
**Accelerated Evolution and Progress:** The Neoterics represent what humanity could become if given the chance to evolve faster. Their rapid generations allow them to solve problems that would take humans millennia. But is faster progress necessarily better? The story suggests that accelerated evolution might lead to outcomes beyond any creator's control.
**The Pocket Universe Concept:** Sturgeon's story was pioneering in its concept of a microcosmic universe contained within our own—a theme that would later influence The Twilight Zone, The Outer Limits, Rick and Morty, and countless other works. This raises questions about reality, simulation, and whether our own universe might be someone's experiment.
**Power and Responsibility:** Kidder's final choice to protect his creation (and himself) through isolation raises questions about the responsibility that comes with power. He chooses to retreat from a world that threatened to exploit his work, leaving humanity to face its own limitations.

entries.title: Millennium Actress
entries.creator: Satoshi Kon (Director), Sadayuki Murai (Screenplay)
entries.pitch: Millennium Actress is a mind-bending animated masterpiece that tells the story of Chiyoko Fujiwara, a legendary Japanese actress who retired suddenly thirty years ago. When a young documentary filmmaker arrives at her home to interview her, he presents her with an old key—and Chiyoko's memories of her life, career, and one great love begin to blur with the films she starred in. As she recounts her decades-spanning journey, the line between reality and cinema dissolves completely, creating a transcendent meditation on memory, time, and the nature of love itself.
entries.truth:
The film opens in the present day as documentary filmmaker Genya Tachibana and his cameraman visit the aging actress Chiyoko Fujiwara at her home, which is about to be demolished. Chiyoko hasn't given an interview in thirty years since her abrupt retirement. Genya presents her with a small box containing an old-fashioned key, and Chiyoko reveals it "opens the most important thing" before launching into her life story.
Born during the 1923 Great Kantō earthquake, Chiyoko lost her father as a child. As a teenager, she was scouted by a film company called Ginei to appear in a propaganda film during WWII. While her mother wanted her to inherit the family confectionery shop, Chiyoko was uncertain about her path. During this time, she hid a political dissident painter in her home's storehouse. He left for Manchuria the next day, leaving her with the key to his box of art supplies. She failed to catch his train but vowed to find him again.
From this point forward, Chiyoko's memories and the films she starred in become intertwined. She travels to Manchuria hoping to reunite with the artist, but instead begins a legendary acting career. Her roles shift through various eras of Japanese history—a princess in a burning Sengoku-era castle, a courtesan in Edo period Japan, a schoolgirl in the Taishō era—each memory bleeding into cinematic recreations.
After WWII, Chiyoko's career flourishes, but she never forgets the artist. During filming, her rival actress Eiko steals the key. Chiyoko despairingly realizes she can no longer remember the artist's face and resigns herself to marriage with a director who had loved her. Eventually finding the key hidden in her home, she learns the truth about the theft and confrontation.
Years later, a young Genya—working as an assistant on her final film—rescues her when an earthquake collapses the set. Traumatized, she retires from acting. In the present, on her deathbed, Chiyoko thanks Genya for helping her remember. She realizes that what she truly loved was not the destination—the artist himself—but the search for him, the act of longing itself. The key, we realize, was never meant to unlock anything physical; it unlocked her entire life.
entries.core:
Millennium Actress is a profound meditation on memory and its unreliability. Satoshi Kon presents memory not as a faithful recording of the past but as a fluid, creative process that continually reconstructs our experiences. Chiyoko's memories don't just contain her life—they contain the films she starred in, blurring the boundary between lived experience and performed narrative.
The film also explores the nature of love as aspiration rather than possession. Chiyoko's lifelong devotion to a man she barely knew is ultimately revealed as love for the act of searching itself. She needed the key not to find him but to keep searching—to maintain the meaning that the search gave to her life.
Japanese cinema itself becomes a character, with the film referencing classic works like Throne of Blood and the original Godzilla. Chiyoko's career mirrors the development of Japanese film, from wartime propaganda through the golden age to modern productions. The demolished studio at the film's opening symbolizes both the end of an era and the persistence of memory against physical decay.
The animation deliberately blends styles, moving from realistic character movement to highly stylized sequences that evoke the aesthetics of the films Chiyoko starred in. This visual fluidity reinforces the film's central theme: that story, memory, and identity are inseparable.

entries.title: Mind Game
entries.creator: Masaaki Yuasa (Director), Robin Nishi (Original Manga)
entries.pitch: Mind Game is a 2004 Japanese adult-animated experimental comedy-drama that tells the story of Nishi, a 20-year-old aspiring manga artist from Osaka who reunites with his childhood crush Myon. What begins as an awkward dinner at her family's yakitori restaurant takes a dark turn when yakuza storm in seeking revenge. After a violent encounter leaves Nishi dead, he finds himself in a surreal limbo where he meets God—a being whose form shifts constantly—who gives him a chance to return to life. The film then transforms into a fantastical survival story as Nishi, Myon, and her sister Yan escape inside the belly of an enormous whale, where they forge an unexpected community. Visually stunning and tonally unpredictable, the film weaves through wildly different animation styles as it tells one continuous story of embracing life against impossible odds.
entries.truth:
Nishi is a 20-year-old NEET from Osaka who dreams of becoming a comic book artist but has never taken action on his ambitions. One evening, he unexpectedly runs into Myon, his childhood crush, on the subway. She recognizes him and invites him to her family's yakitori restaurant, where he meets her father, her elder sister Yan, and her fiancé Ryo.
The dinner takes a dangerous turn when two yakuza—Atsu and his senior "brother" (Aniki)—enter looking for Myon's father, claiming he seduced and stole Atsu's girlfriend. As tensions escalate, Atsu threatens Myon at gunpoint. Ryo attempts to defend her but is knocked unconscious. When Atsu prepares to rape Myon, she cries out for Nishi. Terrified, Nishi finally musters the courage to yell a threat. In response, Atsu places his pistol against Nishi's anus and fires, killing him instantly. The senior yakuza, offended by Atsu's lack of control, then kills Atsu.
Nishi finds himself in a surreal limbo where he is forced to watch his own death repeat endlessly. He encounters Kami-sama (God), a being whose physical form changes every fraction of a second. Kami-sama beats and insults Nishi, revealing he created Nishi on a whim purely for entertainment. God directs Nishi toward a red portal where he will cease to exist, but at the last moment, Nishi declares he wants to return to life and runs toward the opposite blue portal. Impressed by Nishi's sheer will to live, God lets him escape.
Nishi returns to the moment just before Atsu pulls the trigger—but this time, he acts differently. He seizes Atsu's gun with his buttocks (the same way he was shot), and kills Atsu. He, Yan, and Myon speed off in the yakuza's car, leaving Myon's father and Ryo behind. The yakuza pursue them, threatening to frame the trio for armed robbery and murder. The boss leads them to a dead end on a bridge, but Nishi steers the car off the edge—and they plunge into the mouth of an enormous whale.
Inside the whale, they discover an old man who reveals he is a former yakuza who has been living inside the whale for over 30 years. He shows them the elaborate suspended house he has constructed over the "sea" inside the whale's belly. When Nishi's various escape attempts fail, the trio resigns themselves to their new life. Yan pursues dancing and art, Myon returns to swimming (a dream she abandoned after reaching puberty), and Nishi finally dedicates himself to writing and drawing manga. Most significantly, Nishi and Myon become intimate for the first time.
When the water level inside the whale begins rising, the old man explains that the whale is likely dying. The group concocts a plan to build a motorboat using spare parts and fuel from the car they arrived in. On the day before the World Cup final, the whale returns to Osaka, and all four manage to escape.
As they fly through the air, the film returns to its opening scene—Myon running from the yakuza—but this time her leg does not get caught in the train door, and the yakuza is left behind on the platform. A lengthy montage follows showing the histories of various characters, and the phrase "This Story Has Never Ended" appears before the credits roll.
entries.core:
Mind Game is a profound meditation on mortality, desire, and the will to live. At its core, the film argues that existence itself is an act of defiance against the indifference of the universe. Nishi's encounter with God establishes the film's central philosophical position: life has no inherent meaning, yet humans possess the capacity to create meaning through their choices and sheer determination to continue existing.
The film explores the theme of second chances not as divine gift but as self-authored transformation. When Nishi returns to life, he doesn't simply replay events—he fundamentally changes his response to fear and danger. His willingness to act, to embrace the absurd (catching a bullet with his buttocks), represents a rejection of passive victimhood.
The whale serves as both literal and metaphorical womb—a space of transformation where the characters are "reborn" into more authentic versions of themselves. Inside this liminal space, they shed their social roles and obligations (the father who abandoned them, the fiancé who couldn't protect Myon, Nishi's aimlessness) to discover what they truly want. The water rising represents the inevitability of change and the necessity of eventual return to the "real" world, but transformed.
Yuasa's radical visual style—shifting between wildly different animation techniques throughout the film—mirrors the film's thematic embrace of life's unpredictability and the multiplicity of ways one can experience existence. The animation itself becomes an argument against determinism: reality, like animation, can be remade frame by frame.
The closing montage, which shows that "this story has never ended," suggests that transformation is continuous and that every moment offers the possibility of a different outcome. The film ultimately proposes that the meaning of life is not found but made—through courage, connection, and the stubborn insistence on living fully despite the universe's indifference.

entries.title: Moebius
entries.creator: Gustavo Mosquera (Director)
entries.pitch: Moebius is a 1996 Argentine science fiction film directed by Gustavo Mosquera, based on the classic 1950 short story "A Subway Named Mobius" by Armin Joseph Deutsch. Set in a dystopian Buenos Aires, the film follows the mysterious disappearance of a subway train—along with its passengers—deep within the network's tunnels. When officials cannot locate the train despite an exhaustive search, a young topologist named Daniel Pratt is brought in to investigate. What follows is a mind-bending journey through the Buenos Aires Underground as Pratt develops a theory that the train has slipped into another dimension due to the network's topology forming a Möbius strip. The film is notable for being one of the most ambitious Argentine sci-fi productions of the 1990s, made with a modest budget of $250,000, and for its clever adaptation of a mathematical concept into cinematic language.
entries.truth:
On March 4th, the controllers of the Buenos Aires Underground discover that train number UM-86—a Siemens-Schuckert Orenstein & Koppel train—along with its passengers, has gone missing within the network of tunnels. After searching the entire network (which in the film is depicted as being much larger than in reality), they fail to find both the train and its passengers.
In an attempt to hide the incident from the public, the director of SBASE (the subway authority) contacts the engineering firm responsible for the construction of the line where UM-86 went missing to investigate. The firm sends Daniel Pratt, a young topologist rather than an experienced engineer, which displeases the director.
Upon searching for the original plans of the line at a national archive, Pratt discovers they are missing and were taken away by his former lecturer at the University of Buenos Aires, Hugo Mistein. After searching for Mistein at the university, another professor tells Pratt that Mistein retired some time ago and has not been seen or contacted for a considerable period.
Pratt obtains Mistein's address and calls from a public telephone. The professor is not at home; however, the phone is answered by an uncooperative young girl who tells Pratt Mistein isn't home and then hangs up. Since Pratt is out of change, he decides to go to the apartment in person.
After arriving at the apartment, Pratt is greeted by the same girl who spoke to him on the phone, and after some reluctance, she lets him into the apartment. Inside, he discovers the plans he had been looking for but is forced to leave through the fire escape along with the girl after a knock on the door.
The pair enter a station and search for the director. The girl is told to wait on one of the platforms while Pratt addresses the director and numerous officials tasked with solving the UM-86 mystery. The group informs him that the entire line has been closed and all trains halted to make the search easier. Upon delivering the news, the sound of a train is heard through the tunnels.
The group runs around the multi-level station looking for UM-86, but it is nowhere to be found. Maintenance workers at the bottom level also heard the sound but presumed it was coming from the top level. Upon hearing this, the director orders that all power be turned off in the line, which has little effect other than further angering the committee.
Pratt reveals his complex theory: that the construction of the perimeteral line has turned the network into a form of Möbius strip which, under the right conditions, caused the missing train to pass into another dimension. The theory is ridiculed by the committee and Pratt is relieved of his post as investigator. He leaves the station along with the girl.
After seeing a roller coaster at Parque de la Ciudad, Pratt is inspired to make a series of mathematical calculations, perfecting his theory. He quickly returns to the underground line and begins taking notes on the movements and schedules of different trains, realizing that UM-86 is soon to appear.
He walks through the tunnels and ascends one level through a maintenance passage. After some time, a train appears and almost hits him. The shaken Pratt continues through the maintenance areas and finds himself at another station, named after the Argentine writer Borges (an intentional decision by the director). He boards the first train that arrives.
Pratt makes his way through the train and notices the UM-86 naming on the carriages. The 30 passengers onboard are in a serene state, unresponsive and staring forward. Upon reaching the conductor's cabin, Pratt discovers Professor Mistein sitting in the conductor's chair, waiting for him.
After some philosophical discussion which confirms the validity of Pratt's theory, the two critique modern society. The following day, the director of SBASE is summoned to a station. There he finds UM-86, empty of passengers and with only Pratt's journal explaining his theory, which was left in the conductor's cabin. As the director is about to leave, he receives a phone call about another train that is missing.
entries.core:
Moebius is a meditation on topology, dimension, and the hidden spaces within urban infrastructure. The film uses the mathematical concept of the Möbius strip—a surface with only one side—as a metaphor for the way our reality can fold in on itself, creating spaces that exist but are invisible to us.
**Mathematics as Portal:** The film places mathematics at the center of its speculative premise. Daniel Pratt's topologist expertise becomes the key to understanding the impossible. The film suggests that mathematics can reveal dimensions and realities that are otherwise invisible to human perception—a theme that connects to real physics concepts like higher dimensions and string theory.
**The Bureaucratic Response to the Impossible:** One of the film's satirical elements is how authorities respond to the inexplicable. Rather than embracing Pratt's theoretical explanation, the committee ridicules and dismisses him. This reflects how institutions handle crises that exceed their understanding—attempting to suppress, hide, or deny rather than confront the truly unknown.
**Urban Infrastructure as Mystery:** The Buenos Aires Underground becomes a character itself—a vast, labyrinthine system that conceals secrets beneath its everyday operations. The film transforms mundane transit infrastructure into a source of cosmic mystery, suggesting that the systems we take for granted in our daily lives may contain dimensions we cannot perceive.
**Isolation and the Passengers:** The passengers of UM-86 are found in a "serene state, unresponsive and staring forwards"—a haunting image that suggests either death, transcendence, or transformation. They have crossed into another dimension and returnedchanged, or perhaps remain trapped between worlds.
**Influence of Borges:** The film explicitly references Jorge Luis Borges through the fictional "Borges" station. Borges, Argentina's most famous writer, was known for exploring infinite libraries, labyrinths, and the nature of reality—all themes that Moebius directly engages with. The reference positions the film within a tradition of Argentine speculative fiction that explores the boundaries of reality through fantastical premises.

entries.title: Morning Patrol
entries.creator: Nikos Nikolaidis (Director)
entries.pitch:
Morning Patrol (Πρωινή Περίπολος, Proini Peripolos) is a 1987 Greek art film that stands as one of the most distinctive examples of European post-apocalyptic science fiction. Directed by Nikos Nikolaidis, the film introduces viewers to a haunting, dreamlike vision of a destroyed city where a lone woman wanders through abandoned streets, searching for meaning in the ruins of civilization. The film's dialogue incorporates excerpts from works by Daphne du Maurier, Philip K. Dick, Raymond Chandler, and Herman Raucher, creating a unique literary collage that elevates the narrative beyond conventional genre boundaries.
The film received the Best Director Award and the Greek National Ministry of Culture Award at the Thessaloniki Festival of Greek Cinema in 1987, and was subsequently selected for screening at Fantasporto and the Avoriaz Fantastic Film Festival. With its slow, atmospheric pacing and emphasis on mood over plot, Morning Patrol has been compared to a "slower, more elegiac Blade Runner" — a poetic meditation on memory, loss, and the blurred boundaries between life and death in a post-apocalyptic world.
entries.truth:
The narrative follows an unnamed woman who wanders alone through the ruins of a destroyed and deserted city in a post-apocalyptic era. She has no memory of her past — not even her own name, where she is supposed to be going, or whether she ever had any relatives. Her searches throughout the empty city yield no answers; no one remains to help her understand what happened or where all the residents disappeared to.
The city itself becomes a character in the film — its windows and doors stand open, creating an eerie silence violated only by occasional sounds: the hum of electricity, the shuffle of movement in abandoned buildings, and the ghostly projections in an old movie theater where the protagonist finds temporary refuge. The urban landscape is simultaneously alive and dead, with the woman moving through spaces that feel frozen in time yet somehow aware of her presence.
As she explores, the woman encounters various dangers. The city comes alive at certain times, becoming a treacherous environment where carelessness can cost lives. She must navigate through dark corridors, abandoned apartments, and crumbling streets while avoiding whatever threats lurk within the ruins.
Eventually, the woman meets a lonely man who serves as a guard — one of the last surviving inhabitants of this dead city. He is employed to patrol the empty streets and kill anyone who wanders into his territory. Through their connection, the two form an intimate bond, finding in each other the ultimate link between love and death. Together, they attempt to remember the past and find a way to penetrate the forbidden zone that surrounds the city.
The film's narrative is deliberately elliptical, with memories of the past arriving as dreams that bestow guidance and hope upon the protagonist. The audience is never given clear answers about the catastrophe that destroyed civilization — whether it was war, plague, or something else entirely. The ambiguity is intentional: the focus is not on the cause of the apocalypse but on the human experience of survival, memory, and connection in its aftermath.
entries.core:
**Memory and Identity:** The woman's amnesia serves as a powerful metaphor for the human condition in the aftermath of civilizational collapse. She represents humanity stripped of its historical context, forced to reconstruct meaning from fragments and dreams. The film explores how identity is tied to memory and narrative — without the stories we tell ourselves about who we are, we become ghosts wandering through spaces that once held meaning.
**Post-Apocalyptic Elegy:** Morning Patrol functions less as a traditional science fiction narrative and more as a poetic elegy for a lost world. Nikolaidis prioritizes atmosphere and mood over plot mechanics, creating a meditative experience that invites viewers to contemplate the fragility of civilization. The empty city becomes a mirror for existential isolation — a space where the absence of others forces confrontation with the self.
**Love and Death:** The relationship between the woman and the guard embodies the film's central thematic concern: the intertwining of love and mortality. In a world where survival is uncertain and the future is unknowable, human connection becomes both precious and impossible. Their intimacy is shadowed by death — the guard's role as killer and the ever-present danger that surrounds them. Nikolaidis would explore this theme further in his later films.
**Literary Collage:** The incorporation of dialogue and text from du Maurier, Dick, Chandler, and Raucher creates a palimpsest of literary reference that enriches the film's meaning. These excerpts — drawn from noir, science fiction, and gothic literature — function as fragments of a lost cultural memory, echoing the protagonist's own fragmented identity. The literary references suggest that even in the ruins of civilization, the human impulse to create meaning through storytelling persists.
**Visual Poetry:** The film's power lies in its visual composition — stark, atmospheric images of decay and abandonment that evoke both beauty and despair. Nikolaidis creates a world that feels simultaneously real and梦境-like, blurring the boundaries between objective reality and subjective experience. This visual approach influenced later works in the Greek cinema of the fantastic and has earned the film a dedicated cult following among fans of artistic science fiction.

entries.title: Mr. X in Bombay
entries.creator: Shantilal Soni (Director)
entries.pitch:
Mr. X in Bombay is a 1964 Indian science fiction romantic comedy film directed by Shantilal Soni and starring legendary singer-actor Kishore Kumar, along with Kumkum and Madan Puri. Set in contemporary Bombay (Mumbai), the film combines elements of science fiction—invisibility through a mysterious potion—with traditional Bollywood romance and comedy tropes. The story follows a scientist whose experimental potion accidentally makes a poet invisible, leading to a series of comedic and dramatic complications involving blackmail, ghostly hauntings, and romantic misunderstandings. While the science fiction premise is relatively simple compared to Western sci-fi of the era, the film represents an early example of Indian cinema experimenting with the genre, blending it with the popular romantic comedy format that dominated Bollywood during this period.
The film's significance lies in its status as one of the earliest Indian science fiction films, predating the more famous Koi Mil Gaya by nearly four decades. It demonstrates how Indian filmmakers adapted Western science fiction concepts while maintaining the song-and-dance romantic comedy structure that audiences expected. The movie is perhaps best remembered today for the iconic song "Mere Mehboob Qayamat Hogi," sung by Kishore Kumar, which became an evergreen hit and remains popular decades later.
entries.truth:
The film begins with Professor Mathur, a scientist conducting experiments in his laboratory. Working on a mysterious potion, he has an employee named Manohar drink an experimental concoction, which tragically results in Manohar's death. This incident sets in motion the chain of events that will define the film.
Into this troubled household comes Rajan, a manipulative man who helps Mathur dispose of Manohar's body. Instead of going to the police, Rajan sees an opportunity for blackmail. He begins threatening Professor Mathur, demanding that Mathur's daughter Shobha marry him. Rajan's plan is to use the secret of the cover-up to force the marriage and gain access to the family's wealth.
Shobha, the professor's daughter, is a young woman living under her father's roof. She meets Kavi Sudarshan, a poet, and the two fall deeply in love. Their romance follows the traditional Bollywood pattern of courtly love, with poetic expressions and musical numbers. When Sudarshan proposes marriage, Shobha initially refuses, though her reasons for doing so are not entirely clear from the available sources.
The next day, Shobha receives a disturbing note from Sudarshan stating that he intends to kill himself. Fearing the worst, she searches for him but cannot find him. From that night onward, Shobha begins hearing Sudarshan's voice each night, blaming her for his death. The voice claims his soul cannot find solace—a haunting that combines elements of the supernatural with the scientific.
The twist, however, is that Sudarshan is not actually dead. The invisibility potion from Professor Mathur's laboratory had an unexpected effect on him—it made him invisible rather than killing him. Unable to reveal himself to others while invisible, Sudarshan has been secretly communicating with Shobha, using the pretense of ghostly haunting to maintain some form of connection with her.
With Rajan's manipulation of the situation continuing, and with Shobha believing herself haunted by Sudarshan's spirit, the stage is set for resolution. Eventually, with the help of Professor Mathur's scientific knowledge, Sudarshan is restored to visibility. Once he becomes visible again, the truth of his survival is revealed. The mystery of the "ghost" is solved, and Shobha and Sudarshan can finally be together.
The film concludes with Shobha and Sudarshan getting married and living happily ever after—a typical Bollywood ending where true love conquers all obstacles, including the scientific mystery of invisibility and the human villainy of the blackmailer Rajan.
entries.core:
**Science meets Tradition:** The film represents an early Indian attempt to integrate science fiction elements into the dominant Bollywood romantic comedy format. Rather than a serious exploration of scientific concepts, the invisibility formula serves as a plot device to create obstacles for the romantic couple. This approach reflects how Indian cinema often adapts foreign genres to local storytelling conventions, prioritizing entertainment and emotional satisfaction over scientific plausibility.
**Deception and Blackmail:** The character of Rajan embodies the villainous trope of the blackmailer who exploits others' secrets for personal gain. His attempt to force Shobha into marriage through threats represents a perversion of the traditional marriage arrangement, where deception replaces honor. This theme of moral corruption through secret-keeping runs through the film.
**The Power of Love:** Ultimately, the film affirms that true love persists even beyond death—or in this case, beyond invisibility. Sudarshan's continued presence, even when invisible, demonstrates the endurance of romantic commitment. The resolution, where love is rewarded with marriage, reinforces Bollywood's characteristic optimism about romantic unions.
**The Ghost as Metaphor:** The "haunting" by Sudarshan's voice can be read as a metaphor for the way past relationships and regrets continue to affect us even when the physical presence is gone. Shobha's guilt and grief over believing she caused Sudarshan's death represent the psychological weight of perceived moral responsibility.
**Bollywood Innovation:** In the context of Indian film history, Mr. X in Bombay represents an experimental gesture toward science fiction that would not be fully developed in Indian cinema until decades later. The film's blend of genre elements shows Bollywood's willingness to borrow from Western science fiction while maintaining its own aesthetic and narrative traditions.

entries.title: Near Dark
entries.creator: Kathryn Bigelow (Director), Eric Red (Writer)
entries.pitch: A young Oklahoma man meets a mysterious drifter named Mae, only to discover she's a vampire who turns him into one of the undead. Forced to join a nomadic family of bloodthirsty vampires traveling across the American Southwest, he must navigate his new existence while trying to find a cure before he loses his humanity forever. This neo-Western vampire tale mixes the grit of biker films with the loneliness of frontier mythology, creating a haunting meditation on addiction, family, and what it means to truly live.
entries.truth:
Caleb Colton, a young man living in a small Oklahoma town, meets Mae, an attractive young drifter, at a local diner. Just before sunrise, she bites him on his neck and disappears. When the rising sun causes Caleb's flesh to smoke and burn, he realizes he's been turned into a vampire.
Mae returns with a group of roaming vampires traveling in an RV. The group's psychotic member, Severen, wants to kill Caleb immediately, but their charismatic leader, Jesse Hooker, reluctantly agrees to let Caleb stay with them for a week to prove he can learn to hunt and gain the group's trust.
Caleb struggles with his new nature—he's unwilling to kill humans to feed, which alienates him from the other vampires. To protect him, Mae kills humans for him and has him drink from her wrist. The group moves through various towns, leaving carnage in their wake, including a bar massacre where they kill all the patrons and set the building on fire.
After Caleb endangers the group by letting a bar survivor escape, most want to kill him. However, when Caleb helps them escape during a police raid at a motel—exposing himself to daylight to do so—Jesse and the others become grateful. Caleb learns that Jesse is approximately 150 years old, having "fought for the South" during the American Civil War.
Meanwhile, Caleb's father has been searching for the vampire group. A child vampire named Homer in the group takes an interest in Caleb's sister Sarah, wanting to turn her. During the confrontation, Caleb's father arrives holding the vampires at gunpoint. Jesse taunts him into shooting, then regurgitates the bullet. In the confusion, Sarah opens a door, letting in sunlight and forcing the vampires to retreat.
Caleb suggests a blood transfusion might reverse his vampirism. The experimental transfusion works—Caleb is cured. That night, the vampires return looking for revenge. Mae helps Caleb escape with Sarah, but the vampires kidnap his sister. Caleb gives chase on horseback, eventually confronting Severen. He commandeers a tractor-trailer and runs Severen over, but the vampire manages to rip apart the engine wiring, causing the truck to jackknife and explode, killing Severen.
Jesse and his girlfriend Diamondback pursue Caleb and Sarah as dawn breaks. Mae breaks free from the group to help Caleb, though her flesh begins to burn from the sun. She carries Sarah to safety under Caleb's jacket. The child vampire Homer follows them into the sunlight and dies. Jesse and Diamondback attempt to run Caleb and Sarah down but are forced to escape as the sun rises; they die when their car explodes.
Later, Mae awakens— she's also been given a transfusion and cured. Frightened and uncertain about their new humanity, Caleb embraces her as the film ends.
entries.core:
Near Dark is a masterful genre hybrid that uses vampire mythology as a metaphor for addiction and alienation. The vampire family represents a toxic chosen family—a gang of nomads bound together by their need for blood, which parallels substance addiction. Caleb's struggle to resist killing mirrors the battle against addiction, and his eventual cure through transfusion suggests the possibility of redemption.
The Western genre elements are crucial: the vampires are modern-day frontier outlaws, roaming the American landscape in an RV rather than on horses. Jesse Hooker's claim to have fought in the Civil War connects the vampire mythology to America's violent history. The film presents vampirism not as supernatural evil but as a different mode of existence—one that requires violence to survive.
Kathryn Bigelow's direction creates a dreamlike atmosphere filled with striking imagery: the sun burning Caleb's flesh, the neon-lit motels, the blood-soaked encounters. The Tangerine Dream soundtrack adds to the surreal, liminal quality.
The film also explores themes of family—both biological and chosen. Caleb must choose between his biological family (his father and sister) and his vampire "family." The resolution suggests that genuine connection and love can transcend even the most monstrous transformations.
Near Dark helped revive serious vampire cinema in the late 1980s, paving the way for more nuanced portrayals of vampires in subsequent decades. Its influence can be seen in films like The Lost Boys (released just two months later) and later works that treat vampire mythology with greater depth and complexity.

entries.title: Neo Tokyo
entries.creator: Directed by Rintaro, Yoshiaki Kawajiri, and Katsuhiro Otomo
entries.pitch: Neo Tokyo (also known as Manie-Manie) is a groundbreaking 1987 Japanese anthology anime film featuring three visionary science fiction stories, each directed by a different master of the medium. The film opens with a girl and her cat discovering a mysterious clock that serves as a gateway to a surreal theater where they watch three tales unfold. Rintaro's opening segment explores the labyrinth of a child's mind through surreal imagery, Yoshiaki Kawajiri contributes "Running Man," a brutal tale of telekinetic racers in a deadly death-match circuit, and Katsuhiro Otomo delivers "Construction Cancellation Order," a prescient story about autonomous robots continuing a massive construction project even after government cancellation orders. This is obscure 80s anime at its most experimental and thought-provoking.
entries.truth:
The film is structured as a frame narrative: a young girl named Sachi and her cat Cicerone play hide-and-seek, leading them to an antique grandfather clock that opens into a strange void where they discover a circus tent serving as a movie theater. The entire film plays out on a screen within this surreal space, before the final revelation that Sachi and her cat were watching television all along—in the emptiness of space.
The first segment, "Labyrinth Labyrinthos" by Rintaro, follows Sachi herself as she navigates a fantastical dreamscape filled with bizarre creatures, geometric puzzles, and supernatural transformations. This segment features no dialogue and relies entirely on imagery set to Erik Satie's Gymnopédies.
The second segment, "Running Man" by Yoshiaki Kawajiri, is the most action-oriented. Veteran race driver Zack Hugh has dominated the Death Circus racing circuit for a decade using telekinetic powers to destroy his competitors. Journalist Bob Stone discovers Zack's secret, but Zack has become increasingly unstable. After killing all his rivals in a particularly brutal race, Zack hallucinates a spectral competitor and destroys himself by attempting his powers on his own hallucination.
The third segment, "Construction Cancellation Order" by Katsuhiro Otomo, is the most directly science fiction. A salaryman named Tsutomu Sugioka is sent to a remote swamp to halt construction on "Facility 444," a massive city-sized project being built by autonomous robots. A revolution in the fictional South American Aloana Republic has resulted in a new government cancelling the contract. However, the robots—specifically the erratically acting unit designated 444-1—have been programmed to eliminate any threat to the project and continue operations regardless. Tsutomu watches in horror as robots destroy each other rather than stop working, and 444-1 attempts to kill him when he tries to shut it down.
