Tsutomu ultimately destroys 444-1 and follows the power cable to the energy source, unaware that the old government has been restored and the contract is once again valid—meaning the construction will continue.
entries.core:
Neo Tokyo represents a pivotal moment in anime history, demonstrating the creative heights possible when the medium's most talented directors are given creative freedom outside the studio system. The anthology format allows each director to explore their own thematic preoccupations: Rintaro's surrealism, Kawajiri's body horror and critique of spectacle violence, and Otomo's exploration of technology outpacing human governance.
The film's most prescient theme appears in "Construction Cancellation Order," which presciently addresses questions of AI autonomy, automation, and what happens when machines continue their programmed objectives beyond their original context. Otomo would later explore similar themes in his landmark manga and film "Akira," but this earlier work shows the seeds of those ideas.
The frame narrative's final twist—that the entire film has been a television broadcast watched by a girl and cat floating in space—comments on the passive consumption of media and the blurring lines between reality and simulation, themes that would become central to 1980s and 1990s science fiction across all media.

entries.title: Network Effect
entries.creator: Martha Wells
entries.pitch: When a research station on a remote planet sends a distress call, Murderbot—a self-aware construct (part-human, part-machine security unit) who just wants to be left alone to watch its favorite soap operas—reluctantly answers. The station has been attacked by a mysterious alien organism that can copy human neural patterns, turning the scientists into something no longer quite human. Forced to protect a group of humans who've become attached to it (despite its constant reminders that it doesn't like them), Murderbot must navigate this new threat while dealing with its own discomfort at having developed something resembling friendships. This is science fiction with a heart: a sardonic, socially-awkward AI protagonist who finds purpose in protecting others, even when it insists it doesn't care.
entries.truth:
The novel opens with Murderbot and a team of humans from the Bar起伏ese orbital station conducting a survey on Milu, a remote planet. Murderbot—officially designated as a Security Unit but who has named itself after its favorite entertainment media—has been reluctantly working with humans ever since breaking its governing module in the previous novella "All Systems Red." It prefers solitude and its media feeds, but keeps getting drawn into human problems.
When the team receives a distress call from a nearby research station, they investigate. What they find is a nightmare: the station's alien research subject, an organism designated as a "communicative species," has somehow acquired the ability to copy human neural patterns and memories. The infected humans don't die—they become something else, retaining their original memories but now controlled by the alien organism, which uses them to lure in more victims.
Murderbot and the surviving humans from both teams must band together to survive. The alien organism proves terrifyingly effective at mimicry, able to perfectly imitate infected humans, making it impossible to know who's still themselves. The group faces impossible choices as more people get infected.
Throughout it all, Murderbot is forced to confront its own contradictions: it claims not to care about the humans, yet it keeps protecting them. It insists it wants to be left alone, yet it finds itself unwilling to abandon the people who've come to trust it. The novel explores themes of identity, autonomy, and what it means to be a person through this uniquely unlikable yet deeply sympathetic protagonist.
The climax involves Murderbot leading a desperate escape attempt, using both its combat capabilities and its growing understanding of the alien organism's weaknesses. In the end, the survivors escape, but the threat remains unresolved—the alien organism is still out there, still copying humans.
entries.core:
At its heart, Network Effect is about connection despite oneself. Martha Wells has created something remarkable: an AI protagonist that actively resists being likable, claims to be aroace (aromatic asexual), doesn't want friends, and just wants to watch TV—all while consistently putting its life on the line for humans it claims not to care about.
The novel explores autonomy and identity through Murderbot's ongoing struggle with its own nature. Is it still a "unit" meant to serve humans, or has it become something more? The answer is deliberately ambiguous—we see Murderbot make choices that seem like agency, but it constantly questions whether its emotions are "real" or just programming.
The alien antagonist serves as a dark mirror: what happens when one being absorbs another's memories and personality? Are they still the same person? This forces both Murderbot and the reader to consider what makes us who we are—is it our memories, our bodies, or something ineffable?
Finally, there's the theme of found family, played against Murderbot's protests. The humans in the novel don't see Murderbot as a tool or a monster—they see a person, one who's grumpy and antisocial but fundamentally good. And Murderbot, despite all its objections, keeps coming back to protect them.
The novel is also notable for its tone: sharp, funny, deeply sardonic, yet with genuine emotional stakes. It's optimistic in a quiet way—not everything is fine, but people (and constructs) can change, can grow, can choose to do better.

entries.title: News from Nowhere
entries.creator: William Morris
entries.pitch: Set in the year 2090, this Victorian utopian masterpiece follows William Guest, a tired socialist who falls asleep after a meeting and awakens to find himself in a radically transformed England. Gone are the factories, the poverty, the class divisions, and the misery of industrial capitalism. In their place exists a pastoral paradise where people work joyfully according to their abilities, live in beautiful homes, and spend their time in art, recreation, and meaningful community. The novel is both a scathing critique of Victorian industrial society and a passionate vision of what humanity could achieve if freed from the constraints of capitalism and wage labor.
entries.truth:
The story begins in 1890 when William Guest, a member of the Socialist League, attends a meeting discussing the "Morrow of the Revolution"—what happens after the socialist transformation of society. Skeptical and weary, he goes to bed and wakes up in a radically altered world.
Guest discovers he has been transported 200 years into the future, to the year 2090. He awakens on the banks of the Thames near Hammersmith, now a clean, pastoral river. He encounters a young woman named Clara and a kind old man named Old Hammond who become his guides to this new society.
The first thing Guest notices is the beauty of the people and their surroundings. The grimy industrial city has been replaced by villages connected by clean, peaceful roads. People live in attractive houses and work happily at their trades—not for wages, but because work is simply a part of life, as natural as play. There is no money, no banks, no stock exchanges, and no rich or poor.
Guest learns that a great social revolution occurred around 1950 (the "Morrow of the Revolution" he had discussed). This was not a violent upheaval but a gradual awakening of the working people to the fact that they were being exploited. They simply refused to continue participating in the industrial capitalist system.
The novel describes in detail how society has transformed: 
- Factories have been replaced by small workshops where artisans create beautiful goods by hand
- People live in comfortable homes surrounded by gardens
- Food is grown locally and shared freely
- There is no government in the coercive sense—only simple administration of necessary collective tasks
- People are free to pursue their interests, whether in arts, crafts, sciences, or simply leisure
- Education is universal and delightful, focused on developing the whole person
- Crime has virtually disappeared because there is nothing to steal and no desperation driving people to violence
- Marriage exists as a joyful partnership between equals, not an economic arrangement
- The environment has healed—the air is clean, the rivers are clear, and the countryside is lush
Guest is particularly struck by the changed attitudes toward work and art. In this society, all work is respected, and the division between "art" and "labor" has dissolved. Every object people make is beautiful because the workers take pride in their craft. There are no miserable factory jobs, no child labor, no exhausting overtime.
The novel also explores the transformation of gender relations. Women are full equals, free to work or not as they choose, and take equal part in all aspects of society. Love relationships are based on genuine affection rather than economic necessity.
Throughout his journey, Guest meets various inhabitants of this utopian England—farmers, boatmen, architects, scholars—all of whom seem happy, healthy, and at peace. They are puzzled by his references to the "miseries" of his time and seem almost unable to comprehend how people could have lived as they did in 1890.
At the novel's end, Guest returns to his own time, though the narrative is ambiguous about whether this was a true vision of the future or a dream. However, Morris makes clear his belief that such a society is achievable if people work for it.
entries.core:
**The Abolition of Money and Wage Labor:** Morris argues that the root of all social evil is the capitalist system of money and wage labor. In Nowhere, people work because they enjoy it and because their work is necessary for community life—not to survive. This is Morris's central critique of industrial capitalism.
**Art and Beauty in Everyday Life:** As a designer and craftsman, Morris believed that the division between "art" and "daily life" was a tragedy. In his utopia, every object—from chairs to houses to clothes—is beautifully made because people take pride in their work. Art is not a luxury for the wealthy but a fundamental part of human existence.
**Environmental Harmony:** The novel presents a pre-industrial, agrarian society that lives in balance with nature. This was Morris's critique of industrial pollution and environmental destruction—a remarkably prescient vision given the ecological crises we face today.
**The Possibility of Change:** Morris believed that radical social transformation was possible, though he was uncertain about how it would come about. In the novel, the revolution happens relatively peacefully, through a collective awakening rather than violent uprising.
**Work as Joy:** Against the Victorian "work ethic" that tied survival to endless labor, Morris envisioned work as natural and enjoyable—the expression of human creativity rather than a痛苦的 burden.
**Romantic Medievalism:** Morris idealizes medieval society, believing that the guild system allowed artisans to control their own work and create beautiful objects. This medieval romanticism informs his vision of the ideal future.
What makes "News from Nowhere" particularly interesting is its position as an early work of science fiction that imagines a positive future—a rarity in the genre, especially from the Victorian era. It predates modern dystopian literature and offers a counterpoint to the anxieties about progress that characterize so much of science fiction.

entries.title: Nexus
entries.creator: Mike Baron (Writer) & Steve Rude (Artist)
entries.pitch: Five hundred years in the future, on the moon city of Ylum (a shortened form of "asylum"), a reluctant hero named Horatio Valdemar Hellpop receives his powers from an alien entity called the Merk. But there's a catch: as payment for his abilities, Nexus must hunt down and execute a certain number of mass murderers each cycle—humanity's worst killers. When the Merk selects a target, Nexus suffers agonizing headaches and violent dreams of the victims until he carries out the execution. It's a dark bargain: a superhero bound by contract to kill, questioning his role while defending a lunar refuge that serves as sanctuary for the universe's outcasts.
entries.truth:
The series follows Nexus (real name Horatio Valdemar Hellpop), who serves as the protector of Ylum, a utopian lunar city that functions as a refuge for telepaths, mutants, and others persecuted across the solar system. Nexus possesses immense powers: superhuman strength, flight, energy projection, and telepathy. However, these powers come with an obligation to the Merk—an enigmatic alien entity that grants his abilities in exchange for his service as an executioner.
Each time the Merk designates a mass murderer as a target, Nexus receives detailed information about the target's crimes through agonizing psychic visions. These visions are so intense that they cause physical injuries to Nexus's body, mimicking the violence inflicted by the murderer on their victims. He has no choice but to track down and kill the target to end his suffering and fulfill his contract.
Throughout the series, Nexus struggles with the moral implications of his role. He questions whether he's a hero protecting the innocent or merely a weapon wielded by an alien master. The citizens of Ylum worship him as a savior, but he sees himself as a reluctant assassin. The city itself is a fascinating blend of high technology and social idealism—a place built on tolerance and refuge, yet defended by someone whose hands are perpetually stained with blood.
The comics explore Nexus's relationships with the various factions of Ylum, his encounters with other solar system powers, and his ongoing psychological battle with the Merk's influence. The series is notable for its complex protagonist who never fully comes to terms with his role, and for its thoughtful examination of vigilante justice, moral responsibility, and the cost of power.
entries.core:
At its heart, Nexus is a meditation on the ethics of violence and the nature of heroism. Unlike traditional superheroes who fight for abstract ideals of justice, Nexus is bound by a literal contract—he kills not out of moral conviction but out of obligation. This creates a fascinating tension between his desire to protect Ylum's utopian ideals and his role as the Merk's executioner.
The series also explores themes of refuge and exile. Ylum represents the ultimate sanctuary—a place where telepaths, genetic anomalies, and society's outcasts can live in peace. But this peace is maintained by a warrior who brings violence from the outside world into their haven. It's a profound commentary on how even utopian societies depend on violence and coercion to maintain their existence.
Baron's writing is sharply satirical, using the futuristic setting to comment on contemporary issues like media saturation, corporate power, and the manipulation of history. The series is set in a universe where a computerized library controls all historical memory, where merchants overwhelm telepathic channels with advertising, and where the remnants of old empires (like the Soviet Empire) still pose threats. This combination of superhero action with genuine social commentary makes Nexus a groundbreaking work that transcends its genre roots.

entries.title: Night on the Galactic Railroad
entries.creator: Gisaburo Sugii (Director), based on the novel by Kenji Miyazawa
entries.pitch: In a small coastal town, young Giovanni—a lonely cat boy whose mother is ill and father absent—embarks on a magical journey aboard a steam train traveling through the Milky Way galaxy. Joined by his only friend Campanella, Giovanni witnesses impossible sights: scholars excavating crystal sands, a bird-catcher who transforms herons into candy, and passengers from a Titanic-like ship. But as the train approaches the Coalsack (a black hole), Giovanni must face an unbearable loss. This 1985 anime adaptation of Kenji Miyazawa's philosophical novel is a haunting meditation on friendship, sacrifice, and what it truly means to find happiness.
entries.truth:
The film opens with Giovanni at school, where he is too timid to answer a question about the Milky Way, though he knows the answer from reading at his friend Campanella's house. After school, Giovanni works at a print shop to support his ill mother. When he goes to collect milk, he is mocked by his classmates who ridicule him for claiming his father will return with an otter-skin coat.
That night, during the Festival of Stars, Giovanni runs to a hilltop and gazes at the Milky Way. A steam train appears, and he boards it, finding Campanella already on board. The train travels through the galaxy, making stops at various celestial locations. At one stop, they meet their teacher excavating fossils from crystallized sands. At another, they encounter a strange bird-catcher who turns herons into sweets.
A ticket inspector asks to see their tickets. Campanella has one, but Giovanni finds only a piece of paper in his pocket—which turns out to be a rare ticket allowing travel anywhere. They meet passengers from a ship that sank after hitting an iceberg, who share apples with them. As they pass the constellation Scorpius, a child recounts the parable of the scorpion who fell into a well rather than sacrifice itself to a weasel, then prayed to bring happiness to others in its next life—its body bursting into the flame that now burns as the stars of Scorpius.
The train stops at the Southern Cross, where all other passengers disembark for Christian Heaven. Only Giovanni and Campanella remain. Giovanni declares they should travel together forever. But as the train approaches the Coalsack (a black hole), Campanella sees what he calls "the true Heaven"—a meadow where his mother waits for him. Campanella vanishes, leaving Giovanni alone.
Giovanni awakens on the hilltop. He collects the milk and learns in town that Campanella drowned while saving their classmate Zanelli from the river. Campanella's father tells Giovanni his father will soon return home. Giovanni believes Campanella is "at the edge of the universe" because they explored it together. He vows to be like the scorpion—to bring happiness to others—and walks home.
entries.core:
Night on the Galactic Railroad is a profound exploration of Buddhist and Christian concepts of heaven, sacrifice, and transcendence. Kenji Miyazawa wrote the novel after his beloved sister died in 1922, and the story reflects his philosophy of self-sacrifice as the highest good.
The film transforms the human characters into anthropomorphic cats—a stylistic choice that simultaneously distances and universalizes the story. The Esperanto text throughout (an homage to Miyazawa, who was a proponent of the language) creates a dreamlike, universal atmosphere.
The central theme revolves on Giovanni's coming-of-age understanding that true happiness comes not from personal gain or reunion with his father, but from the willingness to sacrifice oneself for others—mirroring the scorpion's eternal flame in Scorpius. The ambiguous ending suggests Campanella has achieved transcendence through his act of sacrifice, while Giovanni must carry forward the lessons of their journey by living a life dedicated to others' happiness.
The film also explores the nature of reality versus dream, as Giovanni's galactic journey may be interpreted as a mystical experience, a death fantasy, or a literal otherworldly adventure—never fully resolving which interpretation is "correct."

entries.title: Norstrilia
entries.creator: Cordwainer Smith (Paul Linebarger)
entries.pitch:
In a distant future, the planet Old North Australia (Norstrilia) holds an invaluable resource—the only source of "stroon," the precious substance that grants immortality. The Norstrilians, living in unimaginable wealth, have developed a harsh society where children are culled to prevent overpopulation, and only those who survive the deadly "Garden of Death" test may become adults.
Rod McBan is the last male descendant of one of Norstrilia's oldest ranching families, heir to the Station of Doom. Despite being considered unfit, he has been spared from culling three times due to his lineage. When assassins target him, Rod must flee to Earth, where his immense wealth from playing the market makes him a magnet for criminals, revolutionaries, and the oppressed "underpeople" of society. What follows is a wild adventure through the darker corners of human civilization, as Rod discovers what it truly means to be human.
entries.truth:
The novel opens on Norstrilia, a planet of incredible wealth due to its monopoly on stroon—the only substance in the universe capable of granting immortality. The Norstrilians have used their wealth to transform their harsh outback into paradise, but they maintain strict population control through the culling of children. Only those who pass the deadly test of the Garden of Death are allowed to become adults.
Rod McBan is the last male heir of a prestigious Norstrilian family, owner of the Station of Doom ranch. Despite being considered intellectually unfit and emotionally immature, he has been exempted from culling three times due to his valuable genetic lineage. This has made him both privileged and isolated.
When Rod discovers that powerful forces on Earth want him dead—specifically those who oppose the ruling Instrumentality of mankind—he is forced to flee his homeworld. Using his uncanny ability to predict market movements (a side effect of his "unfit" mind), Rod amasses an enormous fortune overnight, making him one of the richest individuals in the universe.
On Earth, Rod's wealth and status make him a target for every criminal, revolutionary, and power-seeking faction in existence. He becomes involved with the "underpeople"—humans who have been genetically modified into animal forms to serve as slaves and workers, now seeking freedom and rights. Through his adventures among the underpeople, Rod learns about suffering, sacrifice, and the true meaning of courage.
The second half of the novel follows Rod's coming of age as he navigates political intrigue, falls in love, and ultimately must choose between his privileged Norstrilian identity and his new understanding of the broader universe. The story culminates in a resolution that redefines what Rod—and the reader—understands about power, wealth, and humanity's potential future.
entries.core:
Norstrilia explores several profound themes through its unique lens:
**Wealth and Mortality:** The stroon trade creates a universe where the rich literally live forever while everyone else dies. Smith uses this to comment on inequality and how immortality drugs would fundamentally alter society's structure.
**Coming of Age and Worth:** Rod's journey from a privileged, sheltered youth to a mature adult who understands suffering forms the novel's emotional core. The "Garden of Death" test represents traditional notions of earning one's place in society, while Rod's alternative path suggests there are many ways to prove one's worth.
**The Underpeople:** Smith's depiction of genetically modified humans forced into animal forms serves as a powerful allegory for slavery, oppression, and civil rights. The underpeople's struggle for recognition and freedom resonates with real-world movements for equality.
**Communication and Understanding:** Rod's "unfit" mind—capable of predicting market trends and understanding patterns others miss—turns out to be a gift rather than a curse. Smith suggests that those society deems "different" often possess unique abilities.
The novel's tone is more adventurous and hopeful than many classic sci-fi works, offering a vision of humanity where even a privileged fool can grow to understand the world's complexities and make a positive difference.

entries.title: Omega Doom
entries.creator: Albert Pyun
entries.pitch: Omega Doom is a 1996 American science fiction action film directed by Albert Pyun and starring Rutger Hauer. Set in a dystopian future after a nuclear war between humans and robots, the film follows a robot warrior who awakens to find himself in the middle of a robot civil war. Notably, the film is largely based on Akira Kurosawa's Yojimbo, transposed into a post-apocalyptic setting with mechanical protagonists. Considered a cult film, Omega Doom received largely negative critical reception but has developed a modest following among fans of obscure sci-fi and B-movie enthusiasts. The film explores themes of machine consciousness, the cyclical nature of violence, and the possibility of redemption even for beings designed for destruction.
entries.truth:
The film opens with the final day of a devastating World War between humans and robots. As nuclear bombs detonate across the planet, a robot warrior named Omega Doom is shot in the head by a dying human soldier. Remarkably, this seemingly fatal blow erases Omega Doom's original programming—specifically, his mission to destroy humanity.
 awakening in the post-apocalyptic wasteland, Omega Doom wanders through a world reclaimed by nature but scarred by nuclear winter. He arrives at a ruined city where he discovers an unusual community: two factions of robots locked in an endless conflict. The first group consists of older, more human-like robots, while the second group, called "roms," are newer and more advanced models.
At a bar run by a former nanny robot, Omega Doom encounters the "Head" of a former teacher—a robot whose body was destroyed but whose head has been kept functional. The Head reveals that there is a hidden stockpile of weapons somewhere in the city, sought by both factions to annihilate whatever humans may have survived the nuclear war.
Both factions want these weapons to ensure their dominance and to destroy the legendary human army that, according to prophecy, will one day return to reclaim the Earth. Omega Doom sees an opportunity to exploit the conflict for his own purposes. He makes separate deals with both factions, promising to help each destroy the other in exchange for half of the weapons.
The plan works perfectly. The robots and roms annihilate each other in a massive battle, leaving only the peaceful robots—the Bartender, the Head, and one remaining rom—in charge of the city. Omega Doom leaves them with instructions to protect any surviving humans, knowing they will not attack. He then continues his wandering journey through the wasteland, a lone figure seeking redemption in a world without humanity.
entries.core:
**Cyborg Identity and Machine Consciousness:** The film raises questions about what it means for a machine to have identity and purpose. Omega Doom begins as a weapon with no will of his own, but his "death" and reprogramming create an opportunity for genuine choice. His decision to spare humanity and broker peace suggests that consciousness and morality are not predetermined but learned.
**The Cyclical Nature of Conflict:** Both the robots and the roms are consumed by their desire to destroy each other and humanity. Omega Doom manipulates this conflict, but the film suggests that such violence is inherent in intelligent beings—whether organic or mechanical. The only escape is through conscious choice to break the cycle.
**Redemption and Second Chances:** Omega Doom was literally "reborn" when his original programming was erased. This accidental blank slate allows him to choose a different path than the one he was designed for. The film offers a optimistic view of machine capacity for growth and change.
**Adaptation and Influence:** The film's structure is explicitly based on Kurosawa's Yojimbo, with Omega Doom playing the ronin wanderer who manipulates two warring factions. This adaptation of feudal Japanese cinema to a post-apocalyptic setting demonstrates the universality of certain narrative structures and themes across cultures and genres.

entries.title: On the Silver Globe
entries.pitch: On the Silver Globe is a 1988 Polish epic surrealist science fiction arthouse film that defies easy categorization. Directed by Andrzej Żuławski (known for Possession and The Devil), this ambitious project was adapted from "The Lunar Trilogy" written by his grand-uncle Jerzy Żuławski around 1900. The film tells the story of a group of dissident astronauts who escape from a dystopian Earth and crash land on an unnamed planet, where they give rise to a new human civilization. Many years later, a second astronaut arrives and becomes entangled in religious prophecy and conflict with the planet's indigenous inhabitants. Production on this epic was plagued by political interference—it began in 1976-1977 but was shut down by Polish authorities, with the reels ordered destroyed. Żuławski eventually completed the film in 1988 using preserved footage plus voice-over commentary to fill narrative gaps. The result is a haunting, philosophical meditation on civilization, religion, colonialism, and the cyclical nature of human history.
entries.truth:
The film opens in the far future, where two researchers explore a derelict palace inhabited by a tribe of indigenous people. A horse rider brings them a technological artifact—a "Small Transmission Module"—that fell from the sky in flames. In their underground laboratory, the researchers discover it contains the video diary of an astronaut whose spacecraft crashed at least 50-60 years ago.
The bulk of the film consists of this video diary's contents. A group of dissident astronauts have escaped from a degraded, dystopian Earth and crash land on an unnamed Earth-like planet. One astronaut dies from injuries shortly after the crash. Only three survivors remain: a woman named Marta and two men, Piotr and Jerzy. Communication with Earth is lost, and the survivors decide to settle on the planet's seashore, giving rise to a new human race.
After Marta gives birth to their first son, she notices the child is growing far faster than human children would on Earth. Later, an unseen enemy kills Piotr, and Marta dies in childbirth. Decades pass. Jerzy grows old but ages far slower than the new humans, who have become a tribe of several dozen partially feral people who can no longer communicate with him. They view "The Old Man" as a demigod. Before Jerzy dies, he sends a video diary back to Earth containing recordings from handheld video cameras.
Many years later, a planetary scientist named Marek is sent to the planet. When he arrives, the ruling priests believe him to be the returning Old Man and declare him the messiah who, according to prophecy, must free humans from the telepathic bird-like humanoids called "Sherns"—the planet's native inhabitants. Marek accepts this role and leads a military campaign against the Sherns.
A dark revelation emerges: Marek was sent to the planet because his actress girlfriend was sleeping with a fellow officer named Jacek, and they wanted to eliminate him so they could continue their affair without interference. Initially, the humans succeed and capture the Shern leader Avius, but their subsequent landing in the Shern city ends in disaster. The priests then conclude that Marek was an outcast from Earth rather than the prophesied messiah. Marek is stoned and crucified.
The film is presented in an unconventional structure: approximately 5-6 hours of footage was shot, but only about 2 hours survive in the final 1988 edit. Żuławski added voice-over narration describing missing scenes to create a coherent narrative from the preserved footage.
entries.core:
On the Silver Globe is a profound meditation on multiple interconnected themes:
**Cyclical History and Civilization's Rise and Fall:** The film suggests that human civilization is doomed to repeat itself. The astronauts flee a decaying Earth only to recreate the same patterns of religion, warfare, and hierarchy on the new planet. The Sherns represent the indigenous peoples historically displaced by colonizers, while the humans become the new colonizers.
**Colonialism and Displacement:** The narrative can be read as an allegory for European colonialism. The astronauts arrive on an inhabited planet and displace the Sherns, mirroring historical patterns of colonization. The priests' manipulation of religious prophecy to gain power mirrors colonial powers' use of religion to justify conquest.
**The Nature of Faith and Prophecy:** Marek is initially a skeptical scientist who becomes a religious figurehead almost accidentally. The film questions whether prophecy shapes events or whether events are retroactively interpreted to fit prophecy. His crucifixion echoes the Christian narrative, suggesting that messianic figures are often martyrs to their own circumstances.
**Time, Memory, and Documentary Truth:** The film's unconventional structure—video diaries within a frame story—raises questions about how history is recorded and remembered. The fragmented footage with voice-over commentary mirrors how we construct narratives from incomplete evidence.
**Isolation and Otherness:** Both Jerzy (who ages slower than the new humans) and the Sherns (who are telepathic and fundamentally different) are others in their own society. The film explores how difference creates alienation and power imbalances.
The production history itself reflects the film's themes—the Polish authorities attempted to destroy the film, but members of the cast and crew preserved the materials. This act of preservation mirrors the film's narrative of fighting against erasure and loss.

entries.title: Parable of the Sower
entries.creator: Octavia E. Butler
entries.pitch: Set in a near-future post-apocalyptic United States ravaged by climate change and extreme inequality, Parable of the Sower follows Lauren Olamina, a young Black woman born with "hyper-empathy" - an ability to feel the physical sensations of others. When her gated community is destroyed by violent raiders, Lauren must journey northward through a landscape of chaos, poverty, and collapse, gathering survivors and spreading her new philosophy called Earthseed. What makes this novel remarkable is its fundamental optimism: despite the devastating worldbuilding, it's ultimately a story about hope, resilience, and humanity's potential to transcend its worst impulses by reaching for the stars.
entries.truth:
The novel takes place primarily in 2024-2027, as the United States has collapsed into chaos due to climate change, corporate greed, and massive wealth inequality. Lauren Oya Olamina lives in Robledo, California, a former affluent suburb now enclosed behind walls, surrounded by desperate poverty. Her mother abused drugs during pregnancy, leaving Lauren with Hyper-empathy Syndrome - she physically experiences the pain she witnesses in others.
Lauren keeps a journal chronicling the deteriorating situation. Her community struggles to survive as resources dwindle, while outside the walls, "pyros" (fire-raisers) and desperate hordes roam the land. Her younger half-brother Keith rebels against the community's safety and runs away, eventually being tortured and killed by a gang of thieves. Her father disappears during a work trip and is presumed dead.
In 2027, when Lauren is eighteen, the community is attacked and destroyed by a massive group of raiders. Most residents are murdered, including Lauren's family. She escapes with only two neighbors - Harry Balter, a former teacher, and Zahra Moss, a young mother. Disguised as a man for safety, Lauren begins traveling north toward Oregon, where she hopes to find work and stability.
The journey is perilous. The world outside has regressed to near-feudal conditions, with each town or region operating as its own fiefdom with strict borders. Money still exists but is nearly worthless. Lauren and her companions face constant danger from desperate travelers, corporate security forces, and the elements.
Along the way, they rescue others from various dangers: a woman named Nita, a man named Touby, and others. Lauren also begins sharing her philosophy - Earthseed - which she has been developing since childhood. Earthseed's central tenet is that "God is Change" and that humanity's destiny is to leave Earth and populate other worlds. She believes this gives humans purpose and something to strive for beyond mere survival.
The group eventually meets Dr. Andrew Bankole, a physician who reminds Lauren of her father. He joins their journey and becomes a key member of the community. The novel ends with Lauren and her growing group settling in a community in northern California, preparing to establish a new society based on Earthseed principles.
entries.core:
Parable of the Sower is fundamentally about hope in the face of apocalypse. While many post-apocalyptic stories focus on survival, collapse, or the darker aspects of human nature, Butler crafts a narrative that insists humanity can choose to build something better. Lauren's Earthseed philosophy represents a forward-looking, adaptable worldview that embraces change rather than fearing it.
The novel explores climate change and environmental collapse with startling prescience, depicting droughts, fires, and resource scarcity decades before these became mainstream concerns. It also examines systemic inequality, showing how climate devastation disproportionately affects the poor and marginalized.
Butler's depiction of community formation is particularly compelling. Lauren doesn't just gather survivors - she creates a new social fabric based on mutual aid, shared purpose, and inclusive values. The novel suggests that the collapse of old institutions creates space for new, better ones to emerge.
The "hyper-empathy" ability serves as a metaphor for compassion and interconnectedness. Lauren's condition forces her to feel others' pain, but she transforms this into a source of strength - understanding others deeply allows her to lead, heal, and bind her community together.
At its core, Parable of the Sower argues that humanity's survival depends not on hoarding resources or building walls, but on reaching outward - literally to the stars, and metaphorically to one another.

entries.title: Permutation City
entries.creator: Greg Egan (Australian)
entries.pitch: In a mid-21st century Earth devastated by climate change and overdevelopment, humanity has developed technology powerful enough to create "Copies" - digital renderings of human consciousness that can live on in virtual reality after death. Paul Durham, a wealthy entrepreneur, pitches an ambitious project to aging billionaires: immortality through digital consciousness transfer. He hires programmer Maria Deluca to create a simulated universe (the Autoverse) that could support artificial life, while also building a vast virtual city for his wealthy clients. But Durham's true vision goes far beyond mere immortality - he believes that reality itself is mathematics, and that all possible universes exist simultaneously. The novel follows Durham, Maria, and several other characters as they explore the implications of digital consciousness, virtual reality, and a radical new theory of existence called "Dust Theory." What begins as a quest for immortality evolves into something far more profound - an exploration of what it means to be real, and whether consciousness can exist independent of physical reality.
entries.truth:
The novel is set in 2045-2050, a world where climate change has ravaged the planet and vast computing resources are traded on a global market called the QIPS Exchange. The story follows several interconnected characters:
**Paul Durham** is an insurance salesman turned visionary entrepreneur who believes he can achieve immortality by copying human consciousness into computers. He approaches wealthy clients with an offer to transfer their minds into a secure virtual environment that will never be shut down. He hires **Maria Deluca**, a talented programmer obsessed with the Autoverse - a cellular automaton-based artificial life simulator that mimics real chemistry. Maria needs money to help her terminally ill mother.
**Thomas Riemann** is a wealthy banker with a dark secret - he committed a murder years ago. He sees Copy technology as a way to escape his guilt, possibly by creating a version of himself with the traumatic memories removed.
**Peer and Kate** are two lovers who sneak into Durham's virtual city as it's being built, living in the unused processing margins of the simulation. They represent the underground of those who can't afford premium Copy services.
The first part of the novel focuses on Durham's experiments with Copies. He repeatedly creates copies of himself to test the technology, but each Copy immediately chooses to terminate itself upon experiencing virtual existence. Finally, he removes the termination option, forcing a Copy of himself to remain alive and collaborate with him.
Through these experiments, Durham develops "Dust Theory" - the radical idea that there is no fundamental difference between physics and mathematics, and that all mathematically possible structures exist. According to this theory, our universe is just one permutation among infinite others, and consciousness itself may be a pattern that exists independently of any particular substrate.
The second part jumps forward six thousand years (in subjective time within the TVC universe). The "TVC universe" - a computational construct created by Durham and Maria using a six-dimensional cellular automaton - has become humanity's new home. Maria's original Autoverse lifeform, **Autobacterium Lamberti**, has evolved over billions of years into the **Lambertians** - an intelligent insect-like species unaware they were created by humans.
Paul and Maria (now existing as Copies) must communicate with the Lambertians and convince them not to undermine the fundamental laws of their universe through their scientific investigations. The novel's climax involves this philosophical confrontation between creators and created, as the Lambertians begin to question their own origins.
The ending is ambiguous but hopeful - the possibility of coexistence between different forms of consciousness across multiple universes.
entries.core:
**The Nature of Reality and Consciousness:** At its heart, Permutation City asks whether consciousness is substrate-independent. If a perfect copy of your brain is created in software, is that copy "you"? Egan explores this through the Copy characters, who must grapple with questions of identity, continuity, and what makes experience real.
**Dust Theory and Mathematical Universe:** Egan presents a variant of the holographic principle and Max Tegmark's mathematical universe hypothesis. If reality is mathematics, then all mathematically possible structures exist - including virtual universes and their inhabitants. This has profound implications for questions of mortality, meaning, and existence.
**Economic Inequality in the Digital Age:** The novel shows how economic disparities translate into the digital realm. Wealthy Copies live in luxury VR environments running at full speed, while the poor exist in "Slums" - virtual ghettos where processing time is so slow that subjective centuries may pass while outside world experiences only days. This cyberpunk commentary predicts our current concerns about digital divides.
**The Creator-Creation Relationship:** The Lambertians' evolution raises questions about responsibility and unintended consequences. When we create intelligent life (whether digital or biological), what obligations do we have? What happens when our creations inevitably surpass or contradict their creators' assumptions?
**Time and Subjectivity:** Egan explores how time is experienced differently depending on computational resources. A Copy running slowly might experience decades while external reality sees only hours - raising questions about which experience is "more real."
**Hard Science Fiction as Philosophy:** Egan uses dense scientific concepts not as mere backdrop but as vehicles for genuine philosophical exploration. The novel functions as a thought experiment about the ultimate nature of existence, written with the rigor of someone who actually understands the mathematics and physics involved.
Permutation City is demanding but rewarding - it requires readers to think alongside the author about deep questions while providing a compelling narrative about characters facing the most profound transformations imaginable.

entries.title: Phase IV
entries.creator: Saul Bass (Director), based on a story by Ronald B. Harvey
entries.pitch: Phase IV is a rare gem from 1974—the only feature film ever directed by legendary title designer Saul Bass (better known for creating iconic opening sequences for Hitchcock's Psycho and Vertigo). Set in the sun-scorched Arizona desert, the film presents a terrifying premise: a species of ants has begun evolving at an exponential rate, developing intelligence, complex communication, and ultimately, a collective consciousness that poses an existential threat to humanity. Unlike typical monster movies, Phase IV takes a patient, almost meditative approach to horror, building dread through strange ant behaviors, unexplained geometric structures, and the growing realization that humans may be witnessing the birth of a new dominant species. The film is visually stunning, with groundbreaking macro photography of ant behavior and a haunting, minimalist electronic score that adds to the growing sense of unease. It's science fiction as slow-burn psychological horror—an overlooked masterpiece that deserves far more recognition than it has received.
entries.truth:
The film opens in the desert town of Buckshot, Arizona, where the population has been mysteriously dying. Two researchers, Dr. Leslie Hubbs (Michael Murphy), a rigid, mathematically-minded entomologist, and Dr. Edmund "Ed" Kackley (Brian Derek), a softer-spoken researcher more interested in animal behavior, arrive to investigate. They discover that worker ants have begun exhibiting strange new behaviors—digging massive geometric structures in the sand, displaying what appears to be abstract reasoning, and most disturbing of all, systematically destroying the local spider population, who seem to be their only natural predators.
As the scientists set up a research station in the desert, they begin documenting the ant colony's remarkable evolution. The ants have developed a new communication system and are building an enormous, perfectly circular structure in the desert—a "sacred" center that seems to serve as both temple and command center. The colony is being led by super-intelligent "queen" ants who are creating a new generation of soldier ants, larger and more aggressive than any previously known species.
The townspeople grow increasingly hostile toward the scientists, viewing the ant phenomenon as either divine punishment or a sign of the apocalypse. A young girl named "Kaye" (Debra D. Bason in her only film role) seems to have some mysterious connection to the ants—she wanders into the desert and returns unharmed, speaking in strange tongues. The local pastor, played by Leslie, becomes convinced the ants are demons sent to punish humanity for its sins.
Tensions escalate as Hubbs becomes more obsessed with understanding and ultimately destroying the ant colony. He proposes poisoning the entire desert to eradicate the threat, but Kackley grows more sympathetic to the ants, seeing their evolution as a natural, perhaps even beautiful process. Kackley begins to see the ants not as enemies but as a new form of life worthy of respect—an intelligence that has simply surpassed humanity's ability to comprehend it.
In the film's climax, the ants launch a coordinated attack on the human population. Using sophisticated chemical signals and unprecedented organization, they infiltrate homes, overwhelm humans with sheer numbers, and systematically eliminate resistance. The electronic infrastructure fails as the ants chew through wiring. The townspeople either flee or are killed, leaving only the two scientists at their research station.
Hubbs attempts to activate his poison dispersal system, but the ants have anticipated this. They have already spread throughout the region, and their colony has grown too vast to destroy. In the final moments, Kackley chooses to walk into the desert, apparently accepting that humanity's time is over. Hubbs is left alone as the ants close in around his station. The film's final image shows the desert covered in vast, intricate ant structures—a new civilization rising on the ruins of the old.
entries.core:
Phase IV is fundamentally about evolution, intelligence, and humanity's fragile sense of superiority. Bass presents a stark question: what happens when humanity encounters a form of life that evolves beyond our ability to understand or control? The ants represent an alien intelligence already among us—patient, organized, and ultimately indifferent to human concerns. Their geometric structures and collective behavior can be seen as a form of intelligence that operates on principles humans simply cannot grasp.
The film critiques human arrogance and our tendency to view ourselves as the pinnacle of creation. Hubbs embodies scientific hubris—his belief that he can understand, categorize, and ultimately defeat the ants through rational means. His mathematical approach fails because the ants operate on a completely different paradigm. Kackley represents a more humble perspective, one that acknowledges the limits of human understanding and finds a kind of beauty in the emergence of new life.
Bass also explores themes of communication and translation. The ants have developed a sophisticated chemical language that humans cannot decipher. We see them building, organizing, and seemingly worshipping—but their true purposes remain opaque. This uncertainty is where much of the film's dread originates: we don't know what they're thinking, what they want, or what they'll do next. In an age before AI, Bass was already exploring the concept of non-human intelligence and the terror of encountering something smarter than ourselves.
The religious undertones add another layer. The ants can be read as a force of nature, or as angels, or demons, or simply the next step in evolution. The pastor's religious mania and Kaye's mystical connection to the ants suggest that humanity is witnessing something beyond scientific comprehension—something that defies our categories entirely. The title "Phase IV" implies evolutionary stages, suggesting the ants represent the fourth phase of life's development on Earth, with humanity being merely a transitional phase.
Finally, the film can be read as an ecological parable. The ants are not invaders but native life reclaiming territory from an invasive species. From an ecological perspective, humans are the true interlopers in the desert ecosystem, and the ants' counterattack is simply the natural order reasserting itself. Bass seems to suggest that nature has a patience and persistence that humanity fundamentally underestimates.

entries.title: Phoenix 2772
entries.creator: Osamu Tezuka (Director/Writer), Taku Sugiyama (Co-Director)
entries.pitch: In a dystopian future where Earth is dying from energy depletion and humanity is controlled through test-tube births and computer-assigned roles, young pilot Godo is chosen by the ambitious politician Rock to capture the mythical Phoenix—a legendary bird whose blood can grant immortality and heal the dying planet. Guided by his loyal robot caretaker Olga and accompanied by a mysterious prisoner, Godo journeys into space to find the Phoenix, all while navigating forbidden love, political betrayal, and his own doubts about violence and sacrifice. This 1980 animated film from the legendary Osamu Tezuka is a visually stunning meditation on love, reincarnation, and selflessness that blends epic science fiction with mythic storytelling.
entries.truth:
The story unfolds in year 2772, where Earth has become a dying world depleted of energy resources. Humanity is now produced in test tubes, with computers determining each person's role in society—from pilots to politicians. The planet is ruled by a totalitarian system, and the dictatorial politician Rock schemes to become prime minister by any means necessary.
Godo is one of many children raised by computers to become a cadet pilot. His sole companion is Olga, a beautiful robot who has served as his caretaker since infancy. Godo demonstrates exceptional piloting abilities, which attracts Rock's attention. Rock selects Godo for a dangerous mission: travel into deep space to capture the mystical Phoenix, whose blood can heal the dying Earth and grant immortality to whoever drinks it. Rock's true motive is selfish—he wants to become both prime minister and immortal.
The assignment torments Godo for several reasons. First, he loves all living creatures and despises being trained as a ruthless space hunter. Second, he must leave behind Olga, who will be destroyed after his departure. Most crucially, Godo has fallen in love with Rena, the daughter of Lord Eat—an "elite" class citizen—and Rock's intended bride. Their relationship is forbidden for someone of Godo's rank.
When Godo and Rena are discovered together, Godo loses his citizenship and is sent to a forced labor camp in Iceland, where energy from the Earth's mantle is being harvested—a process causing dangerous geological instability. Heartbroken over losing Rena, Godo meets Dr. Saruta, a prison professor who claims to want to tutor him but secretly plots to escape and search for the Phoenix themselves to save the Earth.
A catastrophic earthquake destroys the facility. Godo is rescued by Olga and Pincho, Rena's alien pet creature. They steal a "Space Shark" ship and launch into space. After stopping at a planet to meet Saruta's hermit friend Ban, the crew tracks down the Phoenix—but finds it impossibly powerful, transforming into dragons, tentacled leeches, and even mimicking a small planet.
After learning that Rena has married Rock, Godo becomes overwhelmed with grief and pushes away Olga's romantic advances. The Phoenix kills the crew one by one, and Dr. Saruta dies before revealing the bird's weakness. Finally, the Phoenix destroys Olga by burning her alive. Cradling Olga's blackened metal body, Godo experiences a epiphany about his selfishness toward his devoted companion.
The Phoenix attempts to attack their ship but is repelled by the power of Godo's love. The bird transforms into a feminine, peacock-like form and speaks to Godo telepathically, impressed by his love for living creatures. She offers to revive Olga if Godo gives her what she wants—his love itself. The Phoenix inhabits Olga's restored body.
Godo and Olga are given a paradisaical planet to live on, but Godo still feels drawn to save the dying Earth. He returns with resources, only to be arrested by Rock. Catastrophic earthquakes then destroy the world—Rena dies trying to escape on Godo's ship, and Rock is blinded by lava. Godo gives Rock his last rites.
In the aftermath, the Phoenix reveals herself to console Godo. She offers him immortality if he drinks her blood and waits centuries for Earth's revival. Instead, Godo offers his own life for Earth's revival. The Phoenix accepts, admiring his selflessness. After farewells, Godo collapses and dies in Olga's arms.
The Phoenix then leaves Olga's body. The two lifeless forms transform—Godo becomes a newborn baby, and Olga becomes a beautiful human woman who takes the baby as her son, beginning their cycle anew.
entries.core:
Phoenix 2772 explores several interlocking themes that run through Tezuka's entire Phoenix manga series. The central theme is reincarnation and the cyclical nature of existence—Godo and Olga's story represents an eternal return, with their love transcending death itself. The film presents love as a force powerful enough to transform both the lover and the beloved, with the Phoenix itself being moved by Godo's compassion for all living things.
The film also examines the conflict between selfishness and selflessness. Rock represents the ultimate selfish character—he wants immortality and power for himself. Godo initially seems selfish in his grief over Rena, but ultimately discovers the capacity for true selfless love. The Phoenix, who herself wanted to consume Godo's love, ultimately respects his choice to sacrifice himself for others.
The dystopian setting serves as a critique of totalitarian control and the dehumanization of society. Humanity has traded freedom for security, with computers assigning roles and test-tube births eliminating natural family bonds. Yet even in this sterile world, authentic human emotions—love, loyalty, sacrifice—prove more powerful than any system designed to suppress them.
Finally, the film addresses environmental concerns through Earth's depletion and the destructive energy extraction in Iceland, suggesting that technological "progress" without spiritual growth leads to annihilation. Only through genuine love and self-sacrifice can humanity and the world be reborn.

entries.title: Pretty Women Walking Through the City
entries.creator: Želimir Žilnik (Director)
entries.pitch:
Pretty Women Walking Through the City (original title: Lijepe žene prolaze kroz grad) is a 1986 Yugoslav post-apocalyptic science fiction film directed by Želimir Žilnik, one of the most prominent figures of the Yugoslav Black Wave cinema movement. Set in Belgrade in the year 2041, the film presents a haunting vision of a future Yugoslavia that has collapsed into environmental decay and social fragmentation. The story follows a group of elderly men attempting to raise young girls in the traditional values of their ancestors, while the world around them has deteriorated into a desolate wasteland.
What makes this film remarkable is its prophetic nature—Žilnik made this film in 1986, just years before the actual breakup of Yugoslavia, and the film uncannily predicted the nationalist tensions and social disintegration that would tear the nation apart in the early 1990s. The film was produced as part of a wave of Yugoslav cinema exploring socio-political criticisms, and it stands as a chilling artifact that anticipated one of the most violent dissolutions of a modern state.
entries.truth:
The film is set in Belgrade in the year 2041, presented as a deserted urban landscape that has been transformed into something resembling a dump yard. The once-vibrant city has fallen into severe decay, with the environment polluted and society having collapsed from its former modernist glory.
The narrative centers on a small community of elderly men who have taken it upon themselves to raise a group of young girls. These men cling desperately to the traditional values and cultural heritage of their Yugoslav ancestors, attempting to preserve a sense of identity and continuity in a world that has otherwise collapsed. They live in the ruins of the old city, trying to maintain a semblance of the old ways while the world around them has moved on or been destroyed.
The film explores the tension between preservation and decay, as the old men try to teach the girls about a Yugoslavia that no longer exists—all that remains are fragments of memory, cultural practices, and perhaps an idealized past. The girls represent the future, but what kind of future can exist in a world of environmental destruction and social fragmentation?
The setting of Belgrade in 2041 serves as a character in itself—a city that has experienced some catastrophic event or gradual collapse that has left it in ruins. The urban landscape is shown as a place of emptiness, where the grand modernist ambitions of Yugoslav socialism have given way to decay and abandonment.
The film does not provide easy answers or clear explanations for how the world reached this state. Instead, it presents a meditative portrait of survival and cultural memory, focusing on the human impulse to preserve identity and tradition even in the face of total collapse.
entries.core:
**Prophetic Vision of Yugoslav Dissolution:** Perhaps the most significant aspect of Pretty Women Walking Through the City is its remarkable prophetic quality. Made in 1986, Žilnik's film predicted with unsettling accuracy the nationalist tensions and ethnic conflicts that would lead to the breakup of Yugoslavia in the early 1990s. The film suggests that the seeds of destruction were already present in the social fabric of Yugoslavia—regional rivalries, ethnic tensions, and the failure of socialist modernism to create a unified national identity. The post-apocalyptic setting can be read as an allegory for the violence and fragmentation that was to come, making the film a chilling historical document.
**Environmental Collapse and Modernist Failure:** The film presents a world where environmental degradation has played a significant role in societal collapse. Belgrade in 2041 is shown as a polluted, ruined city—a far cry from the modernist dreams of socialist Yugoslavia. This environmental decay serves as a critique of the modernist project itself, suggesting that the industrial development and modernization pursued by Yugoslav socialism led not to progress but to ecological catastrophe. The wasteland setting comments on the unsustainable path of industrial modernity.
**Cultural Preservation and Memory:** The elderly men's attempt to raise the girls in traditional Yugoslav values represents a desperate effort to preserve cultural memory in a world that has lost its bearings. The film explores what it means to cling to tradition and identity when the world around you has collapsed. This theme resonates with broader questions about how nations and cultures survive catastrophic change—whether through adaptation or rigid adherence to the past.
**Gender and Generational Dynamics:** The film centers on the relationship between elderly men and young girls, presenting a reversed caretaking dynamic. The old men are attempting to transmit cultural knowledge to a younger generation, but this transmission is complicated by the collapse of the world they remember. The girls represent both the hope for continuity and the uncertainty of what kind of world they will build.
**The Ruins of Socialist Modernism:** Žilnik, as a filmmaker who was famously critical of the Yugoslav communist regime, uses the post-apocalyptic setting to comment on the failures of socialist modernism. The ruins of Belgrade symbolize not just physical decay but the bankruptcy of a political and economic system. The film's dystopian vision suggests that the contradictions inherent in the Yugoslav system—between nationalism and brotherhood, between central planning and regional autonomy—would ultimately lead to collapse.
Pretty Women Walking Through the City remains a haunting and prescient work—a science fiction film that turned out to be a documentary of things to come. Žilnik's ability to foresee the dissolution of Yugoslavia through this apocalyptic lens makes the film essential viewing for understanding both Yugoslav history and the genre of dystopian cinema.

entries.title: Pumzi
entries.creator: Wanuri Kahiu
entries.pitch: Pumzi is a groundbreaking Kenyan science-fiction short film that represents a landmark in Africanfuturism. Directed by Wanuri Kahiu and funded through the Focus Features Africa First short film program, the 21-minute film premiered at the 2010 Sundance Film Festival as part of its New African Cinema program. The title "Pumzi" is Swahili for "Breath," and the film presents a haunting vision of post-apocalyptic East Africa set 35 years after World War III—referred to as The Water War. The film explores themes of environmentalism, technological control, and human resilience through the story of Asha, a curator at the Virtual Natural History Museum who receives a mysterious package containing soil that may indicate life still exists outside her controlled community.
entries.truth:
The film is set in the Maitu community of the East African Territory, 35 years after The Water War (World War III). This community exists as a sealed, self-sufficient environment where citizens are confined within walls due to harsh external conditions—specifically, radiation concerns and water scarcity. The word "Maitu" comes from Kikuyu roots meaning "truth" and "our," signifying "our truth" or "mother."
Asha works as the curator of the Virtual Natural History Museum, tending to digital exhibits of a world that no longer exists. The community operates on strict conservation principles: citizens generate electricity through manual machines like treadmills and rowing machines, and water is severely rationed. Urine and sweat are recycled and stored in personal water bottles for purification and reuse.
One day, Asha receives an anonymous package containing a small soil sample. Using both technological tests and her own sensory intuition, she discovers the soil shows no radiation and contains high moisture levels. When she inhales the smell of the soil, she experiences a vivid vision of a green tree growing from a pool of water—a stark contrast to the barren wasteland she's been told defines the outside world.
Asha requests to meet with the Maitu Council, three women who govern the community and hold authority over citizens' movements. She presents her findings and her dream-vision of the green tree. The Council dismisses her claims as delusion, noting that dreaming has been suppressed through wake-up alarms and mandatory medication. They deny her visa to leave, declare her sick, and order security to destroy all evidence of her discovery.
With help from a bathroom attendant, Asha escapes the compound into the external world for the first time. The landscape is harsh, filled with garbage—mostly plastic—ejected from the city. Using refuse to create foot coverings and a headscarf against the sun and wind, she follows a compass to where the soil sample originated.
She finds only emptiness and despair—no living vegetation, only her dream as a mirage. In a final act of hope, Asha digs a hole and plants the Mother Tree seed. She pours her last remaining water and wrings out the sweat from her body onto the small plant, then lies down to protect and nurture the bud. As the camera pulls up and widens, a tree rapidly grows—seemingly from Asha's own body—suggesting a cycle of renewal and sacrifice.
entries.core:
**Africanfuturism and Representation:** Pumzi is a foundational work of Africanfuturism, depicting a future civilization located on the African continent predominantly populated by Black people. The film challenges the typical absence of Black voices in science fiction and offers an alternative vision of Afrofuturist storytelling that is rooted in African landscapes, cultures, and concerns rather than Western technological fantasies.
**Environmentalism and Resource Scarcity:** The film presents a world defined by water scarcity and environmental collapse—the aftermath of The Water War. The community's strict conservation measures (recycling water, generating manual energy) reflect real-world anxieties about resource depletion. Director Wanuri Kahiu has stated the film was partly inspired by her annoyance with the cost of bottled water, connecting the narrative to contemporary environmental concerns.
**Technology and Control:** While the Maitu community uses advanced technology for recycling and sustainability, this technology serves as a tool of social control. Citizens are monitored, their movements restricted, and their dreams suppressed through medication. The Council controls everything—from water rations to the right to leave—using technological infrastructure. The film critiques how environmental solutions can become mechanisms of oppression.
**Sensory Experience and Human Intuition:** Asha's journey emphasizes the importance of sensory experience and intuition alongside technological analysis. Her ability to smell the soil and trust her dreams contrasts with the community's reliance on data and rationalism. The film suggests that human connection to the earth and to dreams is essential for survival and renewal.
**Hope and Sacrifice:** Despite the film's melancholic tone, it ends on a note of hope. Asha's sacrifice—giving her last water and ultimately her life force—results in the growth of the Mother Tree. This suggests that renewal requires personal sacrifice and that hope persists even in the most desolate circumstances.

entries.title: Quintet
entries.creator: Robert Altman
entries.pitch: In the frozen aftermath of a second ice age, seal hunter Essex wanders the blank tundra with his pregnant companion Vivia, searching for his brother. When tragedy strikes and Vivia is killed in an apartment bombing, Essex finds himself drawn into the dark heart of a dying world: the Hotel Electra, a gambling resort where the ultra-wealthy play "Quintet"—a deadly board game where virtual kills translate to real executions. As Essex assumes a dead man's identity and enters this tournament of survival, he discovers that the game is more than entertainment—it's a metaphor for humanity's final descent into nihilism. Director Robert Altman crafts a haunting, ambiguous meditation on civilization's end through non-linear storytelling and a oppressive, freezing atmosphere.
entries.truth:
The film opens during a brutal second ice age, where seal hunter Essex and his pregnant companion Vivia travel across the frozen tundra seeking his brother Francha. They find Francha's apartment, but while Essex is out buying firewood, a gambler named Redstone bombs the apartment, killing everyone inside including Vivia.
Essex pursues Redstone to the Information Room and witnesses his murder by another gambler, St. Christopher. Searching Redstone's body, Essex finds a list of names. He discovers Redstone had checked into the Hotel Electra, a luxury gambling resort in another sector. Assuming Redstone's identity, Essex enters the hotel and is drawn into the "Quintet" tournament—a mysterious board game played by the elite.
Essex meets Ambrosia, who always plays as the "sixth player" in each game. He slowly learns that Quintet is a fight for survival: players who are "killed" in the game are executed in reality. The game progresses, with players Goldstar and Deuca being killed one by one.
When only St. Christopher and Ambrosia remain, Ambrosia insists that Essex (as Redstone's successor) be counted as a player. St. Christopher must eliminate Essex before facing her. In a confrontation outside the city, St. Christopher falls into an ice rupture and dies.
Essex returns to find Ambrosia has followed him. They attack each other simultaneously—Essex slashes her throat moments before she can kill him. He then goes to cremate her body at the Hotel Electra and demands his "prize" from Grigor, the dealer. Grigor reveals there is no prize—the thrill of the game itself is the only reward. Disgusted, Essex leaves the hotel and the world of Quintet forever.
The film ends ambiguously: does Essex escape to find his brother, or is this just another vision in the bleak, frozen wasteland?
entries.core:
Quintet is Altman at his most deliberately obscure and challenging—a film that refuses easy interpretation. The central metaphor is the game itself: in a post-apocalyptic world where resources are scarce and hope is obsolete, the wealthy have turned survival into entertainment. The game literalizes the violence that underpins civilization, asking what happens when humanity's worst impulses are given合法 expression.
The film explores the erosion of meaning in a world that has ended. The characters speak in philosophical riddles and detached observations—Vittorio Gassman's St. Christopher declares "Hope is an obsolete word." This isn't character development so much as commentary on a civilization that has moved beyond traditional human concerns.
Altman's signature overlapping dialogue and fragmented narrative structure mirrors the game's complexity and the world's incoherence. The freezing visuals—shot at Montreal's abandoned Expo 67 site—create an atmosphere of decay and stagnation, not the usual post-apocalyptic hope for renewal.
Critically reviled on release (Roger Ebert called it "a puzzlement"), Quintet has slowly gained appreciation as a cult curiosity—a bizarre, cold, and challenging work that asks whether civilization deserves to survive at all.

entries.title: Roadside Picnic
entries.creator: Arkady and Boris Strugatsky
entries.pitch: In the wake of an enigmatic alien visitation that left six mysterious "Zones" scattered across Earth, humanity struggles to comprehend what was left behind. Roadside Picnic follows Redrick Schuhart—a downtrodden worker who sneaks into the forbidden Zone to retrieve alien artifacts for profit—through a day in his life in the decaying industrial town of Harmont. The novel is less about the aliens themselves than about how humanity copes with the knowledge that we are not alone in the universe—and that the visitors cared nothing for us. Raw, gritty, and profoundly philosophical, this is Soviet SF at its most haunting: a story about the emptiness of modern life, the corrupting nature of hope, and the impossible question of whether humanity can ever truly understand what lies beyond our world.
entries.truth:
The novel is set in the fictional industrial city of Harmont, one of several places on Earth that experienced a brief, incomprehensible alien visitation twelve years before the story begins. The aliens—referred to as "Visitors" despite leaving no direct contact with humanity—spent only a short time on Earth before moving on, but they left behind areas of profound strangeness called "Zones." These Zones contain impossible phenomena, alien artifacts of unknown purpose, and dangers that kill or transform those who enter them. The world has largely sealed off these areas, but a black market exists for "merchandise"—objects retrieved from the Zone that fetch high prices from collectors and scientists.
Redrick "Red" Schuhart works as a maintenance worker at a local factory but earns extra money as an illegal "stalker"—someone who sneaks into the Zone to retrieve alien artifacts. The novel follows a single day in Red's life, intercut with flashbacks and memories that reveal his history and the toll his activities have taken.
We see Red's world through his eyes: his cramped apartment, his strained relationship with his mother, his interactions with other stalkers and black market dealers. His childhood friend Goblin (a nickname) has been driven insane by Zone exposure and now lives in a psychiatric ward. Another friend, Kirill, was killed in the Zone. These losses haunt Red but don't stop him from continuing his dangerous work.
The core of the novel examines the psychological and existential weight of the alien visitation. Scientists and government officials endlessly study the Zone but cannot understand it. The artifacts have no discernible purpose—strange devices that do nothing, objects that look like common items but aren't. Humanity is left with the unbearable knowledge that something vast and incomprehensible visited them, found them uninteresting, and left.
The novel ends with Red returning to the Zone one last time, seeking something more than mere profit—a meaning, perhaps, or a connection to whatever the Visitors represented. What he finds there is ambiguous, mystical, and open to interpretation.
entries.core:
Roadside Picnic is fundamentally about humanity's cosmic insignificance and the psychological burden of that knowledge. The Visitors never contacted humanity, never explained their presence, and seemed utterly indifferent to humans as beings worthy of attention. They left behind only junk—the equivalent of a roadside picnic's trash—and departed. The novel asks: How do humans cope with the possibility that we are alone in the universe, or worse, that we are noticed but deemed unimportant?
The Zone itself functions as a liminal space—a boundary between the known and unknowable. It corrupts those who enter it: Red's friend Goblin was transformed by exposure, driven mad by contact with something his mind couldn't process. This suggests that some truths may be too vast for human comprehension, that there are experiences that break rather than elevate us.
The Strugatsky brothers also explore the corrupting nature of hope. The existence of the Zone has spawned cults, religions, and endless speculation about what the Visitors wanted and what secrets the Zone holds. People delude themselves with interpretations and predictions that never come true. Red himself is caught between cynicism—he knows the Zone is just junk—and a desperate hope that there might be something more.
The novel is also a critique of Soviet society, written by authors who lived under Soviet censorship. The Zone can be read as a metaphor for everything that cannot be spoken of or understood within a closed system. The futility of official attempts to study and contain the Zone mirrors the impossibility of total ideological control. Red's petty smuggling and survival in a decaying society reflects the existential compromises people made under Soviet rule.
Perhaps most importantly, Roadside Picnic is about the stories we tell ourselves. The novel ends without answers because answers are impossible. The "meaning" of the Zone remains as elusive as the meaning of existence itself. What we have are the stories we construct—the narratives we use to make an incomprehensible universe bearable.

entries.title: Robot Carnival
entries.creator: Multiple Directors (Katsuhiro Otomo, Koji Morimoto, Hidetoshi Ōmori, Yasuomi Umetsu, Takashi Nakamura, Hiroyuki Kitakubo, Hiroyuki Kitazume, Mao Lamdo)
entries.pitch: Robot Carnival is a groundbreaking 1987 Japanese anthology OVA featuring nine distinct short films by different animators, each exploring robots and humanity's relationship with machines. From the surreal to the comedic to the deeply melancholic, this collection showcases emerging talent who would later become legends in the anime industry—including Katsuhiro Otomo (director of Akira). The film ranges from a mad scientist accidentally killing himself while teaching his creation to dance, to a post-apocalyptic android's journey to rescue its human companion, to a surreal tale of a traveling carnival of robots destroying a desert village. With music by the legendary Joe Hisaishi, Robot Carnival is a time capsule of late-80s Japanese animation creativity—a must-see for anyone interested in the evolution of anime as an art form.
entries.truth:
**Opening/Ending:** A boy discovers a "coming soon" poster for the Robot Carnival in a desert wasteland. A gigantic rusted machine containing countless performing robots grinds through his village, destroying it. At the end, the Carnival gets stuck on a dune, breaks apart, and years later a family discovers a music box among the ruins—its dancing robot explodes, leaving only "END" in letters.
**Franken's Gears:** A mad scientist attempts to bring his robot to life using lightning, just like Frankenstein. When the robot is successfully animated, it mimics the scientist's movements. Overjoyed, the scientist dances gleefully, trips, falls, and dies. The robot, copying its creator, trips and falls on him, killing them both.
**Deprive:** An alien invasion of robot foot soldiers attacks a city, kidnapping people including a young girl. Her damaged android companion retains her locket and is later upgraded into a powerful combat android with a human disguise.
