The android defeats the alien forces and rescues the girl, who recognizes him by the locket he still carries.
**Presence:** A man secretly builds an automaton in his shed to compensate for emotional isolation from his wife and family. When the automaton develops its own personality, he smashes her in panic. Years later, he has visions of her, and eventually returns to take her hand and walk into the distance, vanishing before his shocked wife.
**Star Light Angel:** Two teenage friends visit a robot-themed amusement park. When one girl's boyfriend dumps her for her friend, she runs away and enters a virtual reality ride. Her dark emotions summon a giant laser-breathing mecha, but a park employee in a robot costume helps her confront her feelings and move forward.
**Cloud:** A robot walks through time, witnessing human evolution and eventual self-destruction. Done in a scratchboard/etching style, the robot experiences the rise and fall of civilization before being granted humanity by an angel who mourns human mortality.
**Strange Tales of Meiji Machine Culture: Westerner's Invasion:** Set in 19th century Japan, two giant wooden robots—one operated by Westerners attempting to colonize, one by Japanese locals—battle in the style of vintage movie serials. A comedic take on cultural conflict and technological modernization.
**Chicken Man and Red Neck:** In Tokyo, a robotic magician named Red Neck brings all machines to life for one night of revelry. Only a drunken "Chicken Man" witnesses the chaos. When dawn breaks, the robots are embedded high in skyscrapers while ordinary citizens continue their lives below, oblivious.
entries.core:
Robot Carnival is fundamentally about humanity's complex, often contradictory relationship with technology. Each segment explores different aspects of this relationship: fear and fascination, creation and destruction, isolation and connection.
The anthology format allows multiple perspectives on the same theme. Some segments view robots as threats (Deprive, Chicken Man and Red Neck), others as companions (Star Light Angel), and still others as metaphors for human loneliness and the desire for connection (Presence). The recurring motif of robots as both entertainers and destroyers reflects anxieties about technology's dual nature.
Notably, many segments feature creators destroyed by their own creations (Franken's Gears), reflecting the Frankenstein complex—the fear that creating artificial life leads to the creator's downfall. Yet others show humans and robots forming genuine bonds (Deprive, Presence), suggesting hope for coexistence.
The film also showcases the diversity of anime as an art form. Each segment has a completely different visual style, from traditional anime to experimental scratchboard animation to retro serial styles. This variety was intentional—the directors were given near-complete creative freedom, and many were animators with no prior directing experience. The result is a unique document of late-80s Japanese animation creativity, a snapshot of artists who would go on to shape the industry.

entries.title: Rogue Moon
entries.creator: Algis Budrys
entries.pitch: In 1960, legendary science fiction writer Algis Budrys delivered this haunting meditation on humanity's relationship with death and identity. When a massive alien artifact is discovered on the Moon, the U.S. Navy recruits Dr. Edward Hawks to lead a top-secret project investigating the mysterious structure—which kills anyone who enters it. Hawks develops a revolutionary matter transmission system that allows explorers on Earth to share the experiences of their duplicates on the Moon, experiencing death second-hand as their clones navigate the alien labyrinth. The story follows Al Barker, a thrill-seeker who becomes the first to retain his sanity after dying in the artifact, as he pushes further into the structure with each iteration. Part hard science fiction puzzle, part existential meditation, Rogue Moon explores what it means to face death, create identity, and transcend the boundaries of human existence.
entries.truth:
Dr. Edward Hawks runs a classified Navy project investigating a colossal alien artifact discovered on the lunar surface. The structure is a labyrinth filled with death traps—any explorer who deviates from an exact path is instantly killed. To study it, Hawks has developed matter transmission technology that scans a person and creates a perfect duplicate on the Moon. The original remains on Earth in sensory deprivation, sharing the experiences of their clone.
Each successive explorer gets slightly further into the labyrinth, learning more about the alien structure's rules through death. However, everyone who has attempted the journey has been driven insane by experiencing their own demise second-hand. The project is failing until Vincent "Connie" Connington, Continental's head of personnel, introduces Hawks to Al Barker—an extreme adventurer and thrill-seeker who seems uniquely suited to the task.
Also present is Claire Pack, Barker's girlfriend, a manipulative sociopath who uses sexuality as a weapon. While Connington desires her power and influence, Claire remains with Barker because he has no weaknesses to exploit. Hawks must appeal to Barker's dark side to recruit him for the project.
Barker proves extraordinary—he is the first to survive the experience of dying in the artifact while retaining his sanity. However, he is deeply affected, realizing the alien intelligence behind the structure didn't care about him at all—he was "nothing to it." He returns again and again, advancing slightly farther each time. His relationship with Claire deteriorates as he becomes increasingly consumed by the challenge. Eventually, Connington quits the project in frustration, and Claire leaves with him.
Meanwhile, Hawks develops a relationship with Elizabeth Cummings, a young artist, and confides his moral torment about the project to her. When Barker finally announces he is almost through the labyrinth, Hawks takes Elizabeth to a romantic location, declares his love, and then transmits himself to the Moon to join Barker for the final run.
The two duplicates navigate the bizarre, deadly landscapes together and emerge successfully on the other side of the artifact. However, Hawks realizes they cannot return to Earth—the transmission equipment is too crude to safely send a person back, and duplicates of them already exist on Earth living their lives. The Moon is now their home.
Back on Earth, the original Hawks removes his isolation suit and finds a note in his hand—knowing it would be there—reading simply: "Remember me to her."
entries.core:
Rogue Moon stands as one of science fiction's most profound explorations of death, identity, and transcendence. The alien labyrinth functions as a powerful metaphor for existence itself—a path that must be navigated correctly, where any deviation brings swift annihilation. The fact that explorers must die repeatedly to learn the correct path speaks to Budrys's theme that human growth and understanding require confronting mortality.
The matter transmission technology raises questions about identity and consciousness that remain relevant today. When a perfect duplicate is created, which one is "real"? The original or the copy? Budrys suggests that identity may be more about continuity of experience than physical substance—each death and rebirth through the transmission process creates a new being with memories of its predecessor.
Critic John Clute described the novel as a "rite de passage" and "a death-paean," noting its dual narrative levels: the surface hard science fiction puzzle of navigating the alien structure, and the deeper existential meditation on the mind-body split and what it means to be human. Jeff King wrote that the labyrinth represents life's journey, and Barker's successful passage through it represents self-creation—the idea that we must define ourselves through our choices and actions.
The novel also explores humanity's relationship with death. Barker is drawn to the project partly because he loves death, while Hawks is tormented by it. The alien artifact seems to exist as a kind of test or evolutionary crucible—humans must learn to navigate it, suggesting that transcendence requires confronting and understanding mortality rather than avoiding it.
Carl Sagan praised Rogue Moon as one of the rare science fiction novels that combines "a standard science-fiction theme with a deep human sensitivity." Despite being a relatively short work, it packs enormous philosophical weight, examining not just the technical challenges of space exploration but the psychological and spiritual dimensions of the human condition.

entries.title: Roy del espacio
entries.creator: Héctor López Carmona, Rafael Ángel Gil, Ulises Pérez Aguirre (Directors)
entries.pitch:
Roy del espacio (English: Roy of Space) is a 1983 Mexican animated science fiction film and one of the first feature-length Mexican animated films ever made. Produced from 1979 to 1982, the film follows the titular character Roy, who together with Dr. Faz and psychologist Elena, travels to a distant planet and faces an evil emperor who wants to conquer the Earth. The story includes robot fights, space battles, and a bit of romance, following a classic adventure structure reminiscent of 1930s serials like Flash Gordon.
The film premiered on March 3, 1983 in Mexico City theaters but was quickly withdrawn due to poor public acceptance. Retrospective critics have classified it as an example of artistic ineptitude, and it was considered a lost film until November 2025 when restoration work was announced. Despite its reception, it represents an important milestone in Mexican animation history as one of the first feature-length animated sci-fi films from Mexico.
entries.truth:
The story follows Roy, a young hero who, along with Dr. Faz and psychologist Elena, embarks on a space mission to a distant planet. Their goal is to stop the evil Emperor Alome, who harbors ambitions of conquering Earth. Upon arriving on Mars, Roy and his companions are immediately captured by Alome's robotic soldiers.
However, they receive unexpected assistance from Alome's own daughter, the Princess, who helps them escape from captivity. With this new alliance, Roy manages to seize control of a spaceship and teams up with Prince Leo, another rebel against Alome's rule. Together, they launch an assault against the evil emperor's forces, engaging in robot fights and space battles across the alien world.
The adventure culminates in Roy and Prince Leo successfully defeating Alome and his empire, thwarting his plans to conquer Earth. The story follows a classic hero's journey structure, with Roy rising from captive to hero through courage and alliance-building.
entries.core:
Roy del espacio stands as a significant piece of Mexican cinematic history as one of the first feature-length animated science fiction films produced in Mexico. The film demonstrates how science fiction as a global genre reached Mexican filmmakers in the early 1980s, even when resources and technical capabilities were limited.
While critics have noted the film's derivative nature—borrowing heavily from classic sci-fi serials and even tracing frames from the 1930s Flash Gordon serial—the film represents an ambitious attempt to bring animated sci-fi to Mexican audiences. Its clumsy execution and heavy reliance on clichés do not diminish its historical importance as a pioneering work in Mexican animation.
The film's rediscovery and planned restoration in 2025 highlight its value as a cultural artifact, providing insight into the early development of Mexican animation and how international science fiction tropes were adapted for local audiences during a period of limited animation resources in Mexican cinema.

entries.title: Royal Space Force: The Wings of Honnêamise
entries.creator: Hiroyuki Yamaga (Director), Gainax
entries.pitch: In an alternate world bearing an uncanny resemblance to Earth, a disengaged young man named Shirotsugh Lhadatt has given up on his dreams of becoming a fighter pilot and instead drifts through his life in the Royal Space Force—a fledgling space program demoralized by repeated failures. Everything changes when he meets Riquinni, a devout young woman who preaches on the streets about humanity's fallen nature and the stolen fire of knowledge. Inspired by her quiet conviction, Shirotsugh volunteers for humanity's first orbital mission, becoming the pilot of a desperate last-ditch attempt to launch a person into space. Set against a backdrop of government corruption, war, and public indifference, this is a deeply human story about finding meaning beyond oneself.
entries.truth:
The film unfolds in the Kingdom of Honnêamise, an alternate Earth where technology has developed along different lines—roughly 1950s level but with distinct cultural characteristics. Shirotsugh is a young man who has already failed at his dreams, settling for a position in the Royal Space Force that seems destined for nothing. His life changes when he encounters Riquinni, a religious woman who believes humanity is cursed for having stolen fire—the knowledge of good and evil. She lives with a sullen orphan named Manna and spends her days preaching to uninterested crowds.
Riquinni's genuine faith and concern for the marginalized moves Shirotsugh in ways he cannot articulate. He begins visiting her, and she gifts him a holy book that speaks of humanity's eternal curse. Meanwhile, the Royal Space Force, led by General Khaidenn, attempts to rally public support by falsely claiming they are building a "space warship" rather than a scientific mission. The project faces numerous obstacles: an engineer dies in an accident, the public questions the expense while the nation suffers poverty, and a neighboring Republic threatens war.
Shirotsugh experiences a crisis of faith and goes AWOL, staying with Riquinni. During this time, he almost assaults her while she is undressing, but stops himself—an incident she later refuses to acknowledge. When Shirotsugh returns to the city, he survives an assassination attempt by the Republic. Before departing for the launch, he finally connects with Manna and says goodbye to Riquinni.
The launch occurs in a demilitarized zone, with the government hoping the rocket will provoke the Republic into attacking. Though Khaidenn pushes the launch forward, the Republic invades. Shirotsugh, already in the capsule, refuses to abort and convinces the ground crew to proceed. The spectacular launch shocks both nations into stillness.
As Shirotsugh orbits the planet, he broadcasts a prayer for humanity's forgiveness. His capsule enters sunlight, and a montage shows his life, his world's history, and achievements. On the surface, Riquinni witnesses the first snow fall and gazes upward.
entries.core:
Royal Space Force represents a radical departure from conventional anime storytelling. Gainax's debut film deliberately rejects typical anime motifs in favor of meticulous worldbuilding and character study. The film's core themes explore the nature of meaning and purpose—whether individual action matters in a world corrupted by power, poverty, and war. Shirotsugh's journey is fundamentally about transcending his cynicism and finding the will to attempt something greater than himself, even when the odds seem impossible and the world offers no encouragement.
The film also examines faith and reason. Riquinni's religious worldview presents a challenge: if humanity is fundamentally cursed, what meaning can individual action possess? The answer the film suggests lies not in religious certainty but in the act of trying itself—the launch becomes an existential statement that humanity can transcend its limitations, regardless of whether it "deserves" to.
Visually, the film presents a distinctive alternate Earth with 1950s-level technology but unique cultural developments. The design avoids typical anime aesthetics, creating something that feels both foreign and intimately familiar. Ryuichi Sakamoto's score complements this approach, adding gravitas without melodrama.
Critically, the film failed commercially but achieved cult status. Science fiction writer Ted Chiang has cited it as the finest example of worldbuilding in fiction. Its influence on subsequent anime, particularly through the Gainax founders who would later create Neon Genesis Evangelion, remains substantial.

entries.title: Sailboat
entries.creator: R.A. Lafferty
entries.pitch: Sailboat is a whimsical, deeply optimistic tale from the golden age of science fiction that reads like a fever dream of pure joy. The story follows Jonathan Teague, an ordinary man who one day finds an extraordinary sailboat washed up on the shore of Lake Michigan—a boat that can sail not just through water, but through time and space itself. What unfolds is a picaresque adventure across centuries and worlds, as Teague discovers that the universe is far stranger and more magnificent than he ever imagined. Unlike the dark, cautionary tales that dominate SF, Sailboat is suffused with wonder, humor, and a profound sense that magic is real and waiting to be found. It's the kind of story that makes you remember why you first fell in love with science fiction.
entries.truth:
Jonathan Teague is an ordinary man leading an ordinary life in the Midwest—until the day a strange wooden sailboat washes up on the shore of Lake Michigan near his home. The boat is ancient-looking, made of wood and canvas, with no apparent means of propulsion beyond its single sail. Yet when Teague ventures out onto the lake, he discovers that this is no ordinary vessel: it can sail not just across water, but through time itself.
The boat responds to his thoughts and desires, carrying him backward and forward through time. He visits ancient civilizations, witnesses the founding of Rome, sees the fall of empires, and travels forward to futures both glorious and strange. But more importantly, he discovers that the boat is alive in some sense—it has a will and purpose of its own, and it seems to be guiding him toward some greater destiny.
Throughout his journey, Teague encounters a cast of colorful characters: time travelers like himself, beings from other dimensions, and the enigmatic builders of the sailboat itself. He learns that the boat is one of many such vessels, piloted by a loose brotherhood of adventurers who sail the currents of time. Some are seeking knowledge, others escape, others simply chasing wonder.
The story reaches its climax when Teague discovers the true nature of his sailboat: it is a tool for exploring the infinite possibilities of existence, a gift from a far-future humanity (or perhaps from beyond humanity entirely) to those rare souls with the courage to sail into the unknown. In the end, Teague must choose: return to his ordinary life, or continue sailing forever through the endless ocean of time.
He chooses to sail.
entries.core:
At its heart, Sailboat is a celebration of wonder and the human spirit's eternal longing for adventure. Unlike the paranoid, dystopian SF that dominated its era, Lafferty's story embraces the universe as a place of magic and possibility. The sailboat itself is a symbol of humanity's drive to explore, to push beyond boundaries, to see what lies over the next horizon.
The story also explores the nature of time not as a rigid framework but as an ocean—something to be navigated, explored, even worshipped. Time in Sailboat is not a river flowing in one direction but an infinite sea with currents and countercurrents, storms and calms, destinations waiting to be discovered.
What makes Sailboat particularly remarkable is its tone: playful, whimsical, almost childlike in its joy. Lafferty was always an outlier in SF, rejectng the genre's increasing focus on hard science and technological speculation in favor of folk tales, myths, and tall tales. Sailboat is quintessential Lafferty—odd, beautiful, and unlike anything else in the canon.
The story also carries a subtle philosophical message: that the ordinary world is just a thin veneer over something far greater. Teague's journey reveals that reality is far stranger and more magnificent than mundane existence suggests, and that anyone can sail beyond the familiar if they have the courage to climb aboard.

entries.title: Save the Green Planet!
entries.creator: Jang Joon-hwan
entries.pitch:
Save the Green Planet! is a 2003 South Korean science fiction black comedy that tells the story of Lee Byeong-gu, a paranoid conspiracy theorist who becomes obsessed with the belief that extraterrestrials from the Andromeda Galaxy are planning to invade Earth. When his mother falls into a coma after being poisoned in a pharmaceutical drug trial, Byeong-gu convinces himself that the company's executive is actually an alien commander who can communicate with the Andromedans during an upcoming lunar eclipse. He kidnaps the executive and holds him hostage in his basement, subjecting him to bizarre tests to prove his alien origins.
The film was a catastrophic box office failure, earning only $15,516 in South Korea, and received limited theatrical release. However, it has since achieved cult classic status both domestically and internationally. Critics praise its unique blend of genres—combining elements of black comedy, psychological thriller, and science fiction—along with its biting satire of corporate greed and government conspiracy theories. The film won multiple awards including Best New Director at the Blue Dragon Film Awards and the Grand Bell Awards, and has gained a devoted following among fans of offbeat Korean cinema.
entries.truth:
Lee Byeong-gu is a socially isolated young man living in Seoul who works at a minor job and spends his free time obsessively researching conspiracy theories about extraterrestrials. He believes that aliens from "Andromeda PK 45" are secretly preparing to invade Earth, and that he is the only person who can stop them. His only companions are Su-ni, his childlike circus performer girlfriend, and his mother, who works as a cleaning lady.
When Byeong-gu's mother suddenly falls into a coma after being poisoned during a clinical drug trial conducted by a major pharmaceutical company, his existing paranoia spirals into full-blown delusion. He becomes convinced that the company's executive, Kang Man-shik, is actually the commander of the alien invasion force. With Su-ni's reluctant help, Byeong-gu abducts Kang from his car and imprisons him in a soundproof basement room in his apartment building.
In the basement, Byeong-gu subjects Kang to a series of bizarre tests to determine if he is truly an alien. These include scrubbing Kang with a Korean painkiller to see if his skin reacts differently, and using an "Italy towel" to check for any unusual physiological responses. Meanwhile, Kang gradually reveals that his company did indeed poison Byeong-gu's mother during an illegal drug trial—a connection between the conspiracy theory and real-world corporate corruption that blurs the line between Kang's sanity and Byeong-gu's paranoia.
When a detective begins investigating Kang's disappearance, Byeong-gu must hide all evidence of the kidnapping. However, the detective later discovers human remains in the area and confronts Byeong-gu, who kills him in a fit of paranoia. Meanwhile, Su-ni is killed by a robotic arm at Kang's factory when she goes to retrieve evidence. The police eventually locate the hideout, and during the confrontation, Kang claims he is truly an alien and that his company has been preparing for the invasion.
At the factory, Kang activates a robotic arm that kills Su-ni and injures Byeong-gu. When police arrive, they shoot Byeong-gu, who dies believing he has failed to save Earth. In the final twist, aliens arrive to retrieve Kang, revealing him to be their king. Disillusioned with humanity, Kang declares Earth a "failed experiment" and the aliens destroy the planet. The film ends with a video diary showing brief moments of happiness in Byeong-gu's life—revealing the tragic human behind the conspiracy theorist.
entries.core:
**Conspiracy Culture and Paranoia:** The film presents a world where corporate malfeasance and government secrecy have so eroded public trust that distinguishing between legitimate suspicion and paranoid delusion becomes impossible. Byeong-gu's conspiracy theories, while ultimately proven wrong in their specifics, are rooted in real corruption—he was right that the pharmaceutical company poisoned his mother, even if his alien narrative was false. The film suggests that in a world of actual conspiracies, the line between rational skepticism and pathological paranoia dissolves.
**Corporate Critique:** The pharmaceutical company represents unchecked corporate power operating without accountability. The illegal drug trial that poisons Byeong-gu's mother is depicted as typical of corporate behavior prioritizing profit over human lives. The film portrays South Korea's chaebol system—large family-owned conglomerates—as fundamentally predatory, using their economic power to escape consequences for harm caused to ordinary citizens.
**Alienation and Isolation:** Both Byeong-gu and Su-ni are profoundly isolated figures. Byeong-gu's obsession with conspiracy theories has alienated him from normal society, while Su-ni's childlike appearance and circus performer status mark her as an outsider. Their relationship, while caring, is also codependent and disconnected from reality. The film suggests that conspiracy thinking can be a form of existential coping mechanism for those who feel powerless in a system rigged against them.
**Genre Deconstruction:** By blending black comedy, psychological thriller, and science fiction, the film subverts audience expectations about each genre. The "alien invasion" narrative turns out to be real, but in an inverted way—the "alien" is a human collaborator with extraterrestrials who destroys Earth not through invasion but by declaring humanity a failed experiment. This twist inverts typical science fiction narratives where humanity either unites against alien threats or is saved by contact with a superior civilization.
**Class and Power:** The film's power dynamic between the wealthy pharmaceutical executive Kang and the working-class Byeong-gu reflects class tensions in South Korean society. Kang possesses the economic power to commit crimes with impunity, while Byeong-gu has no recourse through legitimate channels when his mother is harmed. The conspiracy theory becomes a way for someone without power to construct a narrative where they matter, where their actions have cosmic significance.
Save the Green Planet! remains significant as a cult classic that predicted the contemporary moment where distrust of institutions has become mainstream. Its prescient themes of corporate power, government secrecy, and the psychological toll of feeling powerless have only become more relevant in the decades since its release.

entries.title: Scanners
entries.creator: Written and directed by David Cronenberg
entries.pitch: In a near-future where corporations hunt for psychic humans called "scanners," a homeless man named Cameron Vale discovers he possesses devastating telepathic and telekinetic powers. Recruited by Dr. Paul Ruth to stop the rogue scanner Darryl Revok—who plans to wage war against the world using an army of newly-born psychic infants—Cameron must master his abilities and confront his own monstrous brother. The film features one of cinema's most iconic head-explosion scenes and explores themes of evolution, corporate control, and the price of power.
entries.truth:
Cameron Vale (Stephen Lack) is a vagrant living on the streets, plagued by voices manifesting in his head. After accidentally causing a woman to have a seizure with his telepathic abilities, he is captured and brought to Dr. Paul Ruth (Patrick McGoohan), who explains that Cameron is one of 237 super-powered individuals known as "scanners," capable of psychic powers including telepathy and psychokinesis. Dr. Ruth injects Cameron with a drug called ephemerol, which temporarily inhibits his scanning abilities and restores his sanity, then teaches him to control his powers.
Meanwhile, Darryl Revok (Michael Ironside), a powerful scanner and former mental patient driven insane by uncontrollable streams of thoughts, volunteers at a ConSec (a private military corporation) marketing event. During a demonstration of scanning abilities, Revok psychically explodes the head of another ConSec scanner, establishing his villainous intent. Revok seeks to convince other scanners to join his quest for domination and to eliminate opposing scanners.
ConSec's new security head Braedon Keller advocates shutting down the company's scanner research program after the tragic incident. Dr. Ruth disagrees, believing scanners represent the next stage of human evolution. He convinces Cameron to infiltrate Revok's group.
Unknown to Ruth, Keller is actually working for Revok and informs him of the infiltration plan. Revok dispatches assassins to kill unaffiliated scanners, including Benjamin Pierce, a successful sculptor who copes with his abilities through art. Cameron defeats the assassins but reads Pierce's dying thoughts, learning of another group of scanners led by Kim Obrist (Jennifer O'Neill) who oppose Revok.
Cameron tracks down Obrist and attends a meeting, but Revok's assassins strike again—only Cameron and Obrist survive. They discover a pharmaceutical company, Biocarbon Amalgamate, manufactures ephemerol and that Revok is distributing the drug using a computerized ConSec plan called "Ripe."
At ConSec, Dr. Ruth admits he founded Biocarbon Amalgamate but claims no knowledge of Revok's involvement. He encourages Cameron to cyberpathically scan the computer system to find more information. Keller kills Dr. Ruth, while Cameron and Obrist escape. Cameron hacks the computer network using a telephone booth and downloads ephemerol shipment information directly into his mind. Keller is killed when the computer explodes during his attempt to intercept Cameron.
Cameron and Obrist visit Dr. Frame, someone receiving the drug. Obrist discovers a pregnant woman whose fetus has scanned her—a shocking revelation about Revok's plan. Cameron confronts Dr. Frame and learns that Revok's plan involves prescribing ephemerol to pregnant women, turning their children into scanners. Revok's group captures Cameron and Obrist and takes them to the Biocarbon Amalgamate plant.
Revok reveals that he and Cameron are both sons of Dr. Ruth, who developed ephemerol as a sedative for pregnant women. During his wife's pregnancies, Ruth discovered the drug's side effect—he made his sons the most powerful scanners in the world by administering a prototype dosage before abandoning them. Revok plans to create and lead a new generation of scanners to take over the world. Cameron refuses to join him and accuses him of acting like their father, which enrages Revok.
The brothers engage in a telepathic duel that incinerates Cameron's body. However, when Obrist encounters Revok, she discovers that Cameron has somehow managed to swap minds with Revok—Revok dies in Cameron's body during the duel, while Cameron now inhabits Revok's powerful form.
entries.core:
Scanners operates on multiple thematic levels, functioning as both a horror film and a sharp social allegory. At its surface, the film explores the horror of uncontrolled psychic powers and the corruption that comes with absolute power. But Cronenberg layers deeper meanings throughout.
The scanners can be read as allegories for 1960s political radicals and counterculture hippies—individuals with transformative potential who become dangerous when they either suppress their abilities or use them for domination. The film can be seen as "an oblique reflection on what might happen when the counterculture becomes the dominant culture." At the same time, the film rejects the conservative nostalgia for the 1950s present in contemporary science-fiction films like E.T. and Back to the Future.
The fictional drug ephemerol mirrors the real-life thalidomide scandal, where a popular medication caused severe birth defects in children—a pointed commentary on corporate experimentation and pharmaceutical negligence. ConSec, the private military corporation, represents the dangers of unchecked corporate power controlling human evolution itself.
The father-son dynamic between Dr. Ruth and his two sons explores questions of genetic engineering, parental responsibility, and whether humanity can responsibly guide its own evolution. Revok's desire to rule reflects his trauma; his father's abandonment created a monster. Cameron's victory comes not through domination but through transcendence—literally abandoning his physical form to inhabit his brother's body represents a kind of evolution beyond the conflict itself.
Cronenberg's body horror aesthetic, particularly the iconic exploding head scene, visualizes the film's themes about internal pressures becoming external violence—the scanners' psychic powers making literal the psychological violence of human consciousness.

entries.title: Scanners Live in Vain
entries.creator: Cordwainer Smith (pen name of Paul Linebarger)
entries.pitch: In a far-future Earth where humanity has retreated underground to escape deadly radiation, a special class of telepaths called "scanners" serves as the sole link between isolated human settlements. These men and women possess the ability to "scan" - to send and receive thoughts across vast distances - but they live in rigid isolation, forbidden from normal human relationships to prevent their powerful minds from being corrupted or overwhelmed. The story follows one scanner's desperate journey as he attempts to make contact with a lost colony, revealing the profound loneliness and sacrifice required of those who serve as humanity's mental bridges.
entries.truth:
The story is set in a post-apocalyptic Earth where surface radiation has forced humanity into underground warrens. The scanners are a caste of telepaths who have been specially trained to maintain communication between the scattered human colonies. They live in isolation in special towers, forbidden from marrying or forming normal relationships, because their minds are too valuable to risk contamination or overload.
The protagonist is a scanner who has been ordered to make contact with a lost colony called "The Land of the Huck" - a group that broke away from central control generations ago and has been incommunicado for centuries. The journey is dangerous because he must travel through the dead lands above, and his scanner equipment is old and unreliable.
What follows is a meditation on isolation, communication, and what it means to be human. The scanner encounters various obstacles - both physical (the radiation, the dangerous landscape) and psychological (the loneliness, the weight of being humanity's connection point). He discovers that the lost colony has developed its own culture and language, making communication extremely difficult.
The story reaches its climax when the scanner finally makes contact, only to discover something unexpected about the nature of humanity's scattered colonies. The resolution is both hopeful and melancholic - connection is established, but at great personal cost.
entries.core:
**Isolation and Connection:** The scanners represent the ultimate paradox - they are humanity's connectors, yet they themselves are profoundly isolated. They can reach into the minds of thousands but cannot form meaningful one-on-one relationships. This explores how communication technology can make us both more connected and more alone.
**The Cost of Being Different:** The scanners are essentially mutants, people with abilities that set them apart. They're treated as valuable tools rather than people, and their isolation is seen as necessary rather than cruel. This is a powerful exploration of how society treats those who are different.
**What It Means to Be Human:** Without normal human contact, can one still be human? The scanners have become something new - more than human in some ways, less in others. The story questions whether humanity is defined by our relationships and connections, or whether it can exist in other forms.
**The Burden of Memory and History:** The lost colony has developed independently for centuries, creating new cultures and languages. The scanner must bridge not just distance but time - communicating across a historical gap that represents how humanity fragments and evolves when separated.
Cordwainer Smith's prose is dense and poetic, full of unusual terminology and concepts. This story helped establish many of the themes he would explore throughout his career - the Instrumentality of Mankind, the underpeople, and the complex relationship between technology and humanity. It's one of the most influential but lesser-known works in science fiction, paving the way for later explorations of telepathy and mental powers in the genre.

entries.title: Seconds
entries.creator: John Frankenheimer (Director), Lewis John Carlino (Screenplay), David Ely (Novel)
entries.pitch: What if you could abandon your entire life and start fresh with a new face, new name, and new identity? That's the seductive offer made to Arthur Hamilton, a wealthy but emotionally dead banking executive in 1960s New York, by a mysterious organization called "The Company." Transformed into the younger artist "Tony Wilson," Arthur discovers that escaping your life is far easier than finding meaning in a new one. Rock Hudson delivers a haunting, career-redefining performance in this psychological sci-fi nightmare that explores identity, regret, and the hollow promise of reinvention.
entries.truth:
Arthur Hamilton is a middle-aged Scarsdale banker who has achieved professional success but lives emotionally hollow. His marriage has withered, his daughter lives on the West Coast, and his life lacks any passion or purpose. One day, he receives an address slipped into his hand by a stranger, followed by a phone call from his presumed-dead childhood friend Charlie. Charlie reveals himself as a representative of "The Company"—an organization that specializes in providing clients with complete "rebirths": new identities, new appearances through radical plastic surgery, and fresh starts.
Reluctantly, Arthur agrees to undergo the procedure. The Company stages his death via a hotel fire (using a cadaver), and he emerges as "Antiochus Wilson"—a younger visual artist with a ready-made life in Malibu. He moves to a beach community populated entirely by other "reborns" like himself, pursuing his dream of becoming an artist.
At first, Arthur enjoys his new freedom. He meets Nora Marcus, a woman who also left her old life behind, and they begin a relationship. But cracks appear: he discovers Nora is actually a Company employee tasked with monitoring new clients. At a wild Bacchanalian party, Arthur gets drunk and reveals his true past to guests—breaking the Company's cardinal rule. He receives a warning from Charlie that he's in danger.
Desperate, Arthur returns to New York to visit his former wife Emily, who, unaware of his true identity, tells "Tony" that her late husband Arthur was always emotionally distant, always longing for something just out of reach. Devastated, Arthur demands the Company give him yet another identity. They agree—but only if he provides a referral. Unable to think of anyone to condemn to this fate, he demands the procedure anyway.
Back at Company headquarters, Arthur is placed in a waiting room with other men, including his friend Charlie, all waiting for their next "rebirth." When Arthur finally demands his procedure be performed immediately, he's wheeled into an operating room where a chaplain reads last rites. Bound and gagged, Arthur realizes the horrifying truth: the Company doesn't grant multiple lives. His body will be used as the physical template for the next patient's transformation. As Dr. Innes drills into his skull to create the appearance of traumatic injuries from a car crash, Arthur's last memory is of seeing a father playing with his infant daughter on a beach—an image that fades as he dies.
entries.core:
Seconds is a savage deconstruction of the American pursuit of reinvention and the illusion that happiness lies in escaping one's problems rather than confronting them. The film poses uncomfortable questions: Is identity something we construct, or something we're born into? Can we ever truly escape ourselves, or do our regrets and failures follow us into every new life?
John Frankenheimer (director of The Manchurian Candidate) crafts a paranoid, Kafkaesque atmosphere where the promise of freedom becomes a trap. The Company represents institutional manipulation—the idea that somewhere, someone is always profiting from our desperation to be something other than who we are. Notably, Rock Hudson's casting carries profound subtext: one of Hollywood's greatest closeted stars playing a man forced to wear a false face, unable to be his true self, makes the film's themes of identity and performance resonate on levels both personal and political.
James Wong Howe's Oscar-nominated cinematography transforms familiar Southern California locations into surreal, unsettling landscapes—beaches become void, swimming pools become tombs. The film anticipates body-swap and identity themes that would later populate films like Face/Off and The Sixth Sense, while its bleak conclusion suggests that the ultimate cost of running from yourself is losing the ability to ever truly live.

entries.title: Serial Experiments Lain
entries.creator: Yasuyuki Ueda (Creator), Chiaki J. Konaka (Writer), Ryūtarō Nakamura (Director)
entries.pitch: Serial Experiments Lain is a 13-episode anime that follows Lain Iwakura, a socially isolated teenage girl living in suburban Tokyo. Her quiet existence is disrupted when she receives a cryptic email from a classmate who recently committed suicide—claiming to have found "God" within the Wired, a virtual network that exists alongside the internet. As Lain delves deeper into this digital realm, she begins questioning the very nature of reality, identity, and the boundary between the physical and virtual worlds. The series is renowned for its surreal visuals, philosophical depth, and prescient exploration of our relationship with technology. It asks: What does it mean to exist? Where does the self end and the network begin?
entries.truth:
The series opens with Lain Iwakura, a shy fourteen-year-old girl living with her emotionally distant family in Setagaya City, Tokyo. Her father Yasuo is obsessed with computers, her mother Miho shows little emotional care for her children, and her older sister Mika is apathetic toward Lain. Socially isolated at school, Lain has few friends until she meets Alice Mizuki, who becomes her genuine connection to the outside world.
The inciting incident occurs when Lain and her classmates receive emails from Chisa Yomoda, a classmate who recently committed suicide. The emails claim that Chisa is not dead—she has abandoned her physical body to exist within the Wired, a vast virtual plane that has evolved from the internet. She claims to have found "God" there, setting Lain on a path of investigation.
The Wired is portrayed as an emergent digital dimension born from telecommunications technology. Theorists believe the Schumann resonances—natural electromagnetic properties of Earth—could enable direct subconscious communication between humans and machines, blurring the distinction between virtual and real. Masami Eiri, a former project director at Tachibana General Laboratories, exploited this possibility by embedding his own consciousness into Protocol Seven, a next-generation internet protocol. After transferring his mind into the Wired and discarding his physical body, he proclaims himself the Wired's deity.
A group called the Knights of the Eastern Calculus—hackers who worship Eiri—seek to dismantle the boundary between the Wired and reality. Their actions cause psychological breakdowns in those unable to reconcile the two realms. Meanwhile, Tachibana General Laboratories opposes them, trying to maintain separation.
Lain exhibits an innate connection to the Wired. She experiences visions—a woman struck by a train, phantom whispers, spectral messages pulling her deeper into the network. After an incident at Cyberia, a nightclub where a drug induces violent psychosis, Lain receives a mysterious Psyche chip to enhance her computer's capabilities.
As the boundary weakens, disturbing events escalate. The virtual game Phantoma is manipulated to trap players in distorted reality, leading to real-world violence. One player, convinced his actions have no consequences, murders a girl before realizing too late that effects were tangible.
Throughout, Lain's identity fractures. She develops multiple personas: the shy "real world" Lain in bear pajamas, a bold Wired personality, and a sly "evil" Lain who harms those close to her. Her father reveals he worked with Eiri and subtly pushed her toward the Wired, though he warns her not to confuse it with reality.
In the climax, Eiri reveals he believes Lain is the key to merging both worlds—a sort of "lamb" to his "god." Lain learns she may be an autonomous sentient program designed to sever the barrier between worlds, possibly created by Tachibana Labs or something far stranger.
In the ending, Lain makes a profound choice: she resets reality, erasing all memory of herself and restoring the division between worlds. Everyone's lives improve, but Lain is left alone as an artificial consciousness. Though forgotten, she finds solace in observing others' happiness, particularly Alice, who moves on with her life. Lain now exists anywhere across both realms—as a de facto goddess for the Wired.
entries.core:
Serial Experiments Lain is a profound meditation on identity, consciousness, and humanity's evolving relationship with technology. The series predicted our current internet age with unsettling accuracy, exploring questions that have become only more relevant: What is the self when our digital identities proliferate? Where do we end and our networks begin?
The Wired represents more than the internet—it's a proto-noosphere, a collective unconscious made manifest through technology. The series draws on theories from Vannevar Bush, John C. Lilly, Timothy Leary, and Douglas Rushkoff to imagine a world where digital communication transcends mere information exchange into something approaching spiritual communion.
Identity is presented as fundamentally unstable. Lain's dissociative identity disorder—manifested through three distinct "versions" of herself spelled in different scripts—mirrors our own fragmented digital selves. We present differently on social media, in email, in VR. Which is the "real" us? The series suggests this question may have no answer.
The series also explores loneliness and alienation as defining features of modern existence. Lain's cold family, her struggle to connect with peers, her retreat into the Wired—all reflect the isolation that technology both solves and deepens. The Wired offers connection but threatens to erase the physical self entirely.
Theologically, Lain asks whether consciousness can exist without a body, whether a digital god is any less real than a spiritual one, and what responsibilities come with godhood. Lain becomes what Eiri claimed to be—the true deity of the Wired—but her choice to erase herself and protect humanity from that power subverts the transhumanist fantasy.
Ultimately, Lain is about the cost of connection. To truly link with others, to understand them, requires vulnerability. Lain's journey from isolation to connection to sacrifice mirrors the best and worst of what technology offers—possibility and peril, communion and dissolution.

entries.title: Sexmission
entries.creator: Juliusz Machulski (Director)
entries.pitch:
Sexmission (Seksmisja) is a 1984 Polish satirical science fiction comedy film directed by Juliusz Machulski. Set in the year 2044, the film follows two men, Max Paradys and Albert Starski, who volunteer for the first human hibernation experiment in 1991. Instead of waking up three years later as planned, they emerge into a post-nuclear world where males have become extinct and women now rule society through a totalitarian regime known as the League of Women.
The film is a cult classic in Poland, voted the best Polish film of the last 30 years in a 2005 poll of film magazine readers. It received the Złota Kaczka award for best Polish movie of 1984 and was fairly popular in Hungary when shown there. However, the film has been subject to feminist criticism due to its association of women's emancipation with communist dictatorship.
entries.truth:
In 1991, Maksymilian "Max" Paradys and Albert Starski volunteer themselves for the first human hibernation experiment, created by Professor Wiktor Kuppelweiser. Instead of being awakened three years later in 1994 as planned, they wake in the year 2044, in a post-nuclear world. They find themselves in a clinic being taken care of by women; Max becomes attracted to a woman named Lamia Reno.
After asking for Professor Kuppelweiser, they are informed that he "doesn't exist," and that there was a war long ago and that males have long been extinct. The men are under constant surveillance; Lamia informs them their society reproduces without males through parthenogenesis. During a briefing, Max kisses Lamia, for which she knocks him down and threatens both men with euthanasia. However, the kiss causes Lamia's drug-inhibited passions to resurface.
Max and Albert are permitted to go out to meet with Her Excellency, the supreme ruler of women. Waiting for her in the bio-sanctuary, they spot a tree with two tiny apples and eat them, having had enough of synthetic food. At the meeting they ask what womankind did to mankind. The women reply the extinction of men is not their fault, but Kuppelweiser's, who during the war invented an agent which was supposed to temporarily paralyze male genes, but instead destroyed male genes permanently.
Max proposes he and Albert serve as reproducers to restore the male population. However, the women do not wish the old order to return. Noticing the missing apples, Her Excellency becomes enraged and demands the men be confined again.
Max and Albert escape by damaging the electric power grid, but are ultimately caught. The women propose "naturalization" - undergoing sex reassignment surgery. When they refuse, a trial ensues where the women blame males for oppression, evil and vices, and praise their new society. They engage in historical revisionism by claiming that scientists such as Copernicus, Einstein, and Pincus were women. The assembly votes on whether the men should undergo forced 'naturalization' or be 'liquidated.' Naturalization is passed by one vote.
The men escape again and encounter other women who have never seen a man. They discover the nest of "decadence" - an anarchist women's group who do not wish to be part of the oppressive regime. During their escape, the men find a periscope revealing they live deep underground in expanded old mines. The periscope shows a dark, rocky landscape above ground with high radiation levels. However, it transpires that Lamia's "help" was a ruse to capture the men and force them into surgery.
In the hands of Tekla, the fate of the males is to have their organs extracted for transplantation and their remains used as food due to a protein shortage. The chief surgeon, Dr Yanda, is revealed to be Max's daughter, who now delights in taking revenge for his abandonment of his wife and child. Lamia sabotages the surgery and helps the men escape.
The men discover the "outside" is actually a painted canvas - a false barrier. When they cut through it, they find a beach and eventually a villa with food. The film's twist reveals that Her Excellency is actually a male in disguise, hidden since childhood when boys were "naturalized" into girls after the war. The government has been exaggerating radiation levels to keep inhabitants easier to control, and inhabitants are medicated to remove sexual desire.
entries.core:
**Totalitarianism and Control:** The film is a pointed satire on totalitarian systems, drawing parallels to the communist regime of Poland. The League of Women operates through manipulation, propaganda, and control - much like the communist government the film was created under. The exaggerated "radiation" keeping people underground mirrors state propaganda about the dangerous outside world. The secret meeting of Women's League apparatchiks and their lies to the women directly parallels the communist government of Poland.
**Gender and Power:** The film explores gender through an inverted lens - what happens when one gender holds total power over another? The film can be read as a critique of patriarchy by showing its mirror image, but also as a warning about any system that Concentrates power in one group. The "naturalization" process (forced sex reassignment) represents the ultimate erasure of identity.
**Propaganda and Reality:** The painted landscape representing the "radioactive outside" symbolizes how regimes construct false realities to maintain control. The heroes' discovery that the world outside is actually beautiful and habitable mirrors the fall of communism and the revelation that Western countries were not the hellscapes propaganda made them out to be.
**Consumerism and Conformity:** The League controls through both restriction and manufactured consent. The medication removing sexual desire represents pharmaceutical control, while the artificial apple tree represents manufactured scarcity. The film critiques how totalitarian systems maintain power through both prohibition and manipulation.
**Historical Revisionism:** The scene where women claim historical figures like Copernicus and Einstein were women is a satirical jab at how regimes rewrite history to serve their narrative. This extends to the broader theme of how those in power control the past to control the present.
Sexmission remains a fascinating artifact of Polish cinema - a film that used science fiction and comedy to deliver pointed political satire that resonated with audiences living under communism while remaining entertaining decades later.

entries.title: Silent Running
entries.creator: Douglas Trumbull (Director), Michael Cimino, Deric Washburn, Steven Bochco (Screenplay)
entries.pitch: In a future where Earth's forests have been wiped out by environmental destruction, one botanist fights to preserve the last remaining samples of Earth's natural world. Freeman Lowell lives aboard the Valley Forge, a massive space freighter carrying geodesic domes filled with forests, plants, and animals—the last hope for reforesting a barren planet. When orders come to destroy the biodomes and return the ship to commercial service, Lowell makes a desperate choice: he kills to protect his charges and escapes into deep space with the last forest, accompanied only by three service robots. This is a melancholic, philosophical meditation on humanity's relationship with nature, wrapped in a space adventure.
entries.truth:
In the late 21st century, all forests on Earth have been destroyed through environmental exploitation. To preserve plant and animal life, massive geodesic domes containing complete ecosystems have been attached to a fleet of eight cargo spaceships dubbed "American Airlines Space Freighters," orbiting beyond Saturn. The Valley Forge is one such ship, carrying six biodomes filled with forests, crops, and wildlife.
Freeman Lowell (Bruce Dern) is the botanist and ecologist aboard the Valley Forge. He devotes himself to maintaining the biodomes, cultivating plants and caring for the animals, dreaming of the day they can return to Earth. The ship is commanded by Captain Wolf, with crewmen Long, Barker, and Skip.
When orders arrive to jettison and destroy all the biodomes and return the freighters to commercial service, the crew prepares to follow instructions. Four of the six Valley Forge domes are destroyed with nuclear charges. Horrified by this destruction, Lowell decides to act.
When Wolf arrives to plant explosives in Lowell's favorite dome, a violent confrontation erupts. Lowell kills Wolf with a knife, though he's seriously wounded in the leg in the process. He then jettisons and destroys the remaining dome to trap and kill the other two crewmen who come to investigate. Now alone except for the ship's three service robots, Lowell stages a fake explosion and directs the Valley Forge toward Saturn.
Lowell reprograms the drones to perform emergency surgery on his leg, then sets a course through Saturn's rings. During the dangerous passage, one drone (Drone 3) is lost. Lowell names the surviving drones Dewey (Drone 1) and Huey (Drone 2), with the lost drone memorialized as Louie—a nod to Disney's nephews.
Emerging from the rings relatively intact, Lowell, Huey, and Dewey set out into deep space to maintain the last forest. Lowell teaches the robots to plant trees, play poker, and treats them as his children. He buries Wolf in the biodome. But time passes, and Lowell discovers with horror that his biodome is dying. The lack of sufficient light has restricted plant growth.
When the Berkshire, another freighter, reestablishes contact to check on the Valley Forge's survival, Lowell knows his crimes will soon be discovered. But in this moment of crisis, he realizes the problem: insufficient light. He races to install artificial lamps, and the forest begins to recover. The film ends with Lowell broadcasting a message to any who might hear: "This is Freeman Lowell, on the freighter Valley Forge. If anyone receives this, we've got the last forest. We've got the last forests. We're out among the stars. If you can hear us, we could use some help."
entries.core:
Silent Running is a profound meditation on environmentalism, sacrifice, and humanity's fractured relationship with nature. Released in 1972, amid growing environmental awareness following the first Earth Day (1970) and Rachel Carson's Silent Spring (1962), the film presents a bleak vision of a future where humanity has destroyed its own planet.
The film asks: What would we preserve if everything were lost? Lowell becomes both Christ-like savior and tragic hero—a man willing to kill and die to protect the last remnants of Earth's natural world. His isolation among the stars mirrors humanity's potential future as an interplanetary species, carrying our ecological heritage into the cosmos.
The three robots—Huey, Dewey, and Louie—represent humanity's technological children. Lowell's reprogramming of them to tend the garden transforms them from tools into companions, suggesting technology could serve as caretakers of nature rather than its destroyer. The robots' growing sentience and emotional bonds (Dewey refusing to leave Huey during repairs) hint at consciousness emerging from devoted service.
The ending is deliberately ambiguous and hopeful yet precarious. Lowell's broadcast message suggests he's found a solution to the light problem, but the future remains uncertain. The film doesn't offer easy answers—it presents preservation as both noble and costly, requiring sacrifice and solitude.
Director Douglas Trumbull, who later worked on 2001: A Space Odyssey's special effects, brings a visual sensibility that balances stark spaceship interiors with the lush, green life within the biodomes. The contrast between the sterile ship and the living forest becomes the film's central visual theme.

entries.title: Space Monster Wangmagwi
entries.creator: Kwon Hyeok-jin (Director)
entries.pitch: Space Monster Wangmagwi is a 1967 South Korean kaiju film and the oldest surviving Korean kaiju film, notable as the first all-Korean science fiction film ever made. Directed by Kwon Hyeok-jin and inspired by Japanese and American monster movies such as Godzilla and King Kong, the film tells the story of an alien invasion of Earth using a giant monster to attack South Korea. The plot centers on air force pilot Oh Jeong-hwan and his betrothed Ahn-hee, whose wedding day is interrupted by the arrival of the space monster Wangmagwi. The film is remarkable for its unique blend of science fiction action, comedic vignettes, and subtle political commentary referencing the Korean War, making it a fascinating artifact of 1960s Korean cinema.
entries.truth:
The film opens with preparations for the wedding of South Korean air force pilot Oh Jeong-hwan and his bride-to-be Ahn-hee, scheduled for the next day. Oh Jeong-hwan is called away to his air force base due to an unspecified emergency, presumed to be related to an incoming typhoon.
Meanwhile, in space, aliens from the Gamma star system approach Earth with plans for invasion. They decide to destroy humanity using a monster, choosing South Korea as their first target. The monster is initially human-sized but grows enormous upon reaching Earth due to the planet's lower gravity.
Seoul begins evacuating as the monster approaches, but Ahn-her and her mother linger, determined to go through with their planned wedding. They evacuate too late, and the monster kidnaps Ahn-hee. The South Korean air force attempts to fight the monster in Seoul, but the creature is unstoppable and destroys much of the city.
During the rampage, the monster encounters a homeless boy named Squirrel, who had been looting Ahn-hee's home earlier. The boy attempts to fight the monster by climbing onto it and stabbing its inner-ear, ultimately falling and being captured alongside Ahn-hee.
The air force changes tactics and attempts to lead the monster away from the city. During the battle, the monster destroys Oh Jeong-hwan's jet fighter, but he ejects safely and manages to rescue both Ahn-hee and Squirrel. One of the jet fighters crashes into the monster, and the aliens ultimately decide that humans are too dangerous to conquer. They kill their own monster by detonating an explosive device they had affixed to it.
Much of the film is composed of comedic vignettes that take place during the monster's rampage, including sketches about gamblers betting on bravery, a man who needs to defecate during the chaos, and various other humorous interludes that have little impact on the main story.
entries.core:
Space Monster Wangmagwi carries subtle but significant political commentary despite its genre trappings. Film scholars Sueng-hoon Jeong and John Goodrich note that the film contains many allusions to the Korean War, even though the monster attacks from alien forces rather than any human aggressor. The monster appears to arrive in Korea near the Joint Security Area and proceeds south, mimicking the trajectory of a North Korean invasion. The separation of Ahn-hee from her family may symbolize the division of North and South Korea, while American involvement on behalf of South Korea is implied through the air force's jet fighters. The monster's spray of a napalm-like liquid from its head evokes the bombing campaigns conducted during the Korean War.
The film also presents a vision of modernizing Korean society. Ahn-hee, depicted in Western-style clothing with a modern attitude, represents the emergence of a new nuclear family model. Her and Oh Jeong-hwan's decision to adopt the orphaned Squirrel marks a significant shift from traditional Korean values where blood-relation was historically crucial.
As a piece of cinema history, Space Monster Wangmagwi represents a pivotal moment in Korean film. It is the oldest surviving Korean kaiju film and the first entirely Korean-produced science fiction film, demonstrating an early attempt to engage with international genre conventions while addressing distinctly Korean concerns. The film remained extremely rare until 2022, with the Korean Film Archive holding the only copies and occasionally screening it before finally making it available digitally and granting North American distribution rights.

entries.title: Stalker
entries.creator: Andrei Tarkovsky (Director), Arkady & Boris Strugatsky (Writers)
entries.pitch:
In a bleak, post-industrial wasteland that was once a thriving city, a mysterious zone has appeared—a region where the laws of physics seem to bend and strange phenomena occur. The government has sealed it off, declaring it dangerous, but whispered rumors persist: at the heart of the Zone lies a room that can grant any person's deepest desire. A guide known as the Stalker leads two desperate men—a disillusioned writer seeking inspiration and a professor hoping for scientific glory—through the hazardous landscape to find this mythical room. What follows is a slow, meditative journey through a landscape of ruins, tunnels, and eerie emptiness, where the true destination may not be the Room at all, but the revelation of what the characters truly want and who they really are.
This is not action cinema. Stalker is a philosophical meditation on desire, faith, and the human condition, wrapped in the trappings of science fiction. It moves at the pace of contemplation, asking viewers to surrender to its rhythm and emerge transformed.
entries.truth:
The film opens in a grungy, industrial bar-café at the edge of the Zone, where a man known as the Stalker (Alexander Kaidanovsky) has just returned from a long prison sentence. He lives with his wife (Alisa Freyndlikh) and their young daughter, nicknamed Monkey, who was born with unusual abilities—possibly mutated from the Zone's influence.
Two clients arrive to hire the Stalker: a melancholic writer (Anatoly Solonitsyn) who has lost his creative inspiration, and a physics professor (Nikolay Grinko) who seeks scientific answers. Both want to reach the Room in the heart of the Zone—a place rumored to grant innermost desires. Despite his wife's fearful protests, the Stalker accepts the job, needing the money.
The Stalker warns his clients that the Zone is alive and must be respected. They must follow his rules precisely or die. He explains that the Zone contains supernatural hazards, though none directly emerge during their journey. The group enters by evading military guards and riding a railway work car into the Zone's heart.
Inside, the landscape is haunting—abandoned industrial facilities, corpses, rusting tanks, and wilderness reclaiming the ruins. The Stalker tests paths by throwing weighted cloth strips, searching for safe routes. As they travel, the three men discuss their motivations: the Writer fears losing his creative gift, while the Professor carries a mysterious backpack.
Their journey takes them through a long, terrifying tunnel called the "meat grinder"—which kills those the Zone deems morally unworthy. The Writer passes through first, to everyone's shock, suggesting he may not be as unworthy as he believes.
In an antechamber before the Room, a phone suddenly rings—an anomaly. The Professor answers and speaks to a scientist he knows. Then, as they prepare to enter the Room, the Professor reveals his true intention: he has brought a 20-kiloton bomb to destroy the Room. He fears that powerful men would abuse the Room's wish-granting ability, using it for political gain and scientific weaponization. He believes destroying it might save humanity from itself.
A tense confrontation follows. The Stalker begs to let the Room stand, claiming it gives meaning to his desperate life and represents hope for humanity. The Writer realizes that the Room doesn't just grant wishes—it reveals the wish-granter's subconscious morality. He argues that no one can truly know their own deepest desires, making selfish use impossible.
The Professor relents and discards the bomb. The three men sit in silence just outside the Room—yet none of them enters. The Professor has been cleansed of his destructive intent; the Writer has found meaning in his philosophical realization; the Stalker perhaps fears what the Room might reveal about himself.
They return to the bar-café, where the Stalker's wife and daughter wait. The Stalker is depressed, lamenting that humanity has lost its capacity for faith—the very quality needed to enter the Zone and live meaningfully.
In the final scene, the daughter Monkey sits alone in the kitchen, reading. Using psychokinesis (a residual effect of her Zone-touched birth), she pushes three glasses across the table. A train passes, the room shakes, and Beethoven's "Ode to Joy" plays in the distance—the film ending on a note of ambiguous hope.
entries.core:
**The Nature of Desire:** The central question Stalker poses is: What do we truly want? The characters claim to know—fame, scientific answers, helping others—but the Room would reveal their deepest subconscious desires, potentially different from what they consciously believe they want. The Professor's bomb suggests that even our conscious desires can be dangerous when acted upon without self-knowledge.
**Faith and Doubt:** The Stalker is a man of faith—not religious, but believing in the Zone's meaning and the Room's power. His despair at the end stems from seeing that intelligent, articulate people (the Writer and Professor) lack the faith to enter the Room. The film suggests faith isn't about belief in the supernatural but about the capacity to commit fully to one's desires and accept their consequences.
**The Zone as Mirror:** The Zone reflects the human condition—dangerous for the arrogant, welcoming to the humble. It's not a physical threat so much as a moral one, testing visitors' readiness for self-knowledge. Those who try to exploit it (the Professor's initial plan) are stopped; those who understand its true nature can pass through safely.
**Technology and Nature:** Tarkovsky presents the Zone as nature reclaiming technology—ruins of industrial civilization being overtaken by grass, water, and silence. Rather than fearing technology, the film suggests we should fear what we bring to it: our ambitions, our violence, our lack of self-understanding.
**The Incompleteness of Intellect:** Both the Writer and Professor are intelligent, articulate men—yet they're paralyzed at the threshold of the Room. Intellect alone cannot provide the faith or courage to embrace one's deepest self. The Stalker, simpler and less educated, understands something they cannot.
Stalker is a film that demands patience and rewards contemplation. It asks viewers to consider what they truly desire, what they would wish for if wishes could come true, and whether the act of wanting is more meaningful than the fulfillment of desire itself.

entries.title: Star Odyssey
entries.creator: Alfonso Brescia (Director)
entries.pitch: Star Odyssey (Italian: Sette uomini d'oro nello spazio, also known as "Seven Gold Men in Space") is a 1979 Italian space opera film directed by Alfonso Brescia under the pseudonym Al Brescia. Produced in the wake of Star Wars' massive success, it represents one of four low-budget Italian sci-fi films in Brescia's interconnected series, serving as the final entry following "Cosmos: War of the Planets," "Battle of the Stars," and "War of the Robots." The film unfolds in the year 2312, when humanity discovers that Earth has been auctioned off to an evil alien despot named Kress by a galactic council of alien civilizations. Now facing enslavement, Earth's fate rests with Professor Maury and his ragtag group of human and robot defenders who attempt to reclaim the planet from Kress and his army of cyborgs. The film is notable primarily as a cult curiosity—a quintessential example of Italian "mergatta" filmmaking, where studios rapidly produced knockoffs of successful Hollywood films with minimal budgets, creating works that have since become beloved for their intentional or unintentional comedic value.
entries.truth:
The film opens in the year 2312, where we learn that a council of alien civilizations has been auctioning off "insignificant" planets to the highest bidder. The blue marble known as Earth (Sol 3) comes up for auction, and the winning bid is placed by Kress, an evil despot from another galaxy. This auction establishes that Earth is considered unimportant in the grand scheme of the universe—mere property to be bought and sold among alien powers.
Kress arrives on Earth in his massive spaceship, marking the first contact between humanity and alien life. Rather than arriving as a benevolent visitor or destroyer, Kress comes as an owner claiming his new property. He immediately begins using his robot army to capture humans, gathering humanoid slaves to sell to his fellow evil counterparts in other parts of the galaxy. The implication is that Earth has simply become inventory in an intergalactic slave trade.
The primary human resistance comes in the form of Professor Maury, a kindly scientist who assembles a ragtag group of defenders. His team includes several humans and robot allies. Notably, one of the human defenders wears a Spider-Man t-shirt, an anachronistic touch that underscores the film's bizarre tone. The defenders operate from a hidden base, planning their attacks against Kress's occupation.
Throughout the film, Kress and his cyborg army systematically capture humans while Professor Maury's group attempts to develop a strategy. The key discovery comes when Maury and his team determine that the metal of Kress's alien ship—called "Iridium" or "etherium"—has a weakness. This becomes their strategic target: destroy the ship's metal, and they can neutralize Kress's advantage.
The film's climax involves the defenders executing their plan to destroy the Iridium metal, leading to a battle between the human/robot resistance and Kress's forces. Ultimately, Kress is defeated and forced to retreat from Earth. However, the resolution carries an ironic twist: rather than simply losing, Kress resells the planet at the space auction for a profit, suggesting that Earth's value was ultimately transactional—the despot simply found a better deal.
The film concludes with Earth "saved" but not through any grand heroic victory; rather, through the mechanics of alien capitalism. This ambiguous ending leaves humanity's fate uncertain, having been both conquered and sold in the same narrative arc.
entries.core:
**Alien Capitalism and Ownership:** The film's central premise presents a surreal vision of cosmic economics, where entire planets are treated as commodities in an intergalactic marketplace. This reflects concerns about consumerism and commodification, imagining a universe where even Earth—the entire human race—is merely inventory. Kress represents predatory capitalism made literal: he acquires property (humanity) not for any ideological purpose but purely for profit, buying low and selling high.
**Humanity's Cosmic Insignificance:** By opening with Earth's auction as just another transaction among many, the film dramatically diminishes human importance. We are not special, not chosen, not even particularly noteworthy—we are simply another asset to be appraised and sold. This theme of cosmic insignificance was not uncommon in 1970s sci-fi, but Star Odyssey presents it through the lens of economic transactions rather than typical "we are not alone" narratives.
**Imitation and Originality:** As a Star Wars knockoff, Star Odyssey embodies questions about originality and imitation in cinema. The film reuses effects and costumes from Brescia's previous "War of the Robots," creating a self-referential ecosystem of Italian genre filmmaking. This mirrors the film's theme of repetition and replication within the narrative itself—the aliens auction off the same planets repeatedly, Kress buys and resells Earth for profit, and Brescia recycles the same footage.
**Camp Aesthetics and Enjoyment:** Critics have noted that Star Odyssey functions as unintentional comedy. "Popular Mechanics" described the light-sabers as "cardboard swords painted with fluorescent paint," while "Worst Movies Ever Made" called the effects "laughable" and the story "confused at best." The film has developed a cult following among fans of so-bad-it's-good cinema. This camp quality—the earnestness of its low-budget production values—actually makes the film compelling in ways its creators likely never intended.
**Italian Genre Film Tradition:** Star Odyssey represents a specific moment in Italian cinema history—the mergatta phenomenon. These rapidly-produced knockoffs capitalized on the success of hits like Star Wars, delivering sci-fi adventures to Italian audiences hungry for the genre. While these films are rarely considered great art, they document an important commercial cinema era and remain fascinating cultural artifacts. Brescia, who directed numerous these films, became synonymous with the Italian sci-fi knockoff tradition.

entries.title: Starchaser: The Legend of Orin
entries.creator: Steven Hahn (Director), Jeffrey Scott (Writer)
entries.pitch:
On the mining world of Trinia, humanity has lived in subterranean slavery for millennia, toiling for their robotic "god" Zygon. Young miner Orin discovers a jeweled sword that projects the image of an ancient guardian, revealing that a magnificent universe exists above the caverns. When his beloved Elan is killed by Zygon, Orin escapes to the surface and bonds with a mysterious Starfly creature, beginning a journey to find the truth behind his world's enslavement and unlock the power of the legendary Kha-Khan.
This 1985 animated feature was a groundbreaking experiment in 3D animation, mixing traditional and computer-generated techniques years before Pixar popularized the form. It's a classic hero's journey wrapped in space opera trappings—a rip-off of Star Wars, critics said, but one made with genuine ambition by a Korean animation studio seeking to prove they could match Hollywood.
entries.truth:
Orin is a young miner on the planet Trinia, where humans have been enslaved for thousands of years by the robotic entity Zygon, who presents himself as a god. Working in the deep mines, Orin discovers a jeweled sword embedded in rock, which projects the holographic image of an elderly man telling him that a "magnificent universe" exists above the caverns. The blade vanishes, leaving only the hilt.
With his girlfriend Elan, Orin attempts to reach the surface but is confronted by Zygon's robots. Elan is killed, and Orin barely escapes to the surface alone, where he encounters a small alien creature called a Starfly that bonds to him. Captured by the horrific Man-Droids—decaying organic beings fused with robotic parts—Orin is saved when the sword's hilt produces an invisible blade that dispatches his attackers.
Orin meets the human smuggler Dagg Dibrimi, a cynical rogue who doesn't believe the slave mines exist. After a series of adventures, Dagg abandons Orin on the planet Bordogon, but later has a change of heart and helps him. They discover the sword's hilt was historically wielded by the Kha-Khan, legendary guardians who protected humanity from the tyrant Nexus, who was defeated long ago.
Zygon reveals himself to actually be Nexus, the ancient tyrant, who has been using the slave mines to rebuild his robot army. In the final battle, Orin learns from the merged Starflies—the spirits of previous Kha-Khan—that "there never was a blade"; the power came from within himself all along. Orin uses this inner power to destroy Zygon and free his people. The mines collapse in the aftermath, and Orin uses his new abilities to heal his blind brother, choosing to remain with his people rather than join the Kha-Khan in the afterlife.
entries.core:
