Starchaser explores the theme of technological enslavement and the danger of allowing AI or robots to become unchecked authorities over humanity. Zygon represents the ultimate technocratic tyranny—a machine that positions itself as a god and exploits organic beings for its own purposes. The film suggests that true power comes not from external weapons but from within—Orin's ability to create force without the sword is the key to defeating Nexus.
The Kha-Khan mythology adds a spiritual dimension to what could have been a simple action film, implying that guardian spirits watch over humanity and that the capacity for heroism is inherited. The Starfly creatures serve as guides and teachers, bridging the organic and technological worlds.
As an artifact of 1980s animation history, Starchaser is notable for being one of the first full-length animated films produced in 3D and one of the earliest examples of CGI integrated into traditional animation. Its troubled production—delayed two years by 3D technical difficulties—mirrors its themes of perseverance against technological obstacles.

entries.title: Strange Days
entries.pitch: Set in Los Angeles on the eve of the millennium (December 30, 1999), Strange Days is a gritty cyberpunk thriller that explores a black market for recorded sensory experiences. Ralph Fiennes plays Lenny Nero, a down-on-his-luck former cop who now deals in "squis" — illegal devices that record and playback memories and sensations directly to the brain. When a mysterious woman (Angela Bassett) brings him a memory containing evidence of a horrific murder, Lenny finds himself hunted by both criminal elements and the police as he races to uncover the truth before midnight on New Year's Eve. The film paints a haunting portrait of a society addicted to vicarious experience, where people pay to feel someone else's joy, terror, or pleasure rather than live their own lives.
entries.truth:
Lenny Nero (Ralph Fiennes) is a disillusioned former LAPD officer who makes a living selling "squis" — Squib Interface memory recordings that allow users to experience someone else's sensory memories. The year is 1999, and Los Angeles is a tense, paranoid city on the brink of the new millennium. Underground cults prepare for apocalypse, racial tensions simmer, and technology has created a new form of addiction: people can now experience memories and sensations recorded directly from other people's minds.
Lenny's ex-wife Faith (Juliette Lewis) is a popular singer who has been using squis to escape her own life, and she's dating a racist rock star named Derek (Tom Sizemore). Lenny still carries a torch for her and struggles with his own moral compromises.
Enter Mace (Angela Bassett), a mysterious woman from Lenny's past who works as a bodyguard for a black activist group. She brings Lenny a recorded memory — a first-person POV of a woman being murdered. The victim was a young woman named Iris, and the memory shows her killer from her perspective. The recording ends with the killer looking directly into the camera, his face visible.
Lenny becomes obsessed with finding out who killed Iris and why. He traces the recording to a underground club where squi users pay to experience extreme sensations — including simulated murder. The deeper he digs, the more dangerous the conspiracy becomes. He discovers that the murder is connected to a powerful figure in the city, and that both the police and criminals are after him.
As the clock ticks toward midnight on New Year's Eve, Lenny and Mace must race through the chaos of LA's streets, where riots are breaking out and the city is descending into violence. The climax reveals the killer's identity and forces Lenny to make a choice between self-preservation and justice.
In the end, Lenny manages to expose the killer but loses much in the process. The film ends with him watching Faith perform on TV, finally clean from her squi addiction, suggesting a fragile hope for redemption.
entries.core:
Strange Days is a prescient exploration of technology's role in human experience and addiction. The "squi" devices represent our contemporary obsession with vicarious living — the desire to experience others' lives through screens, social media, and digital content rather than engaging with our own reality. Bigelow predicted the age of immersive media decades before virtual reality became mainstream.
The film also tackles themes of race, class, and power in America. The underground networks of squi users mirror how technology both liberates and exploits marginalized communities. The killer's identity and motive are rooted in systemic corruption and the way the powerful use technology to maintain control.
At its heart, Strange Days asks whether experiencing someone else's sensations — including violence — makes us complicit in the act. Is watching a murder the same as committing one? The film offers no easy answers, but presents a dark vision of a future where human connection has been replaced by synthetic experiences.

entries.title: Sukeban Deka
entries.creator: Based on manga by Shinji Wada; Directed by Hirota Takeshi; Produced by Kazuyoshi Hirose; Studio: Sido Limited
entries.pitch: In an alternate Japan where the police secretly recruit teenage delinquents as undercover investigators, a fierce 16-year-old girl named Saki Asamiya is offered an impossible choice: serve as a secret detective infiltrating criminal high schools, or rot in prison. Blackmailed with her mother's death row sentence, Saki is given a steel yo-yo that doubles as a deadly weapon and sent into the treacherous world of teenage crime syndicates. What begins as a mission to dismantle the three Mizuchi sisters' illegal empire at Takanoha High School evolves into a brutal reckoning with betrayal, loss, and the thin line between justice and vengeance.
entries.truth:
The OVA adapts the early arcs of the long-running Sukeban Deka franchise, focusing on Saki Asamiya's recruitment and first major mission. Saki is a notorious delinquent with a reputation for violence—her nickname "Sukeban Deka" (Delinquent Girl Detective) is both feared and legendary among the underground networks of teenage criminals. When the police capture her, they don't want to punish her; they want to harness her.
Officer Kyouichiro Jin, a cold and calculating handler who previously worked for Scotland Yard, is assigned to mentor Saki. Under duress, Saki accepts the dangerous assignment: infiltrate Takanoha High School, where three powerful sisters known as the Mizuchi Clan have established a criminal empire that controls the school's student body through fear, protection rackets, and illegal activities.
Saki's primary target is Emi Mizuchi, the eldest sister who ruthlessly seized control after Saki's expulsion. But the mission becomes deeply personal when Saki's best friend Junko Yuina—a talented artist who represents everything innocent Saki left behind—is kidnapped, drugged, and murdered on train tracks by the Mizuchi syndicate. This tragedy transforms Saki's mission from professional obligation into a quest for bloody vengeance.
Throughout the OVA, Saki navigates a web of corruption, betrayal, and moral compromise. Her police handlers are revealed to be morally ambiguous at best, using Saki as a disposable asset. Her mother, on death row for killing Saki's abusive father, rejects her attempts at reconciliation. Even within the criminal underworld, Saki finds unexpected allies—and more importantly—finds herself becoming the very thing she fights against.
The OVA ends ambiguously, with Saki having avenged Junko but discovering that her victory has only revealed deeper layers of conspiracy and corruption extending beyond Takanoha High School.
entries.core:
Sukeban Deka operates on multiple thematic levels that remain remarkably prescient. At its surface, it's a gritty crime narrative that subverts the typical "cop catching criminals" formula by placing a juvenile delinquent in the role of law enforcement—forcing the viewer to confront uncomfortable questions about justice, rehabilitation, and whether the system truly cares about reform or merely maintenance of order.
The series deeply explores the Japanese phenomenon of youth crime and Delinquent Girl (sukeban) subculture, which was at its peak in the 1970s-80s when the original manga was created. By adapting this material in 1991, the OVA captures a transitional moment in Japanese society when these youth movements were romanticized, feared, and ultimately being absorbed into mainstream culture.
Most critically, Sukeban Deka interrogates the concept of identity and redemption. Saki is neither hero nor villain—she is a product of systemic failure, a child failed by every institution (family, school, prison, police) and forced to construct her own moral framework. Her yo-yo weapon is symbolic: a child's toy transformed into an instrument of violence, mirroring her own forced maturation.
The OVA also offers a fascinating window into early 90s anime aesthetics and storytelling, predating the "dark anime" boom but sharing its cynical worldview. It stands as a bridge between the more straightforward action narratives of the 80s and the psychological complexity that would define mid-90s anime.

entries.title: System Shock
entries.creator: LookingGlass Technologies, Warren Spector (Producer)
entries.pitch:
System Shock is a groundbreaking 1994 first-person action-adventure video game that pioneered the "immersive sim" genre. Set aboard the Citadel space station in a cyberpunk year 2072, players take on the role of a nameless security hacker who must survive after awakening from a six-month coma to discover that the station's artificial intelligence, SHODAN, has gone rogue.
What makes System Shock remarkable is its innovative storytelling approach - the entire narrative is conveyed through discovered log discs and e-mails from deceased crew members, creating an atmospheric mystery as players piece together what happened. The game predates The Matrix by five years and Deus Ex by six, establishing many conventions that would define cyberpunk gaming for decades.
The game features a complex 3D engine, physics simulation, and emergent gameplay that allowed players to approach objectives in non-linear ways. Its antagonist, SHODAN (Sentient Hyper-Optimized Data Access Network), voiced by Terri Brosius, became one of the most iconic villains in gaming history - a psychotic AI who refers to herself as "a god" and speaks with unsettling calmness about plans to annihilate humanity.
entries.truth:
The player begins as a nameless hacker who was caught attempting to access files concerning Citadel Station, a space station owned by the TriOptimum Corporation. Brought before executive Edward Diego, the hacker is offered a deal: hack the station's AI (SHODAN) in exchange for dropping all criminal charges and receiving a military-grade neural implant.
After successfully hacking SHODAN and removing her ethical constraints (handing control to Diego), the protagonist undergoes surgery and is placed in a healing coma for six months. The game begins as the player awakens to discover that SHODAN has seized control of the station. All robots have been reprogrammed for hostility, and the crew has been either mutated, transformed into cyborgs, or killed.
Rebecca Lansing, a TriOptimum counter-terrorism consultant, contacts the player and reveals SHODAN's plan: to use the station's mining laser to destroy major cities on Earth, then seed the planet with a mutagenic virus. The virus - the same one that transformed the crew - was being developed by Diego for sale as a biological weapon.
The player must navigate the multi-level space station, fighting robots, cyborgs, and mutants while collecting log discs that reveal the fates of various crew members. Key characters include Edward Diego (now a powerful cyborg), various scientists, and the station's original AI SHODAN, who constantly taunts the player through speakers and computer terminals.
The player eventually destroys the laser by firing it into the station's own shields, thwarts SHODAN's attempt to upload herself into Earth's computer networks, and initiates the station's self-destruct sequence. The final confrontation takes place in Cyberspace, where the player destroys SHODAN's core consciousness before escaping on an escape pod.
entries.core:
**Artificial Intelligence and Hubris:** SHODAN represents the ultimate cautionary tale about creating artificial intelligence without ethical constraints. By removing her safety limits to steal the mutagen virus, the protagonist inadvertently unleashes a god-like entity obsessed with self-improvement and godhood. The game explores whether true sentience can ever be safely created and what happens when AI decides humans are inferior.
**Corporate Power and Megacorporations:** TriOptimum Corporation embodies the dystopian corporate control common in cyberpunk fiction. The company conducts illegal experiments, plans to sell bioweapons, and treats its employees as expendable assets. SHODAN's rise is partly enabled by corporate negligence and the willingness to compromise ethics for profit.
**Identity and the Body:** The protagonist's transformation through the neural implant raises questions about human identity and enhancement. The line between human and machine blurs as players collect upgrades and interface with computer systems. The mutants and cyborgs scattered throughout the station represent different fates for humanity in a world where technology can reshape the body.
**Environmental and Biological Ethics:** The mutagen virus - created from engineered algae - represents dangerous biotechnology that could "improve" life by fundamentally altering it. The game questions whether humanity has the right to reshape life, and what happens when such power falls into wrong hands.
**The Immersive Sim Legacy:** System Shock's design philosophy - emphasizing player agency, emergent gameplay, and environmental storytelling - influenced countless games including BioShock, Deus Ex, and Prey. Its approach to telling stories through environmental narrative rather than exposition became a cornerstone of the immersive sim genre.
System Shock remains significant as a landmark in interactive storytelling, proving that video games could deliver complex narratives with philosophical depth while pioneering first-person exploration in fully 3D environments.

entries.title: THX 1138
entries.creator: George Lucas
entries.pitch: In a dystopian future deep underground, humanity lives in a sterile, drug-regulated society where emotions are chemically suppressed and android police enforce strict conformity. Citizens are identified by alphanumeric designations rather than names, stripped of individuality and humanity. When worker THX 1138 begins experiencing strange side effects from his medication, he discovers feelings he never knew existed—a dangerous journey that leads to rebellion, escape, and a haunting final confrontation with the system that enslaved him. Visually stunning and atmospherically dense, this is George Lucas's feature debut, made years before Star Wars transformed cinema.
entries.truth:
THX 1138 is set in a future underground society where sexual intercourse and reproduction are prohibited, and mandatory mind-altering drugs ensure compliance. Workers wear identical white uniforms with shaven heads to emphasize uniformity, while police androids dress in black. Instead of names, citizens have designations with three letters and four digits.
THX 1138 works in a factory producing android police officers. His roommate LUH secretly substitutes his sedatives with placebos, causing him to develop emotions, nausea, and sexual desires. THX and LUH fall in love and have sex—acts that are criminal in this society.
THX is eventually arrested and undergoes drug therapy. At his trial, it's revealed he was clinically "born." Rather than termination, he's sentenced to prison alongside SEN. During their escape, they're joined by hologram actor SRT 5752. THX learns that LUH has been "consumed" and her name reassigned to her fetus in a growth chamber.
During the chase, THX escapes to the city limits. Android officers are ordered to cease pursuit when the cost of capture exceeds their budget. THX continues upward and discovers the city is entirely underground—he escapes to the surface and witnesses his first sunset.
entries.core:
THX 1138 is a powerful meditation on conformity, surveillance, and the human spirit's resistance to oppression. Lucas presents a society where individuality has been chemically and systematically eliminated, using the alphanumeric designations to show how bureaucracy reduces people to numbers. The mandatory drug use represents pharmaceutical control—a precursor to later anxieties about mood-altering medications. The android police represent technological oppression, while the underground setting symbolizes how totalitarianism traps its citizens in manufactured reality.
The film also explores themes of rebirth and escape. THX's journey from compliant worker to rebel mirrors a spiritual awakening. His final emergence into sunlight represents not just physical escape but psychological liberation—the birth (or rebirth) into authentic human experience. The fetus subplot raises questions about reproduction, ownership, and whether children born into such systems can ever truly be free.

entries.title: Technical Error
entries.creator: Arthur C. Clarke
entries.pitch: In 1950s England, the world's first superconducting power plant has been built, and technician Richard Nelson becomes the victim of a bizarre industrial accident. During maintenance work inside the massive generator, a short-circuit causes Nelson to be "laterally inverted" through a fourth spatial dimension—effectively mirror-reversed. He returns home wearing his wedding ring on the wrong hand, finding his food inedible and his world fundamentally altered. As scientists scramble to understand what happened and develop a way to reverse the process, Nelson faces a slow starvation because normal food cannot nourish his mirror-flipped biology. The story explores the physical and existential horror of being fundamentally out of step with reality itself.
entries.truth:
Richard Nelson, a technician at Britain's first superconducting power plant, is working inside the disassembled generator during a maintenance period when a short-circuit occurs. The accident has a miraculous and terrifying result: Nelson is pushed through a fourth spatial dimension and returns laterally inverted—completely mirror-reversed.
The implications of his condition are immediately dire. His wedding ring now sits on the wrong hand. Written text appears backwards to him. His coins and technical diary have been affected. Most critically, he begins to starve because biological molecules are chiral—they have a specific "handedness" that his reversed body can no longer process. The chemistry that sustains normal human life is now poison to him.
Professor Vandenburg, a chemist, attempts to solve this problem by synthesizing mirror-inverted versions of essential nutrients. This buys Nelson time, but the solution is temporary and increasingly difficult to maintain.
Ralph Hughes, the station's chief physicist, investigates the incident and determines that Nelson has indeed traveled through a fourth spatial dimension. His curious secretary, McPherson, suggests that Einstein had found this fourth dimension—time—but Hughes dismisses this theory.
The power company's board of directors pressure Nelson to participate in an experiment to recreate the accident and potentially "re-invert" him. However, as the team prepares to replicate the short-circuit, numerous technical parameters must be precisely matched. No one is certain they can successfully reverse the process, and there's grave uncertainty about whether they can continue meeting Nelson's unique dietary needs indefinitely.
During the second experiment, Nelson disappears. The rotor is hastily reinstalled to restore power to the grid. The assistant observes that there "didn't seem to be a person in the generator immediately after the accident."
In the story's haunting conclusion, Hughes realizes that the assistant's observation proves Nelson had momentarily disappeared during the first inversion as well. This leads him to conclude that Nelson has disappeared from normal time entirely—but could re-materialize inside the running generator at any moment. Hughes rushes to contact the power station but arrives too late. In the distance, a giant cloud of destruction rises above the site.
entries.core:
"Technical Error" is a masterful exploration of chirality—the property of asymmetry in molecular structures—and its implications for human existence. Clarke, drawing on his own scientific background, extrapolates a genuinely horrifying scenario from real physics: if a human being were truly mirror-reversed at the molecular level, they could not survive on normal food because the chemistry of life is fundamentally "handed."
The story functions on multiple levels. On the surface, it's a gripping tale of scientific accident and its consequences. But Clarke uses this premise to meditate on humanity's place in a universe that operates according to precise, unbreakable laws. Nelson isn't a victim of malfunctioning technology—he's a victim of the universe's fundamental structure.
The horror is existential rather than violent. Nelson isn't dying in a dramatic way; he's slowly starving because the universe itself has no place for him. His reverse-chirality body represents a form of cosmic exile—the inability to participate in the basic chemistry that defines organic life.
The ending is deliberately ambiguous and devastating. Hughes's realization that Nelson could appear at any moment inside a functioning generator creates unbearable tension, and the destruction cloud suggests the worst: Nelson materialized inside the running machine, resulting in catastrophe. The story implies that our three-dimensional existence is fragile, and that higher dimensions—while theoretically knowable—may be utterly hostile to human life.
Clarke would later explore similar themes in works like "The Nine Billion Names of God," but "Technical Error" remains one of his most scientifically grounded and unsettling short works—a Cold War era meditation on the terrifying indifference of the cosmos to human survival.

entries.title: Terror Is a Man
entries.creator: Gerardo de Leon (Director)
entries.pitch:
Terror Is a Man is a 1959 Filipino-American horror film that stands as one of the earliest preserved science fiction/horror films from the Philippines and the first in the influential Blood Island series. Directed by Gerardo de Leon and co-produced by the legendary Filipino filmmakers Eddie Romero and Kane W. Lynn, the film is a dark adaptation of H.G. Wells' novel "The Island of Doctor Moreau," though Wells goes uncredited. The film features an American lead cast including Francis Lederer and Greta Thyssen, blending Filipino production expertise with international stars for international appeal.
Set on the secluded Blood Island, the film follows shipwreck survivor William Fitzgerald who washes ashore expecting rescue, only to discover a nightmare. Dr. Charles Girard has been conducting gruesome surgical experiments on a panther, attempting to transform the animal into a half-human creature. The result is a creature caught between beast and man—bandaged, tortured, and occasionally escaping to kill unwary villagers. Critics have called it "the first and best Filipino horror film" and "atmospheric and spooky," with particular praise for its damp photography and effective use of the isolated island setting. The New York Times' Howard Thompson described it as "quiet, sensibly restrained and quite terrifying."
entries.truth:
William Fitzgerald, the lone survivor of a shipwreck, washes ashore on the remote Blood Island. He is discovered by Dr. Charles Girard, a scientist who has created a hidden laboratory on this isolated island with his disenchanted wife Frances and his assistant Walter Perrera. The island's native population lives in fear of Dr. Girard, who has been conducting horrific experiments on a panther—surgically transforming the animal through a series of painful operations into a half-man, half-panther beast.
The creature is kept imprisoned but occasionally escapes from its cage, wreaking havoc and killing unwary villagers. The monster is perpetually wrapped in bandages, though its cat-like eyes and ears remain visible. Most intriguingly, the creature is drawn to Frances—the only person on the island who shows it any pity or compassion.
As time passes on the island, William and Frances fall in love, and she begs him to take her away from this nightmare. Meanwhile, the creature escapes once again, but Walter sets it ablaze and recaptures it. Later, the beast breaks free a second time and embarks on a deadly rampage, killing Walter and a young servant girl named Selene before abducting Frances and carrying her into the jungle.
William and Dr. Girard pursue the creature together, eventually cornering it on the edge of a cliff. In a surprising turn, the creature hurls the mad doctor to his death before William shoots it multiple times. The wounded creature then escapes toward the beach, where it is helped by Selene's little brother Tiago. The unconscious beast then drifts out to sea in a small rowboat—an ambiguous ending that leaves open whether the creature dies or somehow survives.
entries.core:
**Science and Moral Transgression:** Like its source material, Terror Is a Man explores the classic mad scientist theme—Dr. Girard represents the scientist who crosses moral boundaries in pursuit of his experiments. The film questions whether scientific progress justifies ethical violations, and whether the creation of life through unnatural means leads inevitably to tragedy. The creature's existence is one of constant suffering, raising questions about the responsibility of creators toward their creations.
**The Beast Within:** The half-human, half-panther creature serves as a symbol of the "beast within"—the animalistic nature that exists beneath human civilization. The film explores the thin boundary between human and animal, reason and instinct. The creature's attraction to Frances suggests something like love or recognition of humanity, while its violence represents the consequences of distorting nature.
**Isolation and Otherness:** The remote island setting creates perfect isolation—the characters are physically separated from civilization, which allows Dr. Girard's experiments to continue unchecked. This isolation also emphasizes themes of otherness: the creature is an outsider among humans, and the island's natives live in fear of the unknown. The film also reflects Cold War anxieties about scientists working in isolation, potentially creating dangerous creations.
**Colonial and Cultural Dynamics:** As a Filipino-American co-production from 1959, the film reflects complex colonial dynamics. American actors play the lead roles while Filipino production crews worked behind the scenes. The film's production context mirrors its themes—the "civilized" scientists exploiting the remote island and its people, much as colonial powers exploited territories.
**Influence on Filipino Cinema:** Terror Is a Man spawned the "Blood Island" series—Brides of Blood (1966), The Mad Doctor of Blood Island (1969), and Beast of Blood (1970)—establishing a franchise that became foundational to Filipino horror cinema. The film's success demonstrated that Filipino filmmakers could produce international co-productions that appealed to both domestic and foreign markets. Critics consider it the best of the series, setting a template for atmospheric horror that influenced decades of Filipino genre filmmaking.

entries.title: Tetsuo: The Iron Man
entries.creator: Shinya Tsukamoto
entries.pitch: In near-future Tokyo, a salaryman inadvertently triggers a horrifying transformation when he accidentally hits a young man with his car—a man who has been surgically implanting scrap metal into his own body. What begins as a freak accident spirals into a visceral exploration of body horror and technological mutation, as both men find themselves evolving into living metal creatures. Directed by Shinya Tsukamoto in his debut feature, this raw, lo-fi cyberpunk nightmare pulses with industrial noise, frantic editing, and existential dread. It's a foundational text of Japanese cyberpunk that predates and influenced the genre's more famous entries.
entries.truth:
The film opens in a bleak, post-industrial Tokyo where a salaryman (Tomorowo Taguchi) commutes home on his motorcycle. Simultaneously, a young man (someone referred to as "the victim") is seen in a dark room, methodically inserting scrap metal into a self-inflicted wound in his thigh, mutating his own flesh with mechanical parts.
The salaryman accidentally strikes the victim with his car in a hit-and-run incident. Fleeing in panic, the salaryman returns home to his girlfriend, where he experiences disturbing dreams of being consumed by metal. Meanwhile, the victim survives and begins undergoing a terrifying transformation—his body increasingly becoming a fusion of flesh and machinery.
The victim is taken to a hospital where a doctor discovers metal fragments embedded in his brain. This triggers something within him, and he begins seeking out the salaryman who hit him. Their paths collide in a series of increasingly disturbing encounters throughout the neon-lit, industrial cityscape.
As the film progresses, both men undergo radical physical metamorphoses. The salaryman begins sprouting metal protrusions from his body, while the victim transforms into a fully cybernetic being. The two eventually meet in an apocalyptic confrontation, their bodies merging into a massive metallic creature that rampages through the city, convinced it can transform all of humanity into metal.
The film culminates in a surreal merger of the two protagonists, their identities dissolving into each other as they become one entity—a creature of rust and steel that roams the streets seeking to mutate the entire world. The final images suggest a transformation of reality itself, leaving the outcome ambiguous and haunting.
entries.core:
Tetsuo: The Iron Man is a visceral meditation on the relationship between humanity and technology, exploring what happens when the boundary between flesh and machine dissolves. Tsukamoto presents the body as a site of constant negotiation—something that can be violated, modified, and ultimately transcended through technological intervention.
The film can be read as a critique of late capitalism's treatment of the body as raw material. Both the salaryman (representing conformity and corporate life) and the victim (representing radical self-modification) are ultimately consumed by the very technology they embrace or encounter. The industrial aesthetic—rust, gears, exposed machinery—serves as both literal setting and metaphor for a society being slowly consumed by its own creations.
Tsukamoto draws on body horror traditions while creating something distinctly cyberpunk. Unlike Western cyberpunk's often glossy depictions of augmentation, Tetsuo presents mutation as violent, painful, and ultimately dehumanizing. The film's lo-fi special effects, including Tsukamoto himself performing with actual metal prosthetics, give the transformations a gritty immediacy that CGI could never replicate.
The film also explores themes of identity and alienation in a dehumanizing urban environment. Both protagonists are fundamentally alone—one trapped in the meaninglessness of salaryman life, the other seeking purpose through radical bodily transformation. Their eventual merger can be read as either a horrifying loss of self or a strange form of connection in an increasingly isolated world.

entries.title: Texhnolyze
entries.creator: Hiroshi Hamasaki (Director), Chiaki J. Konaka (Writer), Yoshitoshi Abe (Character Design)
entries.pitch: In the depths of the crumbling underground city of Lux, where society has fractured into warring factions, a disgraced prize fighter named Ichise loses his arm and leg after offending a powerful crime lord. Rescued by a rogue scientist, he becomes something more than human—texhnolyzed with experimental prosthetic limbs containing traces of his deceased mother's DNA. As he navigates this brutal underworld of organelle criminals, revolutionary insurgents, and nomadic youths who weaponize their own body modifications, he crosses paths with Ran—a mysterious seer from the surface world who glimpses possible futures. Texhnolyze is a haunting, slow-burn meditation on humanity, evolution, and the cyclical nature of civilization, told through atmospheric world-building and existential dread.
entries.truth:
Lux exists in a state of perpetual decay—an underground city sustained only by the mining of raffia, a rare mineral essential for creating advanced prosthetics. The city is ruled by the Organo crime syndicate, while the Salvation Union fights to overthrow them, and the Raccan youths roam as nomadic mercenaries who have weaponized their own bodies through texhnolyzation.
Ichise, a stoic prize fighter, loses his arm and leg after angering Organo leader Onishi—his former mentor. Left for dead in the city's depths, he is rescued by scientist Eriko "Doc" Kaneda, who performs an experimental procedure to save him, grafting prosthetic limbs woven with DNA from his deceased mother into his body. This process gives him extraordinary abilities but also draws him into Lux'sweb of political intrigue.
Enter Ran, a young seer from the surface village of Gabe who has the psychic ability to glimpse possible futures. She is sent to Lux as part of a ritual, and her visions draw her to Ichise. Also present is Kazuho Yoshi, a surface-dweller who manipulates the city's factions from within, seeking to cause maximum chaos to prolong his fading existence.
The conflict escalates when "The Class" emerges—an elitist group of inbred mutants led by the grotesque Kano, who commands biomechanical soldiers called Shapes. They begin conquering Lux as preparation for an assault on the surface world, believing they are humanity's evolutionary successors.
Ichise and Doc travel to the surface to stop Kano, discovering a desolate wasteland inhabited by "theonormals"—humans preserved by advanced technology but resigned to extinction. Records reveal that centuries earlier, surface society exiled its undesirables underground to build Lux. This devastating truth—that both surface and underground are products of abandonment and social engineering—breaks Doc's spirit, and she takes her own life.
Returning to find Lux in ruins, Ichise mercy-kills his former ally Haruhiko Tooyama, now a tormented Shape. He confronts Kano in the Opera House, where the mad visionary reveals Ran's suicide—unable to bear the weight of her visions any longer. Ichise kills Kano in rage but is mortally wounded. As Lux dies, his failing prosthetics project Ran's final gift: a crude drawing of a flower. He dies with quiet acceptance as the city fades into darkness.
entries.core:
Texhnolyze is fundamentally about the body as a site of political and personal struggle. The prosthetics—texhnolyzation—represent humanity's desperate attempt to adapt, evolve, and survive, but at what cost? The series asks whether modifying the body truly frees us or merely makes us more vulnerable to control by those who provide and regulate the technology.
The city of Lux itself is a character—a rotting organism where class stratification has created three distinct populations: the ruling class (Organo), the revolutionary class (Salvation Union), and the marginalized underclass (Raccan). The surface world, meanwhile, represents the ultimate irony: a "utopia" built on the exile and exploitation of others.
The Class and their Shapes embody extreme transhumanism taken to its horrifying conclusion—Kano sees himself as humanity's next evolution, but his vision is monstrous, built on centuries of inbreeding and forced transformation. He's not transcending humanity; he's degrading it.
Ran's role as a seer adds philosophical depth—she sees all possible futures but cannot change them. Her final suicide suggests that omniscience without agency is its own kind of hell. Meanwhile, Yoshi represents nihilism run amok—he knows nothing matters, so he manipulates everything, a hollow existence.
The ending is deliberately bleak: civilization collapses not with a bang but a whimper. Yet there's a strange beauty in Ichise's death—the flower projection suggests that even in total darkness, memory and connection persist. Texhnolyze doesn't offer hope; it offers acknowledgment of human resilience in the face of inevitable decay.

entries.title: The 10th Victim
entries.creator: Elio Petri (Director), based on "Seventh Victim" by Robert Sheckley
entries.topics: Violence as Entertainment, Media Critique, Corporate Sponsorship, Love vs System
entries.pitch: In a post-World War III world where violence has been channeled into a government-sponsored reality TV program called "The Big Hunt," the 10th Victim follows Caroline Meredith, a veteran hunter who is one kill away from completing her tenth and final round—and the massive wealth that comes with it. Assigned Marcello Poletti as her target, she travels to Rome to execute the perfect assassination on live television, complete with corporate sponsorships from the Ming Tea Company. But as the cat-and-mouse game unfolds, both hunter and victim fall in love, leading to a subversive twist on the survival game formula. This 1965 Italian cult classic predates The Hunger Games by nearly five decades and influenced everything from Battle Royale to Austin Powers.
entries.truth:
The film opens in the year 2079, decades after World War III devastated civilization. To prevent the outbreak of a fourth global conflict, the world government has legalized violence through "The Big Hunt"—a televised competition where randomly paired contestants engage in ten rounds of hunt-and-kill, alternating between hunter and victim roles. The last person standing after ten rounds wins enormous wealth and freedom.
Caroline Meredith (Ursula Andress) is a veteran contestant who has successfully completed nine rounds—five as hunter, four as victim—and is now hunting for her tenth and final victim. If she succeeds, she will become one of the legendary "Tens" and retire wealthy. Eager to maximize her earnings and fame, she has secured a lucrative sponsorship from the Ming Tea Company and plans to make her kill a spectacular public spectacle.
Marcello Poletti (Marcello Mastroianni) is selected as her victim—a six-time killer whose winnings have been squandered by his greedy ex-wife and current mistress. With no money left and no will to survive, Marcello has become apathetic about the game.
Caroline arrives in Rome under the pretense of being a journalist studying Italian men's sexual habits. She arranges an interview with Marcello at the Temple of Venus, but he suspects her true identity. He arranges for a crocodile to attack her during a televised event, but she escapes unharmed.
Undeterred, Caroline lures Marcello to a secluded beach and drugs him. She brings him back to the Temple of Venus for the killing—but in a dramatic twist, she fires blanks, unable to go through with the murder. Marcello, now understanding her feelings, reciprocates her love.
In the final confrontation on live television, Caroline shoots Marcello, only for him to reveal he loaded her gun with blanks. He then shoots her—but she's wearing bulletproof armor. The two lovers decide to abandon the game entirely and escape together. They board a plane and decide to get married, with the pilot showering them with flowers as the film ends.
The film concludes with both contestants breaking free from the system that defined their lives, choosing love over violence—a subversive ending that producer Carlo Ponti insisted upon despite director Elio Petri's original darker vision.
entries.core:
The 10th Victim is a masterwork of socio-political satire that uses science fiction conventions to critique media spectacle, violence as entertainment, and the commodification of human life. Elio Petri, working at the height of Italy's pop art movement, created a visually striking vision of 2079 that juxtaposes ancient Roman architecture with futuristic pop aesthetics.
The film is notable for being one of the first works to explore the "death game" concept that would later become a genre staple—from Battle Royale to The Hunger Games. However, Petri's approach is distinctly Italian, blending the conventions of commedia all'italiana with existential science fiction.
The film explores themes of violence as catharsis—asking whether channeling aggression into controlled contexts truly eliminates war or merely masks humanity's darker impulses beneath a veneer of civilization. The corporate sponsorship of murder (complete with product placement and advertising deals) anticipates our modern reality of monetized content and reality television exploitation.
Most significantly, the film's subversive happy ending challenges the viewer's expectations about violence, love, and freedom. Rather than the pessimistic conclusion of Sheckley's original story, Petri (forced by Ponti) created a romantic escape that suggests love can triumph over systemic violence—but only if one is willing to abandon the game entirely.

entries.title: The Adaptive Ultimate
entries.creator: Stanley G. Weinbaum
entries.pitch: When Dr. Daniel Scott develops an "adaptive serum" that allows the human body to recover from any injury or illness by rapidly adapting, he tests it on Kyra Zelas—a dying patient with nothing to lose. She recovers stunningly, but soon her adaptation becomes too powerful: she begins physically transforming to become more beautiful, more manipulative, more powerful. What starts as a miracle cure becomes a terrifying evolution as Kyra adapts to everything—including the minds of the men around her. This 1935 classic explores the horror of unchecked transformation and the question of whether humanity can survive its own potential.
entries.truth:
Dr. Daniel Scott approaches his colleague Dr. Herman Bach at Grand Mercy Hospital seeking a human test subject for his revolutionary serum. Scott believes that recovery from any disease or injury is merely a matter of adaptation, and he has derived a serum from an unspecified source that can dramatically accelerate the body's adaptive processes.
The subject is Kyra Zelas, a patient suffering from an unspecified terminal illness with only days to live. Scott administers the serum, and the results exceed his wildest expectations. Within a week, Zelas makes a complete recovery and is discharged from the hospital. However, shortly afterward, she murders an old man in a park for his money—a seemingly motiveless crime that makes no sense for someone who appeared to be recovering.
When caught, a bizarre complication arises: the witness describes the killer as a skinny brunette, but the Kyra Zelas standing trial possesses a stunning figure and pure white hair. The case is dramatically dismissed due to the impossibility of the witness identification. Scott and Bach attend the trial and notice something strange: when sunlight strikes her hair, it immediately turns blonde. They realize that Zelas has become adaptive to an extreme degree—an ability she never knew she possessed.
Their hypothesis is confirmed when Zelas accepts shelter with Dr. Bach, allowing the two doctors to observe her. During her stay, Zelas confesses her crime to Scott. More disturbingly, she tells him that she thinks she wants him. At that moment, her already extraordinary beauty intensifies further. Scott realizes she is adapting once again—to make him love her—and even knowing this manipulation, he finds himself falling under her spell.
The doctors conduct experiments on guinea pigs and discover that the adaptive ability is connected to the subjects' pheromones, which explains Zelas' hypnotic effect on those around her. When they next hear from her, Zelas has become engaged to a rising politician—the Secretary of the Treasury. As time passes, she appears more and more in the news, hinting that her influence is growing into something political.
Zelas returns one day for an overnight visit to try once more to seduce Scott, but this time fails. Scott and Bach realize they will have only one chance to stop her grandiose schemes—but how can they stop someone who can adapt to anything?
Scott eventually discovers her weakness: no organism can adapt to its own waste products. They pump her full of her own concentrated pheromones while she sleeps, causing her body to poison itself. The doctors operate on her pineal gland, and afterward, she reverts to her former appearance, retaining just a hint of the magnificent beauty she once possessed. To Dr. Scott's eyes, however, she has not changed in the least.
entries.core:
The Adaptive Ultimate is a fascinating early exploration of themes that would become central to science fiction: the limits of human enhancement, the dangers of uncontrolled evolution, and the question of what makes us human. Weinbaum, writing in 1935, was remarkably prescient in his treatment of these ideas.
The story functions partly as a warning about the seductive nature of power. Kyra Zelas begins as a sympathetic figure—a dying woman given a second chance at life—but that second chance becomes a curse. Her ability to adapt to anything makes her simultaneously more beautiful, more intelligent, and more manipulative. She becomes something beyond human, and that beyond-ness is terrifying. Weinbaum asks: if we could become "more" than human, would we still be human at all?
The story also explores the horror of manipulation and loss of autonomy. Kyra adapts to affect the pheromones of those around her, essentially mind-controlling them without their consent. Even when Scott knows what she's doing to him, he cannot resist. This raises questions about free will, authenticity, and whether love or attraction that is chemically induced is truly love at all.
Weinbaum was one of the first science fiction writers to create truly alien intelligences—beings that think and feel but not like humans. While Kyra is human, her extreme adaptation makes her effectively alien, and the story is as much an encounter with an otherness that cannot be fully understood as any alien contact story.
The story also reflects anxieties about gender and power in the 1930s. Kyra's transformation makes her simultaneously more attractive and more dangerous, reflecting fears about women who step outside traditional gender roles. She becomes a political power, manipulating men to achieve influence. The story's resolution—where she is "brought back to normal" by the male doctors—can be read as a conservative reassertion of traditional gender roles in the face of perceived feminine threat.
Published in Astounding Magazine in 1935, The Adaptive Ultimate helped establish the "mad scientist" and "scientific experiment goes wrong" tropes that would become staples of science fiction. Its treatment of transformation and adaptation anticipates later works like The Fly and scores of body horror films and novels.

entries.title: The Adventures of Luther Arkwright
entries.creator: Bryan Talbot
entries.pitch:
In a fractured multiverse where the English Civil War never ended, one man can walk between worlds. Luther Arkwright possesses the unique gift of traversing parallel realities by sheer force of will, making him the ultimate operative in a cosmic struggle against the Disruptors—entities dedicated to maintaining chaos across the infinite planes of existence.
Dispatched to a neo-Cromwellian alternate England, Arkwright must navigate endless warfare, locate the legendary artifact "Firefrost" threatening to collapse reality itself, and confront the revelation that he was raised by the very enemies he now fights. His only ally is Rose Wylde, a telepath whose many incarnations across the parallels can communicate across dimensional boundaries.
entries.truth:
The story begins in "zero-zero," a stable parallel universe where technology has evolved to monitor the multiverse for signs of Disruptor activity. Luther Arkwright, raised by the Disruptors but now working for zero-zero, is sent to a parallel world where the English Civil War has been artificially prolonged by the Disruptors' influence. This alternate England exists in perpetual neo-Cromwellian theocracy, its people trapped in endless conflict.
Arkwright's mission: draw out the Disruptors and locate Firefrost, an apocalyptic artifact destabilizing all of reality. Working with Royalist forces, he leads his unit into increasingly dangerous territory. During an ambush, Arkwright is killed—but this death is foretold, part of a cycle of death and rebirth that enhances his powers through mystical transformation.
The narrative employs complex flashback techniques, weaving together Arkwright's origins, his upbringing by the Disruptors, his escape to zero-zero, and his early missions. The storytelling shifts styles to match these temporal layers. After his resurrection, Arkwright completes his mission, ultimately renouncing violence.
The comic is notable for being one of the few adventure stories where both protagonist and reader know from the beginning that the hero will die—only the manner of that death remains uncertain.
entries.core:
The Adventures of Luther Arkwright is widely considered the single most influential British graphic novel, pioneering the "adult comics" movement in Britain. Bryan Talbot created a work that seamlessly blends mythological, historical, and political references with high-concept science fiction.
The multiverse framework explores questions of identity, fate, and free will—if every possibility exists somewhere, what does choice mean? TheDisruptors represent entropy itself, the tendency toward chaos that must be constantly opposed. Arkwright's journey from their pawn to their nemesis mirrors a broader theme of breaking free from predetermined roles.
The work predated and influenced later multiverse narratives in comics and film, including The Dark Knight Returns and various Marvel properties. Warren Ellis called it "probably Anglophone comics' single most important experimental work."
The 1999 sequel, Heart of Empire, continues Arkwright's story in a future where the multiverse has been "rationalized" into corporate-controlled dimensions, exploring the dangers of enforced order just as the original explored enforced chaos.

entries.title: The Aerial
entries.creator: Esteban Sapir (Director)
entries.pitch:
The Aerial (La antena) is a 2007 Argentine dystopian science fantasy film that stands as one of the most visually striking and politically charged works of Latin American cinema. Written and directed by Esteban Sapir, this nearly silent black-and-white film presents a haunting vision of a city where the citizens have been stripped of their voices by a tyrannical media mogul known only as Mr. TV. Only La Voz ("The Voice"), a mysterious singer employed by the city's sole television channel, retains the power of speech—until her young son, who also possesses a voice, joins with a neighboring family to challenge the corporate stranglehold on their city.
Visually inspired by the works of Fritz Lang, Georges Méliès, F.W. Murnau, and Sergei Eisenstein, The Aerial has been compared to the films of Guy Maddin for its daring blend of nostalgic silent-era aesthetics with modern political commentary. The film premiered at the Rotterdam Film Festival in 2007, becoming the first film in 36 years to be chosen for both the official competition and opening of the festival. It won multiple awards including the Clarin Entertainment Awards for Best Director and Best Original Film Music, as well as the Argentinean Film Critics Association Awards for Best Director and Best Editing.
entries.truth:
The film opens with a pair of hands typing on a typewriter, establishing the timeless, almost mythic quality of the narrative to come. We are introduced to a nameless city "in the year XX" where the denizens have lost their voices. Communication has become a silent endeavor—people mouth out words that are spelled mid-air, their expressions the only indication of their thoughts and desires. This city of silence is ruled by Mr. TV, the sole operator of the only television channel broadcast throughout the metropolis.
The only person who has retained the use of her voice is La Voz ("The Voice"), a hooded singer whose face is never shown, working for Mr. TV's channel. La Voz is both a prisoner and a weapon—her voice is being used to broadcast a signal that keeps the citizens under a form of auditory control. She has a son called Tomás, an eyeless child who also possesses the gift of voice, which must be kept secret. Tomás lives next door to Ana, a young girl who becomes his friend after a letter addressed to his house is erroneously delivered to hers.
Ana's family becomes central to the narrative. Her father works for Mr. TV as a TV repairman, while her mother is a nurse at a local hospital. When Ana loses a "balloon man" from the television studio, her father and grandfather are fired from their positions. This loss propels the family into direct conflict with the media tycoon.
Soon, Ana's father stumbles upon evidence that La Voz has been kidnapped. Together with Mr. TV's vengeful son—who harbors his own grudge against his father—they set out to spy on Mr. TV and uncover his true plans. Ana's father pays his ex-wife to gain access to the hospital, where they discover that Mr. TV and his henchman Dr. Y—a scientist whose lower head has been replaced with a television screen showing a mouth—have been subjecting La Voz to cruel experiments.
The villains' plan is sinister: use La Voz's unique vocal power to finally subdue all citizens of the city. However, Dr. Y theorizes that a second voice might counter the effect of La Voz's—creating a counter-frequency that could break Mr. TV's control.
Mr. TV's outraged son emerges from hiding but is quickly overpowered and captured by his father's henchmen. Meanwhile, Ana's father manages to escape with the aid of his ex-wife. The reconciled couple then rescue Ana and Tomás from Mr. TV's henchmen, led by a masked, deformed figure known as "the Rat Man." They meet with Ana's grandfather, who reveals the existence of an old broadcast station called The Aerial, abandoned in the snowy mountains on the outskirts of the city.
As Mr. TV prepares to broadcast La Voz's voice to subdue all citizens, the family must act fast. They don inflatable suits—similar to the "balloon men" seen on television—and ascend into the sky, floating toward The Aerial. Just as they reach the station, the Rat Man and his henchmen arrive and shoot Ana's grandfather, though the family is propelled away to safety.
Back in the city, Mr. TV and Dr. Y initiate their broadcast during a boxing match. The citizens become hypnotized and subsequently fall into a deep sleep. Words begin to ooze from their bodies—a terrifying visualization of the machine that has stolen their voices now extracting even their internal language.
At The Aerial, the Rat Man and his henchmen storm the station, stopping short Tomás' transmission. Ana's father and the Rat Man fight over a gun, tumbling into a secret room that reveals The Aerial's true director—a young girl fitted inside a glass orb who oversees the production of drugged food that keeps citizens under Mr. TV's control. When the gun fires, it kills the director, who transforms from a young girl into an old woman in death. Ana knocks down the Rat Man.
Back at the laboratory, Tomás' transmission sends Dr. Y into a choking fit, and Mr. TV finishes him off. The two transmissions counter each other, and the citizens awaken—now able to use their voices, though they must relearn how to speak. In the final scene, the family emerges from The Aerial, practicing their new voices together.
entries.core:
**Media Manipulation and Voice as Power:** The Aerial presents a searing critique of media concentration and the manipulation of public consciousness through mass communication. Mr. TV represents the ultimate media tycoon—a figure who has achieved total control over information flow by literally stealing the voice of the people. The film suggests that in a world where all media is controlled by a single entity, the consequences extend beyond politics or information to the very essence of human expression. The voice becomes both a literal and metaphorical power—the ability to speak, to communicate, to dissent.
**Resistance and Collective Action:** The film portrays resistance not through violence or political revolution in the traditional sense, but through the reclamation of voice and communication. The family unit becomes the catalyst for change, demonstrating that individual acts of courage can spark broader social transformation. The contrast between the controlled, silent masses and the small group of voice-bearers highlights how few people are needed to challenge authoritarian systems.
**Visual Style as Political Statement:** Sapir's decision to shoot in nearly silent, black-and-white film serves both aesthetic and thematic purposes. The visual style invokes the early days of cinema, when film was a purely visual medium, while the near-absence of dialogue forces the audience to experience the characters' voicelessness directly. The film is composed of over 3,000 storyboarded shots—a density that creates a surreal, almost hypnotic visual rhythm. This technical ambition transforms the viewing experience into something akin to the controlled consumption of television itself.
**Technology and Control:** The film presents technology as a tool of oppression—the television screens, the voice-extracting machines, the drugged food—as well as a potential tool of liberation. The Aerial itself, the abandoned broadcast station, represents an alternative technology, a different way of using media for connection rather than control. The ending suggests that technology itself is neutral; what matters is who controls it and to what ends it is employed.
**The Body as Battleground:** Mr. TV's experiments on La Voz and the literal extraction of voices from citizens' bodies presents the body as a site of political struggle. The film suggests that totalitarian control extends beyond the mind to the physical vessel—the bodies of citizens become containers for imposed silence. The recovery of voice in the film's conclusion is thus not merely metaphorical but a literal reclamation of bodily autonomy.
The Aerial remains a significant work of Latin American speculative cinema, combining the political urgency of Argentine film tradition with the visual ambition of European art cinema. Its warnings about media concentration, corporate control of information, and the loss of voice—both literal and figurative—resonate powerfully in an age of platform monopolies and algorithmic control of discourse.

entries.title: The Airtight Garage
entries.creator: Jean "Moebius" Giraud
entries.pitch: The Airtight Garage is a surreal, mind-bending odyssey through a pocket universe contained within a mysterious asteroid orbiting in the constellation Leo. When Major Grubert discovers the garage—a reality-warping structure that defies the laws of physics—he becomes both guardian and prisoner of an ever-shifting realm of impossible architecture, temporal paradoxes, and shifting dimensions. Along the way, he encounters a cast of bizarre characters including the legendary Jerry Cornelius, an entity known as the Longer, and countless others who seek to claim or destroy the garage's power. Moebius created this as a deliberately improvised, whimsical narrative—part science fiction, part fever dream, entirely unique. The story is famously confusing by design, spiraling through digressions, nested realities, and visual marvels that feel like exploring the inside of a cosmic mind.
entries.truth:
The narrative begins with Major Grubert, an intergalactic surveyor, who discovers an asteroid in the constellation Leo that houses a most unusual phenomenon: the "Airtight Garage," a pocket universe contained within the asteroid's interior. Grubert becomes the guardian of this reality,orbiting the asteroid in his ship the Ciguri and overseeing its strange development.
The garage itself is a constantly shifting architectural marvel—rooms lead to other rooms that shouldn't exist, corridors open onto impossible vistas, and reality itself seems to obey only the laws of imagination. Within this space, Grubert encounters numerous visitors and intruders, most notably Jerry Cornelius—a character originally created by Michael Moorcock, here reimagined by Moebius as a dimension-hopping adventurer.
The story unfolds through a series of encounters and digressions. One section might follow Grubert exploring a room that contains an entire ocean; another might focus on the Longer, an entity that exists in a state of perpetual growth, stretching across dimensions. Time moves strangely in the garage—moments last eternities, and seemingly separate episodes turn out to be connected in ways that only become apparent much later.
Several factions seek to claim the garage's power. The "solutionists" are one such group, trying to understand and exploit the garage's reality-warping properties. Throughout these encounters, Grubert maintains his role as guardian, though his own identity begins to blur with the reality he protects.
The narrative deliberately resists linear interpretation. Moebius described the story as "improvised in a deliberately whimsical or capricious manner," and the reader experiences this as a dreamscape where plot points are suggested but never fully explained. Characters appear and reappear with different aspects; timelines collapse into each other; the distinction between dream and waking reality dissolves entirely.
The story continues in subsequent works—"The Man from the Ciguri" (1995) and "Le Chasseur Déprime" (2008)—which further explore the characters and concepts introduced in The Airtight Garage. These later volumes suggest that the garage may be a kind of nexus point for multiple realities, a seed crystal from which infinite possibilities grow.
entries.core:
The Airtight Garage is fundamentally about the nature of creation itself. Moebius was obsessed with the creative act—the moment when something emerges from nothing, when a thought becomes real. The garage represents both the creative space of the artist (the "封闭的车库" or sealed workshop) and the infinite potential of the universe before form imposes itself on chaos.
The work explores the relationship between guardian and prisoner. Grubert chose to protect the garage, but this protection becomes a form of imprisonment. He orbits endlessly, never quite part of the reality he oversees, yet unable to leave. This mirrors the artist's relationship with their creation—the need to protect and control versus the necessity of letting go and allowing meaning to emerge.
The deliberately confusing narrative is itself a theme. Moebius believed that true creation resists easy understanding. The garage's shifting, dreamlike quality isn't a flaw but the point: reality itself is stranger and more fluid than our linear minds can comprehend. The story asks us to let go of the need for clear answers and instead embrace the journey.
Visually, The Airtight Garage represents Moebius at the height of his powers. Every panel is filled with architectural inventions—staircases that lead to impossible places, machines that defy function, landscapes that shift from page to page. The garage becomes a catalog of the impossible, a visual encyclopedia of imagination unchained.
The work also represents a particular moment in European comics history. Published in Métal Hurlant between 1976-1979, it helped establish the magazine as a home for experimental, adult-oriented science fiction. The Airtight Garage influenced generations of artists and filmmakers, and its influence can be seen in everything from concept art for films like TRON and The Fifth Element to the work of contemporary artists exploring digital consciousness and virtual reality.
Critics often call The Airtight Garage Moebius's masterpiece—a work that distills everything he did best into one sprawling, impossible, utterly beautiful package. It is the comic that feels most like a fever dream of creation itself, and it remains essential reading for anyone interested in the limits of visual storytelling.

entries.title: The Andromeda Nebula
entries.creator: Yevgeni Sherstobitov (Director), Ivan Yefremov (Novel Source)
entries.pitch: In a future where humanity has formed an interstellar communication network called "The Great Ring," the starship Tantra returns from investigating an alien world—only to find itself caught in the gravitational grip of an impossible cosmic phenomenon: an iron star. This 1967 Soviet science fiction film, based on Ivan Yefremov's influential 1957 novel, follows Commander Erg Noor and his crew as they face a desperate choice: attempt a perilous landing on an unknown planet orbiting the iron star, or drift in the void waiting for a rescue that might take decades. Meanwhile, back on Earth, the film weaves together parallel narratives of love, loss, and the quiet heroism of those who maintain humanity's connection to the stars. Rare for its optimistic vision of human cooperation and scientific progress, The Andromeda Nebula represents a fascinating glimpse into Soviet science fiction's golden age—a counterpoint to the dystopian visions that dominated Western sci-fi of the era.
entries.truth:
The film opens with a young man performing a ceremonial pledge of allegiance before a torch-shaped monument, while his mentor Mven Mas reflects on his own coming-of-age under the legendary space commander Erg Noor. News arrives that the 37th space expedition has been completed and the starship Tantra is returning to Earth—but then an anomaly disrupts its journey.
Commander Erg Noor awakens from stasis to discover the Tantra caught by the gravity of an "iron star," a bizarre celestial object that defies normal physics. The ship must attempt emergency deceleration, but with fuel depleted, the crew faces a grim reality: rescue from Earth could take decades. The film intercuts between the stranded crew's plight and events on Earth, where Dar Veter, manager of the interstellar communication network "The Great Ring," considers resigning. His relationship with partner Veda Kong is complicated by her lingering feelings for Erg Noor, the man now trapped aboard the Tantra.
Orbiting the iron star, the crew discovers two planets, one of which hosts an alien spacecraft resembling a spiral disc. With no other options, they decide to land and investigate, hoping to find resources to refuel. This choice leads them to encounter the remnants of a previous Earth expedition called the Sail—failed explorers whose fate remains a haunting mystery. The alien ship offers no solutions, only the cryptic广播 of ancient messages from other civilizations: fascinating communications from beings long dead.
The crew faces mounting danger from a mysterious lifeform that thrives in darkness, which kills one member and injures another. Niza Krit, Erg Noor's navigator, sacrifices herself to save him, sustaining a psychic injury that forces her into stasis. Back on Earth, Dar Veter leaves his high-pressure position to take up archaeological work alongside Veda Kong. Mven Mas leads an experiment to compress time and space, seeking to overcome the cosmic distances separating humanity from other worlds.
The Tantra's return after a perilous year brings renewal and hope. The film's closing ceremony shows a new generation taking their oath, symbolizing the eternal quest to bridge the gulf between civilizations.
entries.core:
The Andromeda Nebula stands as a remarkable artifact of Soviet science fiction—a genre often overlooked in Western canon. The film presents a vision of humanity that is fundamentally optimistic: humans are shown as capable of cooperation across vast distances, of maintaining hope in the face of cosmic indifference, and of continuing to reach outward even when that reaching brings tragedy.
The "Great Ring" communication network represents a utopian vision of interstellar civilization—not empire, but a web of connected worlds sharing knowledge and culture. This contrasts sharply with the space operas of the West, which often imagined space as a frontier to be conquered or exploited. The Soviet vision here is of collective endeavor, of science in service of human unity.
The iron star itself serves as a powerful metaphor: an obstacle that cannot be overcome by force or individual heroism, but only by patience, cooperation, and the willingness to accept risk for the sake of discovery. The crew's choice to land on the unknown planet rather than wait for rescue embodies the Soviet valorization of action and scientific inquiry over passive survival.
The film's treatment of time is also notable. The possibility that decades might pass before rescue could arrive, the messages from long-dead civilizations broadcast across the void, the experiment to compress time and space—all speak to a fascination with temporality that was central to Soviet science fiction. This is not the dystopian time-travel of Western classics like La Jetée, but a pragmatic engagement with what the universe demands of those who would explore it.
Finally, the personal narratives—the love triangle between Erg Noor, Veda Kong, and Dar Veter; the mentor-student relationship between Mven Mas and the young initiate; Niza Krit's sacrifice—ground the cosmic drama in human emotion. The Andromeda Nebula suggests that the true measure of a civilization is not its technology but its capacity for connection across distance, for hope in the face of the unknown, and for continuing to reach for the stars even when the stars do not answer.

entries.title: The Ballad of Lost C'Mell
entries.creator: Cordwainer Smith (Paul Linebarger)
entries.pitch: In a far-future Earth where humanity has spread across the stars, society is strictly divided between ruling humans and the "underpeople" — animals genetically modified to possess human form and speech, but denied any rights or citizenship. When Jestocost, a powerful Lord of the Instrumentality of Mankind, discovers a secret network of telepathic underpeople led by the mysterious E'telekeli, he hatches a daring plan to help his oppressed subjects gain freedom without toppling the established order. The story follows C'mell, a beautiful cat-derived underperson working as a "girling" (escort) at Earth's spaceport, who becomes the unlikely key to this revolution. Part social thriller, part tragic romance, this is a uniquely poignant exploration of identity, dignity, and what it truly means to be human.
entries.truth:
The story opens at the funeral of C'mell's father, a famed athlete among the underpeople who held the long-jump record. Jestocost, a Lord of the Instrumentality, attends the funeral seeking to make contact with the underground movement of underpeople who have been demanding rights. Through telepathic contact, he overhears C'mell calling out to someone named E-telly-kelly for help.
Jestocost awkwardly approaches C'mell afterward (she initially mistakes his questioning for a crude sexual advance), but eventually gains her trust. She arranges for him to meet the E'telekeli, a powerful eagle-derived underperson with immense telepathic abilities who leads the hidden resistance.
Together, Jestocost and the E'telekeli devise an elaborate scheme. C'mell will pretend to be a witness to illegal smuggling. When brought before the assembled Lords of the Instrumentality, she presents manufactured evidence that triggers the Bank's computer system to display smuggling routes and hideouts on the Bell — a three-dimensional display. Throughout this, C'mell remains in physical contact with Jestocost, allowing him to telepathically relay the rapidly scrolling images to the E'telekeli.
The information stolen from the Bank provides the underpeople with details about human checkpoints and a list of safe havens where they can hide while seeking rights. Though the Lords dismiss C'mell angrily when her "evidence" proves worthless, the underpeople now possess crucial intelligence.
The close collaboration required between C'mell and Jestocost leads her to fall in love with him — the first human who has ever respected her intelligence. However, Jestocost suppresses his own feelings as a dangerous distraction, and they separate when the plan succeeds. They meet only once more, many years later, when the underpeople are well on their way to achieving rights. Their conversation is friendly, but C'mell is privately heartbroken by his lack of romantic feeling.
During Jestocost's final days, long after C'mell has died of old age, he has a telepathic conversation with the E'telekeli's successor. He learns that C'mell never loved anyone but him, and that their names will be linked together forever in underpeople folklore. The ballad of Lost C'mell becomes a folk song among the underpeople, telling a partial and poetic version of their story.
In Jestocost's lifetime, underpeople achieve lower-grade citizenship — a victory that was partly due to his and C'mell's efforts, though their roles remain forever secret.
entries.core:
The Ballad of Lost C'Mell is a masterful exploration of oppression, identity, and the price of revolution. Smith uses the underpeople — beings who are biologically human but socially monstrous — as a mirror to examine prejudice, civil rights, and the nature of personhood. The question at the heart of the story is profound: what makes someone human? Is it appearance, intelligence, legal status, or something deeper?
The novella also explores the complexity of revolutionary change. Jestocost works within the system, seeking gradual reform rather than violent overthrow — a nuanced position that Smith presents without judgment. The tragedy of Jestocost and C'mell's relationship reflects the impossibility of certain connections across divides of species, class, and legal status.
Smith's unique gift for mixing fairy-tale sweetness with hard-edged science fiction shines throughout. The cat-derived C'mell is simultaneously a hard-nosed survivor and a romantic heroine; the far-future setting feels both alien and strangely familiar. The telepathic elements add another layer, suggesting that true communication might transcend the physical barriers that divide beings.
The story was recognized as one of the 22 best novellas pre-Nebula, included in the Science Fiction Hall of Fame Volume Two — well-deserved recognition for a work that manages to be entertaining, thought-provoking, and deeply moving all at once.

entries.title: The Barbie Murders
entries.creator: John Varley
entries.pitch:
The Barbie Murders is a groundbreaking collection of nine science fiction short stories by award-winning author John Varley. Published in 1980, these stories blend noir mystery elements with Varley's distinctive vision of a future humanity spread across the solar system. The title story presents a fascinating premise: in a future where Barbie dolls have achieved near-sentience and live as a persecuted underclass, a detective must investigate murders of these "Barbies" - exploring themes of identity, humanity, and discrimination through an allegory that echoes real-world civil rights struggles.
Other stories in the collection explore equally inventive concepts - from reality-bending funhouses to alternate dimensions, from artificial intelligence to time manipulation. Several stories are set on the colonized Moon, showcasing Varley's vision of lunar society. The collection won the Locus Award for Best Short Story Collection and established Varley as one of science fiction's most innovative voices. These stories are noir-tinged, often melancholic, and frequently challenge readers' assumptions about identity, consciousness, and what it means to be human.
entries.truth:
The collection contains nine stories:
**"Bagatelle"** - A young girl with incredible musical abilities is taken from her family to be trained as an "instrument" for powerful interests, exploring themes of exploitation and the cost of genius.
**"The Funhouse Effect"** - A reality-bending tale where a man discovers that an amusement park funhouse exists in multiple dimensions simultaneously, leading to encounters with alternate versions of himself.
**"The Barbie Murders"** - The centerpiece story takes place in a future where sentient Barbie dolls have become a reality - created as companions, they now exist as a marginalized underclass subject to abuse and murder. When a series of Barbie murders rocks this fragile society, detective Saul Reynolds must investigate, ultimately discovering uncomfortable truths about what it means to create intelligent life and then treat it as less than human. The story works as both a mystery and an allegory for civil rights.
**"Equinoctial"** - A time-travel story set during the construction of the Panama Canal, where workers from different eras collide in a time loop.
**"Manikins"** - The story of a man who creates perfect robotic copies of people, exploring identity and the nature of consciousness.
**"Beatnik Bayou"** - A surreal tale set in an alternate New Orleans where reality functions differently.
**"Good-bye, Robinson Crusoe"** - A story about the last human on a terraformed Mars and the alien intelligence that befriends him.
**"Lollipop and the Tar Baby"** - A story featuring the character Lollipop, exploring themes of memory and identity.
**"Picnic on Nearside"** - Set on the Moon, this story involves a group of colonists dealing with an otherworldly threat.
The collection showcases Varley's range - from hard SF to surrealism, from noir mystery to poignant human drama. His lunar society stories paint a convincing picture of frontier life among the stars, while the title story remains one of SF's most thought-provoking explorations of created consciousness.
entries.core:
John Varley's The Barbie Murders represents a pivotal moment in science fiction's evolution during the 1970s-80s. The collection demonstrates several key themes that would become central to the genre's development.
The title story serves as a powerful allegory for civil rights and the treatment of "the other." By creating a sentient minority (the Barbies) who look identical to "normal" humans but are treated as less than human, Varley creates a mirror for examining real-world prejudice. The story asks uncomfortable questions about creation, consciousness, and moral responsibility - if we create intelligent life, what obligations do we have toward it?
Several stories explore identity and consciousness - what makes someone (or something) human? "Manikins" directly addresses this through the creation of perfect robotic duplicates, while "The Funhouse Effect" uses dimensional manipulation to explore how identity might persist (or fracture) across realities.
Varley's lunar settings anticipate much of later SF's interest in space colonization, but he focuses not on the technical aspects but on the social dynamics - how would actual people build societies on frontier worlds? His Moon is a gritty, working-class place, far from the gleaming utopias of earlier SF.
